topics.title: Elementary Concept of Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elementary Concept of Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Elasticity?

**Elasticity** is the branch of solid mechanics that studies the behaviour of solid bodies subjected to external loads, specifically how they deform and how internal forces (stresses) develop within them. A body is said to be **perfectly elastic** if it regains its original shape and size completely upon removal of the applied load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** Elasticity is the property of a material by which it regains its original configuration (shape and size) after the removal of the deforming forces. The study of elasticity deals with the distribution of internal forces and the resulting deformations in a body.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Elasticity

The elementary concept of elasticity rests upon **three fundamental pillars** every KTU student must internalise:

| Pillar | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Stress** | $\sigma$, $\tau$ | Internal force per unit area developed inside a body |
| **Strain** | $\varepsilon$, $\gamma$ | Deformation per unit original dimension |
| **Elastic Constants** | $E$, $G$, $K$, $\nu$ | Material properties linking stress and strain |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Bridge"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a brick resting on a thick foam mattress. The brick's weight is a *load* (force). The foam compresses — this compression is *deformation*. Inside the foam, millions of tiny fibres push against each other; this internal push is *stress*. The foam tries to push the brick back up — this restoring tendency is *elasticity*. The relationship between how much the foam compresses and how hard it pushes back is governed by its *elastic constants* (E, the Young's modulus, is essentially the foam's "stiffness fingerprint").

## 1.4 Stress — The Internal Resistance

When a body is subjected to external forces, internal resisting forces are set up at every imaginary plane within the body. The **intensity of these internal forces per unit area** is called stress.

**Normal Stress** ($\sigma$): Acts perpendicular to the surface.

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta P_n}{\Delta A}$$

**Shear Stress** ($\tau$): Acts tangentially to the surface.

$$\tau = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta P_t}{\Delta A}$$

where $\Delta P_n$ and $\Delta P_t$ are the normal and tangential components of the internal force on a small area $\Delta A$. Units: **N/m²** or **Pa** (with standard SI multiples being kPa, MPa, GPa).

## 1.5 Strain — The Deformation Measure

**Normal Strain** ($\varepsilon$): Change in length per unit original length.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Shear Strain** ($\gamma$): Change in angle (in radians) between two originally perpendicular lines.

$$\gamma = \tan \theta \approx \theta \quad \text{(for small deformations)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Strain is **dimensionless** (no units) since it is a ratio of two lengths. The radian, although a unit, is often retained symbolically.

## 1.6 Hooke's Law — The Cornerstone of Linear Elasticity

**Robert Hooke's Law** (1660) states that, within the elastic limit, stress is **directly proportional to strain**.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Here, **E** is the **Young's Modulus** (or Modulus of Elasticity) — a material constant with units identical to stress (Pa or MPa). It represents the slope of the stress-strain curve in the linear region.

## 1.7 Poisson's Ratio — The Lateral Effect

When a bar is stretched axially, it **contracts laterally**. The ratio of lateral strain to axial strain is called **Poisson's Ratio** ($\nu$).

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

The negative sign ensures $\nu$ is positive (lateral strain is opposite in sign to axial strain). For most engineering materials, **$0 < \nu < 0.5$**; for steel, $\nu \approx 0.3$; for rubber, $\nu \approx 0.5$ (nearly incompressible).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Dimensional State of Stress

At any point inside a loaded body, an **infinitesimal cubic element** can be extracted. The internal forces acting on its six faces give rise to **nine stress components**:

- **Three normal stresses:** $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ (perpendicular to faces)
- **Six shear stresses:** $\tau_{xy}$, $\tau_{yx}$, $\tau_{yz}$, $\tau_{zy}$, $\tau_{zx}$, $\tau_{xz}$ (tangential to faces)

By applying **moment equilibrium** to the infinitesimal element, we obtain the **principle of complementary shear stresses** (Cauchy's symmetry):

$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}, \quad \tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}, \quad \tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}$$

Hence, the stress state at a point is fully defined by **six independent components**: $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Whenever you draw a 3D stress element for the exam, always label all six visible arrows (three normals + three shears) and clearly show complementary pairs. Examiners award 1–2 marks just for a clean, correctly-labelled element.

## 2.2 The Three-Dimensional State of Strain

Correspondingly, the strain state at a point is defined by:

- **Three normal strains:** $\varepsilon_x$, $\varepsilon_y$, $\varepsilon_z$
- **Three shear strains:** $\gamma_{xy}$, $\gamma_{yz}$, $\gamma_{zx}$

For **small deformations** (engineering strain), the relationship between displacement $(u, v, w)$ and strain is given by:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \varepsilon_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}$$

## 2.3 The Generalised Hooke's Law (3D Anisotropic Form)

For a general anisotropic material, the stress-strain relationship is:

$$\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \cdot \varepsilon_{kl}$$

where $C_{ijkl}$ is a fourth-order tensor with **81 components**, reduced to **21 independent constants** by symmetry for the most general anisotropic case.

## 2.4 Hooke's Law for Isotropic Materials

For a **homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic material** (KTU Module 1 focus), only **two independent elastic constants** are needed. The most common form uses $E$ and $\nu$:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}$$

## 2.5 Volumetric Strain and Bulk Modulus

The **volumetric strain** (or dilatation) is the change in volume per unit original volume:

$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0}$$

For hydrostatic pressure $p$ (equal compression on all sides):

$$e = \frac{p}{K}(1 - 2\nu) \cdot 3 = \frac{3(1 - 2\nu)}{E} \cdot p$$

where $K$ is the **Bulk Modulus**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table** — it is the most frequently used cluster of equations in Module 1 problems.

| Concept | Formula | Unit | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal stress | $\sigma = P/A$ | Pa | Axial force per area |
| Shear stress | $\tau = V/A$ | Pa | Shear force per area |
| Normal strain | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless | Axial deformation |
| Shear strain | $\gamma = \theta$ (radians) | dimensionless | Angular distortion |
| Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | Pa | Linear elastic relation |
| Poisson's ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{ax}$ | dimensionless | Lateral effect |
| Shear modulus | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | Pa | Resistance to shape change |
| Bulk modulus | $K = -p / e$ | Pa | Resistance to volume change |
| 3D Hooke (normal) | $\varepsilon_x = [\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]/E$ | dimensionless | Generalised Hooke |
| 3D Hooke (shear) | $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$ | dimensionless | Shear form |
| E-G-$\nu$ relation | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Pa | Important inter-link |
| E-K-$\nu$ relation | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Pa | Important inter-link |
| Volumetric strain | $e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | dimensionless | Cubical dilatation |
| 2D principal stress | $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$ | Pa | Eigen stresses |
| Principal angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | degrees | Orientation of $\sigma_1$ |
| Max shear (2D) | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$ | Pa | Maximum distortion |
| Stress inv. $I_1$ | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$ | Pa | First invariant |
| Stress inv. $I_2$ | $I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^{2} - \tau_{yz}^{2} - \tau_{zx}^{2}$ | Pa² | Second invariant |
| Stress inv. $I_3$ | $I_3 = \sigma_x\sigma_y\sigma_z + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{zx} - \sigma_x\tau_{yz}^{2} - \sigma_y\tau_{zx}^{2} - \sigma_z\tau_{xy}^{2}$ | Pa³ | Third invariant |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently forget that the $\pm$ in the principal stress formula gives $\sigma_1$ (the larger) and $\sigma_2$ (the smaller). The square root is **always positive**; the $\pm$ determines which is which.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The elementary concept of elasticity is the **foundation for every structural design** you will ever encounter:

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of beams, columns, bridges, dams, and foundations; predicting settlements; RCC and PSC analysis.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Pressure vessel design, shaft design, gear teeth analysis, fatigue and creep studies.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Wing structure analysis, fuselage pressurisation, thin-walled structures.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Soil settlement analysis, rock mechanics, tunnel design.
- **Biomedical Engineering:** Bone mechanics, stent design, prosthetics.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Module 1 question paper typically tests both **conceptual clarity** (definitions, units, Hooke's law) and **problem-solving ability** (principal stress, transformation equations, stress invariants).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: 2D Stress Transformation Equations

We will derive the equations that express the normal and shear stresses on an inclined plane in terms of the stresses on the original plane.

### Setup

Consider a 2D element with original stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ acting on faces perpendicular to the $x$- and $y$-axes. Now consider an inclined plane whose outward normal makes an angle $\theta$ (measured counter-clockwise from the $x$-axis) with the $x$-axis.

Let:
- $\sigma_n$ = normal stress on the inclined plane
- $\tau_n$ = shear stress on the inclined plane
- $A$ = area of the inclined face
- $A \cos\theta$ = projected area on the $x$-face
- $A \sin\theta$ = projected area on the $y$-face

### Force Equilibrium in the $x$-direction

Summing forces in the $x$-direction on the wedge:

$$\sigma_n \cdot A \cos\theta - \tau_n \cdot A \sin\theta = \sigma_x \cdot A \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \cdot A \sin\theta$$

Dividing throughout by $A$:

$$\sigma_n \cos\theta - \tau_n \sin\theta = \sigma_x \cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta \quad \text{...(i)}$$

### Force Equilibrium in the $y$-direction

Summing forces in the $y$-direction on the wedge:

$$\sigma_n \cdot A \sin\theta + \tau_n \cdot A \cos\theta = \sigma_y \cdot A \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cdot A \cos\theta$$

Dividing throughout by $A$:

$$\sigma_n \sin\theta + \tau_n \cos\theta = \sigma_y \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos\theta \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Solving the System of Equations

Multiply equation (i) by $\cos\theta$ and equation (ii) by $\sin\theta$, then add:

$$\sigma_n \cos^{2}\theta - \tau_n \sin\theta\cos\theta + \sigma_n \sin^{2}\theta + \tau_n \sin\theta\cos\theta = \sigma_x \cos^{2}\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta\cos\theta + \sigma_y \sin^{2}\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin\theta\cos\theta$$

The $\tau_n$ terms cancel:

$$\sigma_n (\cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta) = \sigma_x \cos^{2}\theta + \sigma_y \sin^{2}\theta + 2\tau_{xy} \sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Using the identities $\cos^{2}\theta = (1+\cos 2\theta)/2$, $\sin^{2}\theta = (1-\cos 2\theta)/2$, and $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$\sigma_n = \sigma_x \cdot \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2} + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2} + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta \quad \text{...(Final Eq. 1)}$$

### Deriving the Shear Stress Expression

Multiply equation (i) by $(-\sin\theta)$ and equation (ii) by $\cos\theta$, then add:

$$-\sigma_n \cos\theta\sin\theta + \tau_n \sin^{2}\theta + \sigma_n \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_n \cos^{2}\theta = -\sigma_x \cos\theta\sin\theta - \tau_{xy} \sin^{2}\theta + \sigma_y \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos^{2}\theta$$

The $\sigma_n$ terms cancel:

$$\tau_n (\sin^{2}\theta + \cos^{2}\theta) = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta)$$

Using the double-angle identities $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta/2$ and $\cos^{2}\theta - \sin^{2}\theta = \cos 2\theta$:

$$\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta \quad \text{...(Final Eq. 2)}$$

### Summary of Stress Transformation Equations (2D)

$$\boxed{\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Principal Stresses and Maximum Shear Stress

### Principal Stresses

**Principal stresses** are the normal stresses acting on planes where the shear stress is zero. Setting $\tau_n = 0$ in Eq. 2:

$$-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta_p = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} \quad \text{...(Principal Angle)}$$

Substituting this condition back into Eq. 1, or using the identity $\cos 2\theta_p = \pm(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/R$ and $\sin 2\theta_p = \pm 2\tau_{xy}/R$ where $R = \sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The larger value (with $+$ sign) is $\sigma_1$ (major principal stress); the smaller (with $-$ sign) is $\sigma_2$ (minor principal stress).

### Maximum Shear Stress

The maximum shear stress occurs at $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$ (i.e., when $\sigma_n$ equals the average stress $(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2$). Substituting into Eq. 2, we obtain:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** $\tau_{max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$. This is the maximum in-plane shear stress and is fundamental for theories of failure (Tresca, Mohr-Coulomb).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Strain in 3D

Consider a rectangular parallelepiped of sides $dx$, $dy$, $dz$. After deformation, the sides become:
- $dx(1 + \varepsilon_x)$, $dy(1 + \varepsilon_y)$, $dz(1 + \varepsilon_z)$

Original volume:
$$V_0 = dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

Deformed volume:
$$V = dx(1+\varepsilon_x) \cdot dy(1+\varepsilon_y) \cdot dz(1+\varepsilon_z)$$

Expanding and neglecting higher-order products of small strains (since $\varepsilon \ll 1$):

$$V = dx \cdot dy \cdot dz \cdot \left[1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_z\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z\right]$$

$$V \approx V_0 \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)$$

Therefore, the **volumetric strain** is:

$$e = \frac{V - V_0}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Relationship $E = 2G(1+\nu)$

Consider a pure shear stress state $\tau$ on a 2D element. From Hooke's law in shear:
$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

Now resolve the pure shear into principal stresses by Mohr's circle or stress transformation. A pure shear state $\tau_{xy} = \tau$ is equivalent to principal stresses:
$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

The corresponding principal strains (using generalised Hooke's law in 2D):
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2) = \frac{1}{E}(\tau - \nu(-\tau)) = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1) = \frac{1}{E}(-\tau - \nu\tau) = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

The maximum shear strain $\gamma$ is related to principal strains as:
$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Equating the two expressions for $\gamma$:
$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **standard KTU derivation question** (Module 1) and a cornerstone relationship in elasticity.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Situation | Use | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Uniaxial loading (axial force) | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Direct Hooke's law |
| Biaxial/Triaxial loading | $\varepsilon_x = [\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y+\sigma_z)]/E$ | Generalised Hooke's law |
| Pure torsion | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Shear Hooke's law |
| Hydrostatic pressure | $e = p/K$ | Bulk modulus relation |
| Stress on inclined plane | Transformation equations | Stress rotation |
| Finding max/min normal stress | Principal stress formula | $\tau_n = 0$ condition |
| Finding max distortion | $\tau_{max}$ formula | $\sigma_n = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y)/2$ |
| Relating elastic constants | $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Identity relationships |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 3D Stress Element — The "Universal Visual"

A correctly drawn 3D stress element is the **single most important diagram** you can produce in any elasticity problem. It carries significant marks in KTU valuation.

```
                σz (top face, pointing out)
                    ↑
                    |
                    |
   σx (back) ←------•------→ σx (front)
   ↑               |               ↑
   |               | τzx           |
   |   τxy         ↓               |
   ←-----•-----------+-----------•
        σy        (origin)        σy
   ↑               |               ↑
   |               | τyz           |
   |               ↓               |
                    |
                    ↓
              σz (bottom face, pointing in)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each face has a normal stress (perpendicular arrow) and two shear stresses (tangential arrows). The complementary pair (e.g., $\tau_{xy}$ on the right face and $\tau_{yx}$ on the top face) are equal in magnitude by Cauchy's symmetry.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Stress-Strain-Stress Path

The following functional architecture flow maps how an external load on a body propagates into internal stresses and strains through the elastic constants.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load P] --> B[Internal Force Distribution]
    B --> C[Stress sigma / tau]
    C --> D{Geometry of cut}
    D --> E[Normal Stress on plane]
    D --> F[Shear Stress on plane]
    C --> G[Strain epsilon / gamma]
    G --> H{Geometric measure}
    H --> I[Normal Strain]
    H --> J[Shear Strain]
    E --> K[Hookes Law sigma = E times epsilon]
    I --> K
    F --> L[Shear Modulus tau = G times gamma]
    J --> L
    C --> M[Volumetric Response e = epsilon x + epsilon y + epsilon z]
    M --> N[Bulk Modulus K = p over e]
    E --> O[Principal Stresses sigma 1 and sigma 2]
    F --> P[Max Shear Stress tau max]
    K --> Q[Material Property Set: E, G, K, nu]
    L --> Q
    N --> Q
    O --> R[Engineering Design Decision]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the flowchart:** Start from the external load at the top, follow the branching down through stress/strain analyses, and converge at the design decision at the bottom. The "Material Property Set" node $E, G, K, \nu$ is the single most important parameter set in the entire analysis.

## 4.3 Mohr's Circle — The Geometric Visualisation

For 2D stress states, Mohr's circle is the **unifying graphical tool**. It is a circle in the $(\sigma, \tau)$ plane with:

- **Center:** $C = ((\sigma_x + \sigma_y)/2, 0)$
- **Radius:** $R = \sqrt{((\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$
- **$\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2$:** the two intercepts on the $\sigma$-axis
- **$\tau_{max}$:** the topmost (or bottommost) point on the circle, equal to $R$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sigma X on X axis] --> D[Point P on Circle]
    B[Sigma Y on X axis] --> E[Point Q on Circle]
    C[Shear XY couples with Sigma] --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F[Center C avg sigma]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Radius R tau max]
    G --> H[Sigma 1 and Sigma 2 on X axis]
    G --> I[Tau max at top of circle]
    H --> J[Principal Stresses]
    I --> K[Maximum Shear Stress]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the circle:** Every point on Mohr's circle represents a particular orientation of the cut plane. The principal stresses (where shear is zero) lie on the horizontal axis. The maximum shear stress lies at the top/bottom of the circle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Pattern]

> **Q1.** Define the following terms with their standard units: (a) Stress, (b) Strain, (c) Young's Modulus.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Stress:** It is the internal resisting force per unit area developed inside a body when subjected to external loads. It has two types: normal stress (perpendicular to the surface) and shear stress (tangential to the surface). **SI unit: N/m² (Pa).**

**(b) Strain:** It is the ratio of change in dimension to the original dimension. For axial deformation, normal strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$. For angular distortion, shear strain $\gamma = \theta$ (in radians). **Strain is dimensionless.**

**(c) Young's Modulus (E):** It is the constant of proportionality between normal stress and normal strain within the elastic limit: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. **SI unit: N/m² (Pa).** For steel, $E \approx 200$ GPa; for concrete, $E \approx 30$ GPa.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Pattern]

> **Q2.** State Hooke's Law. What is Poisson's ratio? Mention its range for engineering materials.

**Model Answer:**

**Hooke's Law:** Within the elastic limit, the stress in a body is directly proportional to the strain produced, i.e., $\sigma = E\varepsilon$. This linear relationship was formulated by Robert Hooke in 1660.

**Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** It is the ratio of lateral strain to axial strain, with a negative sign convention to make it positive:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

**Range:** For most engineering materials, $0 < \nu < 0.5$. For steel, $\nu \approx 0.3$; for aluminium, $\nu \approx 0.33$; for rubber, $\nu \approx 0.5$ (nearly incompressible). Cork has $\nu \approx 0$, which is why it is used in bottle stoppers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** with a typical split of **7 + 7** across two sub-parts. Module 1 of PECET411 is allocated **Q1 in the question paper**, and students must answer either **Q1A OR Q1B**.

---

### Question 1A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Jul 2023, Model Paper Pattern]

> **Q1A.** (a) Define the stress at a point. Explain the nine components of stress at a point with the help of a 3D element. Prove that the stress tensor is symmetric. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** The state of plane stress at a point is given by $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. Find:
>   (i) The principal stresses and the principal plane orientation.
>   (ii) The maximum shear stress and its orientation.
>   **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** Stress at a point is the limiting value of internal force per unit area as the area shrinks to a point in a specific orientation.

**Nine Stress Components on a 3D Element:**
On a small cubic element with sides parallel to the coordinate axes, three faces are visible (the opposite three are hidden). The stresses are:
- **Normal stresses:** $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$ (perpendicular to the faces).
- **Shear stresses:** On the face perpendicular to the $x$-axis: $\tau_{xy}$ (in $y$-direction) and $\tau_{xz}$ (in $z$-direction). Similarly on the other faces: $\tau_{yx}$, $\tau_{yz}$, $\tau_{zx}$, $\tau_{zy}$. This gives nine components in total.

**Proof of Symmetry of the Stress Tensor ($\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$):**

Consider an infinitesimal cubic element of sides $dx$, $dy$, $dz$. Take moments about the $z$-axis through the centre of the cube:

The shear stresses $\tau_{xy}$ and $\tau_{yx}$ produce couples. The net moment about the $z$-axis:

$$\sum M_z = 0$$

$$\tau_{xy} \cdot (dy \cdot dz) \cdot dx - \tau_{yx} \cdot (dx \cdot dz) \cdot dy = 0$$

(The negative sign is due to the opposite sense of the shear stresses on opposite faces.)

$$\tau_{xy} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz - \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dy \cdot dz = 0$$

Since $dx \cdot dy \cdot dz \neq 0$:

$$\boxed{\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}}$$

Similarly, by taking moments about the $x$-axis and $y$-axis:

$$\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}, \quad \tau_{zx} = \tau_{xz}$$

**Conclusion:** The stress tensor is symmetric, leaving only **six independent components**: $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa.

**(i) Principal Stresses and Orientation:**

The principal stresses are given by:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

Compute the average stress:
$$\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

Compute the radius:
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300} = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_1 = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{Major principal stress})$$

$$\sigma_2 = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{Minor principal stress})$$

**Principal Plane Orientation:**
$$\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31° \Rightarrow \theta_p = 28.15°$$

So the major principal stress acts on a plane oriented at **$\theta_p = 28.15°$** counter-clockwise from the $x$-axis.

**(ii) Maximum Shear Stress and Orientation:**

$$\tau_{max} = R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Orientation:** $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45° = 28.15° + 45° = 73.15°$.

The normal stress on this plane:
$$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Summary:**
> - $\sigma_1 = 96.06$ MPa, $\sigma_2 = 23.94$ MPa
> - $\theta_p = 28.15°$
> - $\tau_{max} = 36.06$ MPa at $\theta_s = 73.15°$, with $\sigma_n = 60$ MPa
>
> **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question 1B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Pattern]

> **Q1B.** (a) State and explain Hooke's Law. Derive the relations $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ and $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ for an isotropic linear elastic material. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A steel bar of length 2 m, cross-section 50 mm × 30 mm, is subjected to an axial tensile load of 90 kN. If $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.3$, find:
>   (i) The axial stress and strain.
>   (ii) The lateral strain, change in cross-sectional dimensions, and change in volume.
>   **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Statement of Hooke's Law:** Within the elastic limit, the stress in a material is directly proportional to the strain produced: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (Robert Hooke, 1660). The material returns to its original shape when the load is removed.

**Derivation of $E = 2G(1+\nu)$:**

Consider a state of pure shear stress $\tau_{xy} = \tau$ on a 2D element. By stress transformation, this is equivalent to principal stresses:
$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

Using the generalised Hooke's law in 2D:
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2] = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau)] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1] = \frac{1}{E}[-\tau - \nu\tau] = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

The shear strain is:
$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

But from the shear Hooke's law:
$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

Equating the two:
$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E} \Rightarrow \boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$

**Derivation of $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$:**

Consider a hydrostatic pressure state: $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$ (compression).

The normal strain in any direction:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)] = \frac{1}{E}[-p - \nu(-p - p)] = \frac{1}{E}[-p + 2\nu p] = -\frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

The volumetric strain:
$$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \times \left(-\frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}\right) = -\frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

The bulk modulus is defined as $K = -p/e$:

$$K = \frac{-p}{-\frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

Rearranging:
$$\boxed{E = 3K(1-2\nu)}$$

**[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** Length $L = 2$ m, cross-section $50 \text{ mm} \times 30 \text{ mm}$, axial load $P = 90$ kN, $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$.

**(i) Axial Stress and Strain:**

Cross-sectional area:
$$A = 50 \times 30 = 1500 \text{ mm}^2 = 1500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

Axial stress:
$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{90 \times 10^3}{1500 \times 10^{-6}} = 60 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

Axial strain:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 3 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0003$$

**(ii) Lateral Strain, Change in Cross-Section, and Change in Volume:**

Lateral strain:
$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x = -0.3 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} = -9 \times 10^{-5}$$

Change in width ($b = 50$ mm):
$$\Delta b = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot b = -9 \times 10^{-5} \times 50 = -4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = -0.0045 \text{ mm}$$

Change in depth ($d = 30$ mm):
$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d = -9 \times 10^{-5} \times 30 = -2.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = -0.0027 \text{ mm}$$

Change in length:
$$\Delta L = \varepsilon_x \cdot L = 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.6 \text{ mm}$$

Original volume:
$$V_0 = A \times L = 1500 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

Volumetric strain:
$$e = \varepsilon_x + 2\varepsilon_{lat} = 3 \times 10^{-4} + 2(-9 \times 10^{-5}) = 3 \times 10^{-4} - 1.8 \times 10^{-4} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in volume:
$$\Delta V = e \cdot V_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \times 3 \times 10^6 = 360 \text{ mm}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer Summary:**
> - $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa, $\varepsilon_x = 3 \times 10^{-4}$
> - $\varepsilon_{lat} = -9 \times 10^{-5}$
> - $\Delta b = -0.0045$ mm, $\Delta d = -0.0027$ mm
> - $\Delta L = 0.6$ mm
> - $\Delta V = 360$ mm³
>
> **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Module 1 (Elementary Concept of Elasticity)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting complementary shear stresses:** Students often write $\tau_{xy}$ and $\tau_{yx}$ as independent quantities. They are equal: $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$. Failure to state this explicitly costs **1–2 marks** in any 3D stress element question.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in principal stress formula:** The square root term is **always positive**; the $\pm$ sign determines which value is $\sigma_1$ and which is $\sigma_2$. Many students get confused and assign the wrong sign.
>
> 3. **Unit mistakes:** Stress and strain have different units! Stress = N/m² (Pa), Strain = dimensionless. Mixing these up costs a mark.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the negative sign in Poisson's ratio:** The definition is $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral}/\varepsilon_{axial}$. The negative sign ensures $\nu > 0$ for normal materials.
>
> 5. **In stress transformation, using $\theta$ vs $2\theta$:** All the transformation equations use $2\theta$ in the trigonometric functions. A common error is to use $\theta$ in $\cos\theta$ instead of $\cos 2\theta$. **Always double-check this!**
>
> 6. **Drawing stress element incorrectly:** Shear stresses on opposite faces must be in opposite directions to satisfy moment equilibrium. An incorrectly drawn shear arrow pair will cost **1–2 marks** for the diagram.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning assumptions:** In any derivation (Hooke's law, stress transformation), explicitly state the assumptions: small deformations, linear elastic material, isotropy, static equilibrium.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Elementary Concept of Elasticity"**

- **Elasticity** is the property of a material to regain its original shape after the removal of external load.
- **Stress** = internal force per unit area; units are **Pa** (N/m²) or **MPa/GPa**.
- **Strain** = deformation per unit original dimension; **dimensionless** (often expressed in microstrain $\mu\varepsilon$).
- **Two types of stress:** Normal stress ($\sigma$) and Shear stress ($\tau$).
- **Two types of strain:** Normal strain ($\varepsilon$) and Shear strain ($\gamma$).
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ within the elastic limit.
- **Young's Modulus (E):** Slope of the linear portion of the stress-strain curve; for steel $\approx 200$ GPa.
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral}/\varepsilon_{axial}$; for most materials $0 < \nu < 0.5$.
- **Shear Modulus (G):** $G = \tau/\gamma$; relation $E = 2G(1+\nu)$.
- **Bulk Modulus (K):** $K = -p/e$; relation $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$.
- **Volumetric strain:** $e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$.
- **Complementary shear stress:** $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$ (stress tensor is symmetric).
- **3D stress state at a point:** 6 independent components — $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$.
- **2D stress transformation equations:**
  - $\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta$
  - $\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta$
- **Principal stresses:** $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$
- **Principal angle:** $\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$
- **Maximum in-plane shear stress:** $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ at $\theta_s = \theta_p + 45°$
- **Stress invariants:** $I_1, I_2, I_3$ are independent of coordinate rotation — used in 3D principal stress calculation.
- **Generalised Hooke's Law (3D):** $\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$; analogous for $\varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$; and $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$, etc.
- **Real-world importance:** Foundation for all structural design in civil, mechanical, aerospace, and geotechnical engineering.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stresses in Three Dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stresses in Three Dimensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Concept of Stress at a Point in 3D

In solid mechanics, when external loads are applied to a body, internal forces are generated on every internal cross-section. The intensity of these internal forces, normalized per unit area as the area shrinks to a mathematically infinitesimal point, defines **stress at a point**. In **three dimensions**, a point in a loaded continuum can be sliced by an infinite family of planes, each plane giving a different stress distribution. This totality of stresses at a point, irrespective of the plane chosen, is known as the **State of Stress at a Point**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET411 – Module 1):**
> *"The state of stress at a point in a continuous body in three-dimensional space is completely characterized by a second-order symmetric tensor, called the Cauchy's Stress Tensor, having nine components in general."*

### 1.2 Components of the Stress Tensor

The stress tensor at a point in 3D is represented by a 3×3 matrix:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:
- **σ_xx, σ_yy, σ_zz** = Normal stresses acting perpendicular to the coordinate planes (x, y, z).
- **τ_xy, τ_yz, τ_zx** = Shear stresses acting tangentially on the coordinate planes.
- Due to **moment equilibrium** of an infinitesimal element: τ_xy = τ_yx, τ_yz = τ_zy, τ_zx = τ_xz. Hence, the stress tensor is **symmetric** and has only **6 independent components**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Cube in Quicksand" 🏗️

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine placing a tiny sugar cube on the surface of a vibrating gelatin dessert. The cube is being poked, pulled, and twisted from all six faces simultaneously. If you tilt the cube to expose a slanted face, the force-per-area (stress) on that face is completely different from the top. **Stress at a point in 3D is essentially the complete "stress fingerprint"** that tells you the internal force intensity on *any* arbitrary plane passing through that point — not just the horizontal or vertical ones. The **stress tensor** is the complete mathematical fingerprint of this internal state.

### 1.4 Why Study 3D Stress?

Most real engineering components (pressure vessels, turbine blades, dam foundations, connecting rods) experience **multiaxial loading**. Studying only 1D or 2D stress leads to catastrophic underestimation of failure risk. The 3D stress analysis is essential for:
- Predicting yielding (using **von Mises**, **Tresca** criteria).
- Designing against **fatigue and brittle fracture**.
- Computing **principal stresses** that govern crack propagation.
- Analyzing **geotechnical** and **contact stress** problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cauchy Stress Principle (Derivation Logic)

**Step 1 — Define the Stress Vector (Traction Vector):**
Consider an arbitrary plane passing through a point **P**, with the plane's outward normal being the unit vector **n**. The internal force per unit area on this plane is the **traction vector T(n)**.

**Step 2 — Relate Traction to Stress Tensor:**
Cauchy's fundamental lemma (derived from Newton's 2nd law applied to a tetrahedron in equilibrium) gives:

$$
T^{(n)}_i = \sigma_{ij} \, n_j
$$

This is the **Cauchy's Stress Equation**. It means: *Knowing the stress tensor at a point and the normal of a plane uniquely determines the traction vector on that plane.*

**Step 3 — Decompose Traction into Normal and Shear Components:**
The traction vector **T** can be split into:
- **Normal stress (σ_n):** Component along the plane's normal.
- **Shear stress (τ):** Component tangent to the plane.

$$
\sigma_n = T_i \, n_i = \sigma_{ij} \, n_i \, n_j
$$

$$
\tau^2 = \vert T \vert^2 - \sigma_n^2
$$

## 2.2 Principal Stresses and Principal Planes

A **principal plane** is one on which the **shear stress vanishes** (only normal stress exists). The associated normal stress is the **principal stress**.

To find principal stresses, we enforce: **T** is parallel to **n**, i.e., the shear is zero.

$$
\sigma_{ij} \, n_j = \lambda \, n_i
$$

This is an **eigenvalue problem**. Writing the characteristic equation:

$$
\det(\sigma_{ij} - \lambda \, \delta_{ij}) = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^3 - I_1 \lambda^2 + I_2 \lambda - I_3 = 0
$$

The three real roots λ₁, λ₂, λ₃ are the **principal stresses** (σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ σ₃). Since σ_ij is symmetric, all eigenvalues are real.

## 2.3 Stress Invariants

A remarkable property: quantities derived from the stress tensor (like the coefficients of the characteristic polynomial) are **independent of coordinate system orientation**. They are **invariants**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Significance:** Stress invariants are critical because they have the *same value in every coordinate system*. This is the foundation of all yield criteria and failure theories.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** |
|------|--------------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Cauchy's Stress Equation | $T_i = \sigma_{ij} \, n_j$ | Traction on a plane with normal n |
| 2 | Normal Stress on Inclined Plane | $\sigma_n = \sigma_{ij} \, n_i \, n_j$ | Stress perpendicular to the plane |
| 3 | Shear Stress on Plane | $\tau = \sqrt{\vert T \vert^2 - \sigma_n^2}$ | Tangential stress on the plane |
| 4 | First Stress Invariant ($I_1$) | $I_1 = \sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}$ | Sum of normal stresses (related to volumetric change) |
| 5 | Second Stress Invariant ($I_2$) | $I_2 = \sigma_{xx}\sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz} + \sigma_{zz}\sigma_{xx} - \tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{zx}^2$ | Related to distortional energy |
| 6 | Third Stress Invariant ($I_3$) | $I_3 = \det(\sigma_{ij})$ | Determinant (sign indicates tension/compression) |
| 7 | Principal Stress Equation | $\sigma^3 - I_1\sigma^2 + I_2\sigma - I_3 = 0$ | Characteristic cubic giving σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ |
| 8 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | Largest shear stress in 3D |
| 9 | Octahedral Normal Stress | $\sigma_{\text{oct}} = \dfrac{I_1}{3} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$ | Hydrostatic component |
| 10 | Octahedral Shear Stress | $\tau_{\text{oct}} = \dfrac{1}{3}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ | Distortional stress on octahedral plane |
| 11 | Hydrostatic Component | $\sigma_{\text{hyd}} = \dfrac{I_1}{3}$ | Causes pure volume change |
| 12 | Deviatoric Stress | $s_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} - \sigma_{\text{hyd}} \delta_{ij}$ | Causes shape distortion |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Vessel Design:** Principal stresses govern wall thickness calculations in cylindrical and spherical vessels.
- **Yield Criteria (von Mises, Tresca):** All major failure theories depend on principal stresses.
- **Geomechanics:** Mohr-Coulomb failure in soils uses 3D stress invariants.
- **Fatigue Analysis:** Multiaxial fatigue life prediction requires full 3D stress state.
- **Composite Materials:** Stress transformation is essential for analyzing off-axis laminates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Cauchy's Stress Equation (Tetrahedron Argument)

**Setup:** Consider a small tetrahedron with three faces lying on the coordinate planes (x, y, z) and the fourth (inclined) face having outward unit normal **n** = (l, m, n) (i.e., n_x, n_y, n_z). The three orthogonal faces have areas dA_x, dA_y, dA_z respectively, and the inclined face has area dA.

By geometry, the projections give:
$$
dA_x = l \, dA, \quad dA_y = m \, dA, \quad dA_z = n \, dA
$$

**Equilibrium of forces on the tetrahedron:**

$$
\vec{T}^{(n)} \, dA = \vec{T}^{(x)} \, dA_x + \vec{T}^{(y)} \, dA_y + \vec{T}^{(z)} \, dA_z
$$

Substituting the area relations:

$$
\vec{T}^{(n)} = l \, \vec{T}^{(x)} + m \, \vec{T}^{(y)} + n \, \vec{T}^{(z)}
$$

Writing in index notation with T^(x)_i = σ_ij δ_jx = σ_ix:

$$
T_i^{(n)} = l \, \sigma_{ix} + m \, \sigma_{iy} + n \, \sigma_{iz} = \sigma_{ij} \, n_j
$$

This is the famous **Cauchy's Stress Formula**. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation II: Stress Transformation in 3D

Let a new coordinate system (x', y', z') be oriented relative to (x, y, z) by direction cosines l_ij. The transformed stress components are:

$$
\sigma'_{kl} = l_{ki} \, l_{lj} \, \sigma_{ij}
$$

**Detailed expansion of normal stress σ'_x'x':**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma'_{x'x'} &= l_{x'x}^2 \, \sigma_{xx} + l_{x'y}^2 \, \sigma_{yy} + l_{x'z}^2 \, \sigma_{zz} \\
&\quad + 2 l_{x'x} l_{x'y} \, \tau_{xy} + 2 l_{x'y} l_{x'z} \, \tau_{yz} + 2 l_{x'x} l_{x'z} \, \tau_{xz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Detailed expansion of shear stress τ'_x'y':**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau'_{x'y'} &= l_{x'x} l_{y'x} \, \sigma_{xx} + l_{x'y} l_{y'y} \, \sigma_{yy} + l_{x'z} l_{y'z} \, \sigma_{zz} \\
&\quad + (l_{x'y} l_{y'x} + l_{x'x} l_{y'y}) \, \tau_{xy} \\
&\quad + (l_{x'z} l_{y'y} + l_{x'y} l_{y'z}) \, \tau_{yz} \\
&\quad + (l_{x'x} l_{y'z} + l_{x'z} l_{y'x}) \, \tau_{xz}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation III: Principal Stress Equation (Characteristic Cubic)

We seek values of λ such that on the principal plane, the traction is purely normal:

$$
\sigma_{ij} \, n_j = \lambda \, n_i
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(\sigma_{ij} - \lambda \, \delta_{ij}) n_j = 0
$$

For a non-trivial solution (n ≠ 0), the determinant must vanish:

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx}-\lambda & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_{yy}-\lambda & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_{zz}-\lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0
$$

**Step 1: Expand the determinant along the first row:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\sigma_{xx}-\lambda)\bigl[(\sigma_{yy}-\lambda)(\sigma_{zz}-\lambda) - \tau_{yz}^2\bigr] \\
&\quad - \tau_{xy}\bigl[\tau_{xy}(\sigma_{zz}-\lambda) - \tau_{yz}\tau_{xz}\bigr] \\
&\quad + \tau_{xz}\bigl[\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz} - \tau_{xz}(\sigma_{yy}-\lambda)\bigr] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Expand each bracket:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\sigma_{yy}-\lambda)(\sigma_{zz}-\lambda) &= \sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz} - \lambda(\sigma_{yy}+\sigma_{zz}) + \lambda^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{xy}(\sigma_{zz}-\lambda) - \tau_{yz}\tau_{xz} &= \tau_{xy}\sigma_{zz} - \lambda\tau_{xy} - \tau_{yz}\tau_{xz}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz} - \tau_{xz}(\sigma_{yy}-\lambda) &= \tau_{xy}\tau_{yz} - \tau_{xz}\sigma_{yy} + \lambda\tau_{xz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute back and collect terms in powers of λ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\sigma_{xx}-\lambda)\bigl[\lambda^2 - \lambda(\sigma_{yy}+\sigma_{zz}) + \sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz} - \tau_{yz}^2\bigr] \\
&\quad - \tau_{xy}\bigl[\tau_{xy}\sigma_{zz} - \lambda\tau_{xy} - \tau_{yz}\tau_{xz}\bigr] \\
&\quad + \tau_{xz}\bigl[\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz} - \tau_{xz}\sigma_{yy} + \lambda\tau_{xz}\bigr] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Expanding the first term and distributing:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\sigma_{xx}\lambda^2 - \sigma_{xx}\lambda(\sigma_{yy}+\sigma_{zz}) + \sigma_{xx}\sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz} - \sigma_{xx}\tau_{yz}^2 \\
&\quad - \lambda^3 + \lambda^2(\sigma_{yy}+\sigma_{zz}) - \lambda\sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz} + \lambda\tau_{yz}^2 \\
&\quad - \tau_{xy}^2\sigma_{zz} + \lambda\tau_{xy}^2 + \tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{xz} \\
&\quad + \tau_{xz}\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz} - \tau_{xz}^2\sigma_{yy} + \lambda\tau_{xz}^2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Grouping by powers of λ (λ³, λ², λ¹, λ⁰):**

For **λ³**:
$$
-\lambda^3
$$

For **λ²**:
$$
\sigma_{xx}\lambda^2 + (\sigma_{yy}+\sigma_{zz})\lambda^2 = (\sigma_{xx}+\sigma_{yy}+\sigma_{zz})\lambda^2 = I_1 \lambda^2
$$

For **λ¹**:
$$
-\sigma_{xx}(\sigma_{yy}+\sigma_{zz})\lambda - \sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz}\lambda + \tau_{yz}^2\lambda + \tau_{xy}^2\lambda + \tau_{xz}^2\lambda
$$
$$
= -\lambda\bigl[\sigma_{xx}\sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz} + \sigma_{zz}\sigma_{xx} - \tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{xz}^2\bigr] = -I_2 \lambda
$$

For **λ⁰** (constant term):
$$
\sigma_{xx}\sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz} - \sigma_{xx}\tau_{yz}^2 - \sigma_{yy}\tau_{xz}^2 - \sigma_{zz}\tau_{xy}^2 + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{xz} = I_3
$$

**Step 6: Final compact form — the characteristic equation:**

$$
\boxed{\lambda^3 - I_1 \lambda^2 + I_2 \lambda - I_3 = 0}
$$

The three real roots are σ₁, σ₂, σ₃. ∎

## 3.4 Derivation IV: Maximum Shear Stress

On any plane, shear stress is:

$$
\tau^2 = \sigma_{ij}' n_i' n_j' - \sigma_n^2
$$

For the **principal reference frame**, where the only non-zero stresses are σ₁, σ₂, σ₃, and the plane has direction cosines (l, m, n):

$$
\sigma_n = l^2 \sigma_1 + m^2 \sigma_2 + n^2 \sigma_3
$$

The total traction magnitude squared is:

$$
T^2 = (l\sigma_1)^2 + (m\sigma_2)^2 + (n\sigma_3)^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tau^2 = l^2\sigma_1^2 + m^2\sigma_2^2 + n^2\sigma_3^2 - (l^2\sigma_1 + m^2\sigma_2 + n^2\sigma_3)^2
$$

Using Lagrange multipliers to maximize τ² subject to l² + m² + n² = 1, the **maximum** occurs when the plane bisects the angle between the maximum and minimum principal stress directions, yielding:

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}}
$$

This is the foundation of the **Tresca yield criterion**. ∎

## 3.5 Derivation V: Octahedral Stresses

The **octahedral plane** is the plane whose normal makes **equal angles with all three principal axes** (l = m = n = 1/√3). Substituting into the formulas:

**Octahedral normal stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{oct}} &= l^2 \sigma_1 + m^2 \sigma_2 + n^2 \sigma_3 \\
&= \frac{1}{3}(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3) = \frac{I_1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Octahedral shear stress:**

The total traction is:

$$
T^2 = l^2\sigma_1^2 + m^2\sigma_2^2 + n^2\sigma_3^2 = \frac{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2}{3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tau_{\text{oct}}^2 = T^2 - \sigma_{\text{oct}}^2 = \frac{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2}{3} - \frac{(\sigma_1+\sigma_2+\sigma_3)^2}{9}
$$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$
\tau_{\text{oct}}^2 = \frac{2(\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2) - 2(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)}{9}
$$

Recognizing the identity:

$$
(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2(\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2) - 2(\sigma_1\sigma_2+\sigma_2\sigma_3+\sigma_3\sigma_1)
$$

We get the **compact final formula**:

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\text{oct}} = \frac{1}{3}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}}
$$

∎
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of 3D Stress Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied External Loads] --> B[Internal Force Distribution in Body]
    B --> C[Stress Tensor at a Point sigma_ij]
    C --> D{Decomposition}
    D --> E[Hydrostatic Component sigma_hyd = I1/3]
    D --> F[Deviatoric Component s_ij]
    E --> G[Volume Change Effect]
    F --> H[Shape Distortion Effect]
    C --> I[Principal Stress Computation]
    I --> J[Eigenvalue Problem]
    J --> K[Characteristic Cubic sigma^3 - I1 sigma^2 + I2 sigma - I3 = 0]
    K --> L[Principal Stresses sigma1, sigma2, sigma3]
    L --> M[Principal Planes]
    L --> N[Maximum Shear Stress tau_max]
    L --> O[Octahedral Stresses sigma_oct, tau_oct]
    N --> P[Tresca Yield Criterion]
    O --> Q[von Mises Yield Criterion]
    P --> R[Engineering Failure Prediction]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Tetrahedron for Cauchy's Equation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Origin O] --> node2[Coordinate x axis]
    node1 --> node3[Coordinate y axis]
    node1 --> node4[Coordinate z axis]
    node2 --> node5[Inclined Face dA with normal n]
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node2 --> node6[Face on yz-plane dAx]
    node3 --> node7[Face on xz-plane dAy]
    node4 --> node8[Face on xy-plane dAz]
    node5 --> node9[Traction vector T n]
    node6 --> node10[Stress sigma_ij acts]
    node7 --> node10
    node8 --> node10
    node10 --> node11[Equilibrium yields T = sigma_ij n_j]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Principal Stress Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Input: Stress Tensor sigma_ij] --> stepB[Compute Invariants I1, I2, I3]
    stepB --> stepC[Form Characteristic Equation]
    stepC --> stepD[Solve Cubic Equation]
    stepD --> stepE{Real Roots Found?}
    stepE -->|Yes| stepF[Sort: sigma1 greater or equal sigma2 greater or equal sigma3]
    stepE -->|No| stepG[Recheck Tensor Symmetry]
    stepF --> stepH[Compute Direction Cosines from Eigenvectors]
    stepH --> stepI[Output: Principal Stresses and Principal Planes]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Stress Tensor Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tensor[Full Stress Tensor sigma_ij] --> hyd[Hydrostatic Tensor sigma_hyd times delta_ij]
    tensor --> dev[Deviatoric Tensor s_ij]
    hyd --> vol[Pure Volume Change]
    dev --> dist[Pure Shape Distortion]
    vol --> oct_n[Octahedral Normal Stress]
    dist --> oct_s[Octahedral Shear Stress]
```
