$$dU = \frac{M^{2}}{2EI}\, dx = \frac{1}{2EI}\left(\frac{W}{2}x\right)^{2} dx = \frac{W^{2} x^{2}}{8EI}\, dx$$

**Step 4:** Integrate over the left half (by symmetry, double the result for the full beam):

$$U_{left} = \int_{0}^{L/2} \frac{W^{2} x^{2}}{8EI}\, dx = \frac{W^{2}}{8EI} \left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{L/2} = \frac{W^{2}}{8EI} \cdot \frac{L^{3}}{24}$$

$$U_{left} = \frac{W^{2} L^{3}}{192 EI}$$

**Step 5:** Total strain energy (by symmetry, double the half):

$$\boxed{U = \frac{W^{2} L^{3}}{96 EI}}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 50$ mm $\Rightarrow$ radius $r = 25$ mm = $0.025$ m
- Length $L = 1.5$ m
- Torque $T = 2$ kN·m = $2 \times 10^{3}$ N·m
- $G = 80$ GPa = $80 \times 10^{9}$ N/m²

**Step 1:** Polar moment of inertia for solid circular shaft:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^{4}}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = \frac{19.635 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 6.1359 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 2:** Apply torsion strain energy formula:

$$U = \frac{T^{2} L}{2 G J}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$U = \frac{(2 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 1.5}{2 \times 80 \times 10^{9} \times 6.1359 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$U = \frac{4 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5}{2 \times 80 \times 10^{9} \times 6.1359 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$U = \frac{6 \times 10^{6}}{98{,}174} \approx 61.12 \text{ J}$$

**Answer:** $\boxed{U \approx 61.12 \text{ J}}$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a)** A weight of 2 kN falls through a height of 50 mm onto a collar at the lower end of a vertical bar of length 3 m, area 600 mm², and $E = 200$ GPa. Find: (i) static deflection, (ii) instantaneous deflection, and (iii) maximum stress in the bar. (7 marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between gradually applied, suddenly applied, and impact loads with neat energy-balance diagrams. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:**
- Falling weight, $W = 2$ kN = $2000$ N
- Height of fall, $h = 50$ mm = $0.05$ m
- Bar length, $L = 3$ m
- Area, $A = 600$ mm² = $600 \times 10^{-6}$ m²
- $E = 200 \times 10^{9}$ N/m²

**Step 1: Static Deflection ($\delta_{st}$):**

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{WL}{AE} = \frac{2000 \times 3}{600 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{6000}{120 \times 10^{6}} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Instantaneous (Impact) Deflection ($\delta_d$):**

$$\delta_{d} = \delta_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{st}}}\right]$$

$$\frac{2h}{\delta_{st}} = \frac{2 \times 0.05}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.1}{0.00005} = 2000$$

$$\delta_{d} = 0.05 \times 10^{-3}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 2000}\right] = 0.05 \times 10^{-3} \times \left[1 + 44.71\right]$$

$$\delta_{d} = 0.05 \times 10^{-3} \times 45.71 = 2.286 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 2.286 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Stress ($\sigma_{max}$):**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{W \cdot \delta_{d}}{\delta_{st} \cdot A} = \frac{2000 \times 2.286 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-5} \times 600 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{4.572}{3 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.524 \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}^{2} \approx 152.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\delta_{st} = 0.05$ mm
- $\delta_{d} = 2.286$ mm
- $\sigma_{max} = 152.4$ MPa

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Feature | Gradual Load | Sudden Load | Impact Load |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Application** | Load increases slowly from 0 to $P$ | Load $P$ applied instantaneously (no fall) | Load $W$ falls from height $h$ |
| **External Work $W_{ext}$** | $\frac{1}{2} P \delta$ | $P \delta$ | $W(h + \delta_d)$ |
| **Strain Energy $U$** | $\frac{1}{2} P \delta$ | $\frac{1}{2} P \delta$ | $W(h + \delta_d)$ |
| **Kinetic Energy $KE$** | $0$ | $\frac{1}{2} P \delta$ | $0$ (at instant of max deflection) |
| **Deflection Ratio** | $\delta_{st}$ | $2 \delta_{st}$ | $\delta_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_{st}}\right]$ |
| **Stress Ratio** | $1$ | $2$ | $1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_{st}}$ |
| **Energy Diagram** | Triangular | Full rectangle (split in half) | Wedge plus triangle |

> **Key Insight:** As the severity of loading increases (gradual → sudden → impact), the strain energy stored in the body **increases dramatically**, leading to higher deflections and stresses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in gradually applied loads.** Many students wrongly write $W = P\delta$ for a gradual load, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency.** Always convert mm² to m², kN to N, GPa to N/m² **before** substitution. Wrong units = 0 marks in numerical problems.
> 3. **Sign errors in impact formula.** Take only the **positive root** of the quadratic; the negative root has no physical meaning.
> 4. **Confusing sudden load with gradual load.** Sudden = $W = P\delta$, deflection = $2\delta_{st}$. Sudden does **not** involve height $h$ — that is *impact*.
> 5. **Missing the kinetic energy component.** Under sudden loading, half the work goes to KE (vibration), not strain energy.
> 6. **Applying bending energy formula with non-uniform section without integration.** Always check if $M$ varies along the length.
> 7. **Polar moment of inertia $J$ vs. $I$ confusion.** $J$ is for torsion; $I$ is for bending. $J = 2I$ only for a solid circular section.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Work Done by Forces and Elastic Strain Energy**

- **Strain Energy Definition:** $U$ = Area under load–deflection curve; for linear elastic body, $U = \frac{1}{2} P \delta$.
- **Gradual Load:** $W = U = \frac{1}{2} P \delta_{st}$ — all work is stored.
- **Sudden Load:** $W = P \cdot 2\delta_{st}$, $U = \frac{1}{2} P \cdot 2\delta_{st} = P\delta_{st}$, $KE = P\delta_{st}$. Deflection doubles.
- **Impact Load:** $\delta_d = \delta_{st}\left[1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_{st}}\right]$; stress amplified by the same factor.
- **Axial Energy:** $U = P^{2} L / (2AE)$.
- **Bending Energy:** $U = \displaystyle\int_0^L \frac{M^{2}}{2EI}\, dx$.
- **Torsional Energy:** $U = T^{2} L / (2GJ)$.
- **Shear Energy:** $U = \displaystyle\int_0^L \frac{F_s^{2}}{2GA}\, dx$.
- **Proof Resilience:** Maximum $U$ before permanent deformation; measured up to elastic limit.
- **Modulus of Resilience:** $u_r = \sigma_y^{2} / (2E)$ — material property.
- **Strain Energy Decomposition:** $u = u_v + u_d$ (volumetric + distortional).
- **Distortional Energy** governs yielding in ductile materials → foundation of **von Mises criterion**.
- **Key Assumption:** All formulas are for **linear elastic**, homogeneous, isotropic materials within Hooke's law limits.
- **Units Checklist:** Force in N, length in m, area in m², stress/Young's modulus in Pa or N/m²; energy in J.
- **Master Trick for Impact:** When $h \gg \delta_{st}$, simplify $\delta_d \approx \sqrt{2h\delta_{st}}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocal Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reciprocal Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Reciprocal Relations

In **Advanced Solid Mechanics**, **Reciprocal Relations** constitute a class of powerful theorems that establish symmetry between two independent loading systems acting on the same linearly elastic body. The most celebrated among them is the **Maxwell–Betti Reciprocal Work Theorem**, often simply called **Betti's Theorem**, which forms the mathematical backbone of numerous structural analysis techniques such as the **Unit Load Method**, **Influence Line Diagrams**, and **Flexibility Matrix formulation** in the flexibility (force) method of structural analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> *Reciprocal Relations* are mathematical identities that interrelate the work done (or displacements produced) by one system of forces acting on an elastic body with the work done (or displacements produced) by another independent system of forces acting on the *same* body. The principle is grounded in the **linearity of the stress–strain relationship** and the **uniqueness of the strain energy function** in linear elastostatics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Friends and the Spring"

Imagine two friends, **A** and **B**, pulling on opposite ends of a single linearly elastic spring.

* **Friend A** pulls with force $P_A$ and produces a small extension $\delta_A$. Friend B simultaneously pulls with force $P_B$ producing extension $\delta_B$.
* **Betti's Theorem** says: *The extra work done by Friend A's force as the spring stretches further under Friend B's pull is exactly the same as the extra work done by Friend B's force as the spring stretches further under Friend A's pull.*

In a 2D truss, beam, or frame, the same logic applies — except that now "extra stretch" means **deflection at a point** caused by a load at *another* point. The reciprocity is fundamentally a statement that **cause and effect can be interchanged** without changing the total work exchanged between the structure and the loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Reciprocal Relations Matter to an Engineer**
> 1. They allow a single set of computations to yield *multiple* influence coefficients.
> 2. They provide a **symmetric flexibility matrix**, drastically reducing computational effort in the **force method**.
> 3. They form the theoretical foundation of the **Unit Load Method** for computing deflections in trusses, beams, and frames.
> 4. They enable the construction of **Maxwell's diagrams** and **Müller-Breslau Principle** for influence lines.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $P_i, Q_i$ | Applied Point Forces (Load Systems 1 and 2) | **Newton (N)** |
| $\delta_{ij}$ | Displacement at point *i* in direction of $P_i$ due to load at *j* | **metre (m)** |
| $U$ | Strain Energy | **Joule (J) = N·m** |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus | **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²** |
| $I$ | Second Moment of Area | **m⁴** |
| $A$ | Cross-Sectional Area | **m²** |

## 1.4 Historical Context (Syllabus Note)

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Insight**
> * **James Clerk Maxwell (1864)** first stated the displacement-reciprocal theorem for trusses.
> * **Enrico Betti (1872)** generalized Maxwell's result to a complete reciprocal-work theorem valid for *any* linearly elastic continuum.
> * The theorem is therefore correctly known as the **Maxwell–Betti Reciprocal Work Theorem**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions Underlying the Reciprocal Relations

For the theorems to be strictly valid, the structural system **must** satisfy the following conditions:

1. The material is **linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
2. The deformations are **small** (geometrically linear regime).
3. The supports do **not yield** (or their yielding is included in the displacement count).
4. **Temperature effects** are neglected (or constant, with no thermally induced strains).
5. The principle of **superposition** holds.
6. The structure obeys the **uniqueness of the strain energy density function**: $U$ is a single-valued, quadratic function of the displacements.

## 2.2 Statement of Betti's Reciprocal Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Betti's Reciprocal Work Theorem (General Statement)**
> If a linearly elastic body is subjected to **two independent and complete systems of forces**, $P_i$ and $Q_i$ ($i = 1, 2, \dots, n$), acting on it in any sequence, then the **work done by the forces of the first system acting through the displacements produced by the second system** is equal to the **work done by the forces of the second system acting through the displacements produced by the first system**.

Mathematically, Betti's theorem is expressed as:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \, \delta_{iQ} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \, \delta_{iP}
$$

where:
* $\delta_{iQ}$ = displacement at the point and in the direction of force $P_i$ caused **solely** by the $Q$-system.
* $\delta_{iP}$ = displacement at the point and in the direction of force $Q_i$ caused **solely** by the $P$-system.

## 2.3 Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem (Deflection Theorem)

A direct and extremely useful **corollary** of Betti's theorem is **Maxwell's Reciprocal Deflection Theorem**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem**
> The deflection at point **A** in a given direction due to a **unit load** applied at point **B** in some other (or the same) direction is equal in magnitude to the deflection at point **B** in the direction of that unit load due to a **unit load** applied at point **A** in the original direction.

$$
\delta_{AB} \;=\; \delta_{BA}
$$

This theorem is the theoretical basis for the symmetry of the **flexibility influence coefficient matrix** $[F]$ in the force method, where $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$.

## 2.4 Proof Logic (Conceptual Flow)

| Step | Action | Strain Energy Contribution |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Apply $P$-system fully | $\frac{1}{2} \sum P_i \delta_{ii}$ |
| 2 | Then apply $Q$-system, $P$ stays | $\sum P_i \delta_{iQ} + \frac{1}{2}\sum Q_i \delta_{jj}$ |
| 3 | Total work (Path A) | $W_A = \frac{1}{2}\sum P_i \delta_{ii} + \sum P_i \delta_{iQ} + \frac{1}{2}\sum Q_i \delta_{jj}$ |
| 4 | Reverse: Apply $Q$ first, then $P$ | $\frac{1}{2}\sum Q_i \delta_{jj} + \sum Q_i \delta_{iP} + \frac{1}{2}\sum P_i \delta_{ii}$ |
| 5 | Total work (Path B) | $W_B = \frac{1}{2}\sum Q_i \delta_{jj} + \sum Q_i \delta_{iP} + \frac{1}{2}\sum P_i \delta_{ii}$ |
| 6 | Path Independence: $W_A = W_B$ | $\sum P_i \delta_{iQ} = \sum Q_i \delta_{iP}$ |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sum P_i \, \delta_{iQ} = \sum Q_i \, \delta_{iP}$ | **Betti's Reciprocal Work Theorem** | General 2-system loading |
| 2 | $\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA}$ | **Maxwell's Reciprocal Deflection** | Unit load at A vs unit load at B |
| 3 | $\Delta = \sum \frac{N \, n \, L}{AE}$ | Truss deflection (real $N$ × virtual $n$ × $L$ / $AE$) | Truss problems |
| 4 | $\Delta = \int \frac{M \, m}{EI} \, dx$ | Beam deflection (bending) | Beams |
| 5 | $\Delta = \int \frac{M \, m}{EI} \, dx + \int \frac{N \, n}{AE} \, dx + k \int \frac{V \, v}{GA} \, dx$ | General Unit Load Method (frame) | Plane frames |
| 6 | $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$ | **Symmetry of Flexibility Matrix** | Force method of analysis |
| 7 | $u_{ij} = u_{ji}$ | **Symmetry of Stiffness Matrix** | Displacement method (complementary) |
| 8 | $W_{12} = W_{21}$ | Path-independence of work in linear elastic body | Path integrals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> Reciprocal relations power **Structural Health Monitoring** algorithms (where the symmetry of the dynamic stiffness matrix is exploited), **Influence Line construction** for moving loads on bridges, and the **Mutual Energy Theorem** used in acoustic and seismic analysis of buildings. In **Finite Element Method (FEM)** software, the global stiffness matrix is symmetric precisely because of Maxwell–Betti reciprocity.

## 2.6 Connection to the Unit Load Method

The **Unit Load Method** (also called the **Virtual Work Method** for deflections) is a direct numerical consequence of Betti's theorem. By choosing:
* $P$-system = actual loads on the structure,
* $Q$-system = a *virtual* **unit load** applied at the point and in the direction of the desired deflection,

we obtain:
$$
\Delta = \sum_{i} P_i \, \delta_{iQ} \quad (\text{from Betti})
$$
which, when the work is computed as internal strain energy, yields the classical unit-load integral:
$$
\Delta = \int \frac{M \, m}{EI} \, dx
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Betti's Reciprocal Theorem

### Setup

Consider a linearly elastic body. Let there be **two complete, independent systems of external forces**:
* **System 1 (P-System):** $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$
* **System 2 (Q-System):** $Q_1, Q_2, \ldots, Q_n$

Let $\delta_{ij}^{(P)}$ denote the displacement at the point of application of $P_i$ (and in its direction) caused by unit value of $P_j$. By linearity, the displacement at $P_i$ due to the *full* P-system is:

$$
\delta_{i}^{(P)} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \, \delta_{ij}^{(P)}
$$

Similarly, the displacement at $Q_i$ due to the full Q-system is:

$$
\delta_{i}^{(Q)} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} Q_j \, \delta_{ij}^{(Q)}
$$

### Path A: Apply P-system First, Then Q-system

**Step 1 — Apply the P-system gradually from 0 to its full value.**

In a linear elastic system, the work done is purely quadratic:

$$
W_1 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \, \delta_{i}^{(P)} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_i \, P_j \, \delta_{ij}^{(P)}
$$

**Step 2 — Hold the P-system at its full value, then gradually apply the Q-system from 0 to full.**

* The P-forces do additional work as the Q-system causes further displacements at their points of application: $\delta_{iQ}$.
* The Q-forces themselves do their own quadratic work.

Therefore, the additional work is:

$$
W_2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \, \delta_{iQ} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \, \delta_{i}^{(Q)}
$$

**Step 3 — Total work done by the external loads in Path A:**

$$
W_A = W_1 + W_2 = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} P_i \delta_{i}^{(P)} + \sum_{i} P_i \delta_{iQ} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} Q_i \delta_{i}^{(Q)}
$$

### Path B: Apply Q-system First, Then P-system (Reverse Order)

By the **same logic**, the total work done by the external loads along Path B is:

$$
W_B = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} Q_i \delta_{i}^{(Q)} + \sum_{i} Q_i \delta_{iP} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} P_i \delta_{i}^{(P)}
$$

### Key Justification: Path Independence of Work in a Conservative System

The work done by the external forces on a **linearly elastic conservative system** is stored as **strain energy** in the body. Since strain energy is a *state function* of the final configuration, the total strain energy (and hence the total external work) is **independent of the order of application** of the loads.

$$
W_A = W_B
$$

### Equating the Two Paths

$$
\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} P_i \delta_{i}^{(P)} + \sum_{i} P_i \delta_{iQ} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} Q_i \delta_{i}^{(Q)} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} Q_i \delta_{i}^{(Q)} + \sum_{i} Q_i \delta_{iP} + \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} P_i \delta_{i}^{(P)}
$$

**Cancelling identical quadratic terms on both sides:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \, \delta_{iQ} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \, \delta_{iP}
$$

This is **Betti's Reciprocal Work Theorem**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem (Deflection)

### Setup

Consider a linearly elastic structure. Apply:
* **System 1:** A **unit load** at point **A**, directed along the direction in which the deflection at B is desired.
* **System 2:** A **unit load** at point **B**, directed along the direction in which the deflection at A is desired.

### Direct Application of Betti's Theorem

Setting $P_A = 1$ (with all other $P_i = 0$) and $Q_B = 1$ (with all other $Q_i = 0$), Betti's identity becomes:

$$
(1) \cdot \delta_{AQ}^{(B)} + \sum_{i \neq A} 0 = (1) \cdot \delta_{BP}^{(A)} + \sum_{i \neq B} 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA}\;}
$$

where:
* $\delta_{AB}$ = deflection at **A** in the direction of the unit load at A, caused by the **unit load at B**.
* $\delta_{BA}$ = deflection at **B** in the direction of the unit load at B, caused by the **unit load at A**.

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Truss Deflection via Betti's Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A simply-supported truss of span $L = 4 \text{ m}$ carries a downward load of $P = 10 \text{ kN}$ at joint B (the centre). Using Betti's Theorem, find the **horizontal deflection of joint C**, given the flexibility influence coefficient $\delta_{CA}^{P} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/kN}$ when a unit horizontal load is applied at C.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the two systems:**

* **P-system (real system):** $P_B = 10 \text{ kN}$ vertical downward at joint B.
* **Q-system (virtual system):** $Q_C = 1 \text{ kN}$ horizontal at joint C (unit load).

**Step 2 — Write Betti's identity:**

$$
\sum P_i \, \delta_{iQ} = \sum Q_i \, \delta_{iP}
$$

The only nonzero terms are $P_B$ and $Q_C$:

$$
P_B \cdot \delta_{B}^{(Q)} = Q_C \cdot \delta_{C}^{(P)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
(10 \text{ kN}) \cdot \delta_{B}^{(Q)} = (1 \text{ kN}) \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/kN}) \cdot (10 \text{ kN})
$$

$$
\delta_{B}^{(Q)} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10}{10} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret result:**

The horizontal deflection at C, $\delta_C^{(P)}$, is *also* $1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, because by Betti's theorem the cross-flexibility coefficients are symmetric. The vertical deflection at B due to the unit horizontal load at C equals the horizontal deflection at C due to the actual vertical load at B.

$$
\boxed{\Delta_{C,\text{horizontal}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.5 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Symmetry of the Flexibility Matrix

For a structure with $n$ degrees of freedom, the **flexibility matrix** $[F]$ is defined by:

$$
\{\Delta\} = [F] \, \{X\}
$$

where $\{X\}$ is a vector of redundant (or generalized) forces and $\{\Delta\}$ is the corresponding vector of displacements.

**From Maxwell–Betti reciprocity:**

$$
F_{ij} = \delta_{ij} = \delta_{ji} = F_{ji}
$$

Therefore:

$$
[F] = [F]^T
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequence:** In the **force method** of analysis, the flexibility matrix is always **symmetric**. This allows for major computational savings and is a key reason the force method is mathematically elegant for statically indeterminate structures with few redundants.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart of Betti's Theorem Proof

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Linearly Elastic Body"]
    nodeB["Apply System P (Real Loads)"]
    nodeC["Compute Work W1 = 1/2 Sum P_i delta_iP"]
    nodeD["Hold P Constant and Apply System Q"]
    nodeE["Compute Extra Work W2 = Sum P_i delta_iQ + 1/2 Sum Q_i delta_iQ"]
    nodeF["Path A Total Work = W1 + W2"]
    nodeG["Apply System Q First (Reverse Path)"]
    nodeH["Compute Work W1 prime = 1/2 Sum Q_i delta_iQ"]
    nodeI["Apply System P After Q"]
    nodeJ["Compute Extra Work W2 prime = Sum Q_i delta_iP + 1/2 Sum P_i delta_iP"]
    nodeK["Path B Total Work = W1 prime + W2 prime"]
    nodeL["Path Independence: W_A equals W_B"]
    nodeM["Cancel Identical Quadratic Terms"]
    nodeN["BETTI THEOREM: Sum P_i delta_iQ = Sum Q_i delta_iP"]
    nodeO["Special Case Unit Loads: delta_AB = delta_BA"]
    nodeP["APPLICATIONS: Unit Load Method, Influence Lines, Flexibility Matrix Symmetry"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeF --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM --> nodeN --> nodeO --> nodeP
```

## 4.2 Application Architecture: From Betti to Practical Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sysA["Real Load System P"]
        pnode1["External Forces P_i"]
        pnode2["Real Internal Forces M, N, V"]
    end

    subgraph sysB["Virtual Load System Q (Unit Load)"]
        qnode1["Unit Load 1 at Desired DOF"]
        qnode2["Virtual Internal Forces m, n, v"]
    end

    betti["BETTI RECIPROCAL WORK THEOREM"]
    formula["Delta = Integral M m over EI dx plus Integral N n over AE dx plus k Integral V v over GA dx"]
    output["Required Deflection or Rotation"]

    pnode2 --> betti
    qnode2 --> betti
    betti --> formula --> output
```

## 4.3 Diagram: Symmetry of the Flexibility Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    f11["F11: Deflection at 1 due to X1 = 1"]
    f22["F22: Deflection at 2 due to X2 = 1"]
    f12["F12: Deflection at 1 due to X2 = 1"]
    f21["F21: Deflection at 2 due to X1 = 1"]

    f12 --- symNote["MAXWELL RECIPROCITY: F12 = F21"]
    f21 --- symNote
    f11 --- mat["FLEXIBILITY MATRIX F is SYMMETRIC"]
    f22 --- mat
    f12 --- mat
    f21 --- mat
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: How Reciprocal Relations are Used in the Force Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1["Identify Degree of Static Indeterminacy: n"]
    input2["Choose n Redundant Forces as X1, X2, ..., Xn"]
    input3["Compute Flexibility Influence Coefficients F_ij using Unit Load Method"]
    input4["Apply Reciprocity: F_ij = F_ji for ALL i and j"]
    input5["Form Symmetric Flexibility Matrix F of size n by n"]
    input6["Set up Compatibility Equations: F times X plus Delta_0 = 0"]
    input7["Solve Linear System for Redundants X"]
    input8["Compute Final Internal Forces and Desired Deflections"]

    input1 --> input2 --> input3 --> input4 --> input5 --> input6 --> input7 --> input8
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q:** State **Betti's Reciprocal Theorem** as applied to a linearly elastic body. Mention the essential assumptions.

**Model Answer:**

> *Betti's Reciprocal Work Theorem* states that if a linearly elastic body is subjected to two independent and complete systems of forces, $P_i$ and $Q_i$, then the work done by the first system of forces acting through the displacements produced by the second system is equal to the work done by the second system of forces acting through the displacements produced by the first system.
>
> **Mathematical Statement:**
> $$\sum_{i} P_i \, \delta_{iQ} = \sum_{i} Q_i \, \delta_{iP}$$
>
> **Essential Assumptions:**
> 1. The material is **linearly elastic** and obeys Hooke's Law.
> 2. The deformations are **small** (geometric linearity).
> 3. Supports are **rigid** (no settlement).
> 4. Temperature variations are **absent** or constant.
> 5. The structure obeys the **principle of superposition**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q:** What is **Maxwell's Reciprocal Deflection Theorem**? How is it a special case of Betti's theorem?

**Model Answer:**

> *Maxwell's Reciprocal Deflection Theorem* is a corollary of Betti's theorem, applicable to unit loads. It states that the deflection at point A produced by a unit load applied at point B is equal to the deflection at point B produced by a unit load applied at point A:
> $$\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA}$$
>
> **As a Special Case of Betti's Theorem:**
> Setting $P_i = 1$ (unit load at A) and $Q_i = 1$ (unit load at B) in Betti's identity, all other terms vanish, leaving $\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA}$. This proves Maxwell's theorem is Betti's theorem applied to *unit* load systems.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> * Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** by failing to explicitly *state* the assumptions of the reciprocal theorem before applying it. **Always** begin the answer with: "For a linearly elastic body obeying Hooke's Law and small deformations..."
> * A common error is to apply Maxwell's theorem to **non-linear** or **inelastic** systems — this is a strict no-no and will attract zero marks.
> * In derivation problems, students must **show the path-independence argument** ($W_A = W_B$). Skipping this justification is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> * Unit consistency (N, m, Pa) is mandatory. Watch for mixed units in numerical problems.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

A simply supported beam **ABC** of span $L = 6 \text{ m}$ carries a point load $P = 20 \text{ kN}$ at the mid-span B. Using **Betti's Reciprocal Theorem** (or **Maxwell's Theorem**), determine the **slope at support A** and verify it using the standard double-integration method. Take $EI = 6000 \text{ kN·m}^2$ (constant).

#### (a) Find the slope at A using Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem — **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the two systems:**

* **Real System (P):** Concentrated load $P = 20 \text{ kN}$ downward at B (mid-span).
* **Virtual System (Q):** Apply a **unit moment** $M = 1$ kN·m at support A in the direction of the desired slope.

**Step 2 — Compute slope at A in the real system using the standard formula:**

For a simply supported beam of span $L$ with central point load $P$:

$$
\theta_A = \frac{P L^2}{16 \, EI}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
\theta_A = \frac{20 \times 6^2}{16 \times 6000} = \frac{20 \times 36}{96000} = \frac{720}{96000} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — State Maxwell's Reciprocal Result:**

By Maxwell's theorem, the slope at A due to $P$ at B equals the *deflection at B due to a unit moment at A*. The corresponding conjugate problem yields the **deflection at the centre due to an end moment** (for a simply supported beam of span $L$):

$$
\delta_{B}^{(M=1)} = \frac{L^2}{16 \, EI} = \frac{36}{16 \times 6000} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/kN·m}
$$

Multiplying by $P$ recovers $\theta_A$:

$$
\theta_A = P \times \frac{L^2}{16 \, EI} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\theta_A = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} = 0.0075 \text{ rad}}$

#### (b) Verify using double-integration method — **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Reactions:**

By symmetry: $R_A = R_C = P/2 = 10 \text{ kN}$.

**Step 2 — Bending moment expression** (origin at A, $0 \le x \le L/2$):

$$
M(x) = R_A \cdot x = 10x \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Governing differential equation:**

$$
EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = 10x
$$

**Step 4 — First integration (slope):**

$$
EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \int 10x \, dx = 5x^2 + C_1
$$

**Step 5 — Second integration (deflection):**

$$
EI \, y = \int (5x^2 + C_1) \, dx = \frac{5x^3}{3} + C_1 x + C_2
$$

**Step 6 — Boundary conditions:**

* At $x = 0$, $y = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.
* At $x = L/2 = 3$ m, slope is zero (by symmetry): $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$.

$$
0 = 5(3)^2 + C_1 \Rightarrow C_1 = -45
$$

**Step 7 — Slope at A ($x = 0$):**

$$
EI \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = C_1 = -45 \text{ kN·m}^2
$$

$$
\theta_A = \frac{-45}{6000} = -7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Magnitude:** $\boxed{\vert \theta_A \vert = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} \quad \text{(Verified ✓)}}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]`

For a **propped cantilever beam** of span $L = 4 \text{ m}$ fixed at A and simply supported at B:

* (a) Using **Betti's Theorem**, derive the expression for the flexibility coefficient $F_{11}$ and hence find the redundant reaction at B when a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity $w = 10 \text{ kN/m}$ acts over the full span. **(7 Marks)**
* (b) Using the result, compute the **deflection at the free end of the corresponding cantilever** under the same UDL, and comment on the symmetry of the flexibility matrix. **(7 Marks)**

#### (a) Derivation of $F_{11}$ and Redundant $R_B$ — **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose the redundant:**

Let $X_1 = R_B$ (vertical reaction at the simple support B). The released structure is a **cantilever** of span $L$ fixed at A and free at B.

**Step 2 — Define flexibility coefficient $F_{11}$:**

$F_{11}$ = deflection at B (in the direction of $X_1$, i.e., vertical) caused by a *unit* value of $X_1$ acting alone on the released cantilever.

For a cantilever with a tip point load:

$$
F_{11} = \frac{L^3}{3EI}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta_{10}$ (deflection at B in released structure due to applied UDL):**

For a cantilever with UDL over the full span:

$$
\Delta_{10} = \frac{w L^4}{8EI}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Betti's theorem in the form of the flexibility method:**

The compatibility equation at B is:

$$
F_{11} \cdot X_1 + \Delta_{10} = 0
$$

(Note: the negative sign indicates upward direction of $R_B$.)

**Step 5 — Solve for the redundant:**

$$
X_1 = -\frac{\Delta_{10}}{F_{11}} = -\frac{w L^4 / (8EI)}{L^3 / (3EI)} = -\frac{3wL}{8}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute values:**

$$
X_1 = -\frac{3 \times 10 \times 4}{8} = -15 \text{ kN}
$$

The negative sign indicates the redundant reaction acts **upward**.

$$
\boxed{R_B = 15 \text{ kN} \;\; (\uparrow)}
$$

#### (b) Deflection at Free End (Cantilever) & Symmetry Commentary — **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The deflection at the free end of the cantilever under the UDL (no support at B):**

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{w L^4}{8EI} = \frac{10 \times 4^4}{8 \times EI} = \frac{10 \times 256}{8 \, EI} = \frac{320}{EI} \text{ m}
$$

For $EI = 8000 \text{ kN·m}^2$ (assumed):

$$
\delta_{tip} = \frac{320}{8000} = 0.04 \text{ m} = 40 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify by Betti's reciprocity:**

The cross-flexibility coefficient $F_{12}$ (deflection at B due to a unit moment at A, or equivalently, the slope at A due to a unit vertical load at B) equals $F_{21}$ by Maxwell's theorem.

**Step 3 — Comment on symmetry:**

> The **flexibility matrix** of this 1-DOF statically indeterminate beam is a $1 \times 1$ scalar (trivially symmetric). For higher-indeterminacy structures, the matrix would be:
> $$[F] = \begin{bmatrix} F_{11} & F_{12} \\ F_{21} & F_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \quad F_{12} = F_{21}$$
> This symmetry is a **direct mathematical consequence of Betti's Reciprocal Theorem** and is one of the most powerful computational shortcuts in structural analysis.

$$
\boxed{[F]^T = [F] \quad \text{(Symmetry verified ✓)}}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Reciprocal Relations**

- [x] **Betti's Theorem** is a *work-equivalence* theorem: $\sum P_i \delta_{iQ} = \sum Q_i \delta_{iP}$.
- [x] **Maxwell's Theorem** is a special case (unit loads): $\delta_{AB} = \delta_{BA}$.
- [x] Both theorems require **linear elastic material**, **small deformations**, and **rigid supports**.
- [x] The theorems rely on **path-independence** of work in a conservative elastic system.
- [x] The **flexibility matrix** $[F]$ in the force method is always **symmetric**: $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$.
- [x] The **Unit Load Method** for deflection is a *direct corollary* of Betti's theorem.
- [x] Reciprocity is the foundation of **Müller-Breslau Principle** for influence lines.
- [x] In **FEM**, the global stiffness matrix $[K]$ is symmetric, and $K_{ij} = K_{ji}$ — a direct result of Maxwell–Betti reciprocity applied at the element level.
- [x] Reciprocal relations **DO NOT** apply to: plastic deformation, large deformations (geometric non-linearity), viscoelastic materials, or temperature-induced strains (without modification).
- [x] Always **state assumptions** before applying reciprocal theorems in KTU exam answers.
- [x] Units: Force in **N** or **kN**, length in **m** or **mm**, $E$ in **Pa** or **kN/m²**, $I$ in **m⁴** or **mm⁴**, $EI$ in **N·m²** or **kN·m²**.
- [x] Common **KTU examiner traps**: forgetting to convert $E$ in MPa to Pa, mixing $I$ in mm⁴ with lengths in m, omitting the negative sign convention for upward redundants.
- [x] **Sign convention**: downward deflections positive; clockwise moments positive; upward forces positive.
- [x] Reciprocal relations are the **mathematical signature** of linear elasticity — wherever you see a symmetric stiffness or flexibility matrix, Betti's theorem is at work in the background.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Theorem of Virtual Work

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem of Virtual Work (TVW):** *For a deformable body in static equilibrium under the action of a system of external forces (body forces and surface tractions), the total virtual work done by the external forces through an arbitrary virtual displacement field, which is kinematically admissible (i.e., compatible with the constraints and zero on the prescribed displacement boundary), is equal to the virtual work done by the internal stresses through the corresponding virtual strains.*

In its most compact vector-tensor form, the Principle of Virtual Displacements is stated as:

$$
\int_{V} \mathbf{f} \cdot \delta \mathbf{u} \, dV \;+\; \int_{S_T} \mathbf{T} \cdot \delta \mathbf{u} \, dS \;=\; \int_{V} \boldsymbol{\sigma} : \delta \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \, dV
$$

For a system of discrete rigid bodies or particles, it degenerates to the classical D'Alembert form:

$$
\delta W \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{F}_{i} \cdot \delta \mathbf{r}_{i} \;=\; 0
$$

### 1.2 Foundational Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Virtual Displacement** | $\delta \mathbf{u}$ | An *imaginary*, *infinitesimally small*, *kinematically admissible* change in configuration. Not a real motion. |
| **Virtual Strain** | $\delta \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ | The strain tensor corresponding to the virtual displacement field. |
| **External Virtual Work** | $\delta W_{ext}$ | Work done by the real external forces through the virtual displacement. |
| **Internal Virtual Work** | $\delta W_{int}$ | Work done by the real internal stresses through the virtual strains. |
| **Kinematically Admissible** | — | A virtual displacement that satisfies compatibility, continuity, and is **zero on essential (Dirichlet) boundaries**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction:** A *virtual* displacement ($\delta u$) is **not** a *real* displacement ($du$). The symbol $\delta$ denotes a *variation* — a mathematical probe — whereas $d$ denotes a *physical differential*. The virtual displacement does **not** consume time, is not a motion, and requires no inertia term.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Imagine If" Probe

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a heavy suitcase resting motionless on a hotel luggage scale. The scale reads its true weight because the system is in equilibrium. Now, *imagine* (do not actually do it!) nudging the suitcase by an imperceptibly tiny amount in any direction, while keeping it resting on the scale. Because the suitcase was already in perfect balance, the *first-order* work done by gravity, the normal reaction, and your imaginary nudge must sum to zero. That "imaginary nudge" is the **virtual displacement**, and the resulting mathematical statement is the **Theorem of Virtual Work**. The theorem does **not** predict motion — it **certifies equilibrium**.

The *kinematically admissible* requirement is exactly equivalent to saying: the imagined nudge is allowed by all the physical constraints (the suitcase cannot pass through the floor, the handle cannot stretch, etc.). Any violation of constraints would create *unphysical* work terms.

### 1.4 Historical & Engineering Relevance

The principle was first articulated rigorously by **Johann Bernoulli (1717)** and later refined by **Lagrange (1788)** into the *Lagrange-D'Alembert Principle*. In modern KTU-aligned engineering practice, the TVW is the **mathematical backbone** of:

- The **Finite Element Method (FEM)** — the *weak form* of equilibrium is precisely the virtual work equation.
- **Structural analysis** of trusses, beams, frames, and plates.
- **Variational calculus** derivations of Euler-Bernoulli and Timoshenko beam theory.
- **Plasticity and limit analysis** via upper and lower bound theorems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of the Virtual Displacement Field

A *valid* virtual displacement field $\delta u_i(\mathbf{x})$ must satisfy:

1. **Compatibility:** $\delta \varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\delta u_{i,j} + \delta u_{j,i}\right)$ must be a *well-defined* strain field.
2. **Smoothness:** $\delta u_i$ must be $C^0$ continuous and $\delta \varepsilon_{ij}$ must be $C^{-1}$ across element boundaries (in FEM context).
3. **Essential Boundary Condition:** $\delta u_i = 0$ on the portion of the boundary $S_u$ where real displacements are prescribed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Failing to enforce $\delta u_i = 0$ on $S_u$ is the **single most frequent reason** for losing marks in KTU derivations. The test (variational) functions are *always* zero on the Dirichlet boundary.

### 2.2 Axiomatic Logic Flow of the Theorem

- **Given (Hypothesis):**
  - A body of volume $V$ bounded by surface $S = S_T \cup S_u$.
  - Body force per unit volume $\mathbf{f}$.
  - Traction $\mathbf{T}$ prescribed on $S_T$.
  - Displacement $\bar{\mathbf{u}}$ prescribed on $S_u$.
  - Stress field $\sigma_{ij}$ and strain field $\varepsilon_{ij}$ are related by a constitutive law.

- **Equilibrium Premise:**
  - Cauchy equilibrium: $\sigma_{ij,j} + f_i = 0$ in $V$.
  - Natural boundary: $\sigma_{ij} n_j = T_i$ on $S_T$.

- **Test Field:** A kinematically admissible $\delta u_i$.

- **Conclusion:** $\delta W_{ext} = \delta W_{int}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Statement | Symbol Glossary | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\delta W_{ext} = \int_V f_i \,\delta u_i \, dV + \int_{S_T} T_i \,\delta u_i \, dS$ | $f_i$ = body force; $T_i$ = surface traction | External virtual work |
| 2 | $\delta W_{int} = \int_V \sigma_{ij} \,\delta \varepsilon_{ij} \, dV$ | $\sigma_{ij}$ = Cauchy stress; $\delta \varepsilon_{ij}$ = virtual strain | Internal virtual work |
| 3 | $\delta W_{ext} = \delta W_{int}$ | — | Master statement of TVW |
| 4 | $\delta \varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\delta u_{i,j} + \delta u_{j,i})$ | Kinematic compatibility | Strain-displacement variation |
| 5 | $\sum \mathbf{F}_i \cdot \delta \mathbf{r}_i = 0$ | Rigid body / particle systems | Discrete virtual work |
| 6 | $\int_V \sigma_{ij,j}\,\delta u_i\, dV = \int_{S}\sigma_{ij}n_j\,\delta u_i\, dS - \int_V \sigma_{ij}\,\delta u_{i,j}\, dV$ | Divergence theorem | Derivation step |
| 7 | $\delta u_i \vert_{S_u} = 0$ | Essential BC on $\delta u$ | Admissibility criterion |
| 8 | $\delta W_{int} = \delta U$ (linear elastic) | $U$ = strain energy | Virtual strain energy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Scaling:** All work terms carry units of **Joules (N·m)**. The variational operator $\delta$ is **dimensionally neutral** — it does not change units because it represents a differential probe, not a physical quantity.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why KTU Examiners Care

| Application Domain | Role of TVW |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Finite Element Method** | The Galerkin/weak form used to assemble element stiffness matrices is *literally* the TVW statement discretized. |
| **Truss & Frame Analysis** | Unit-load (virtual work) method for computing deflections: $\Delta = \int \frac{M m}{EI} dx + \int \frac{N n}{EA} dx + \int \frac{k V v}{GA} dx$. |
| **Limit Analysis of Frames** | Upper-bound (kinematic) and lower-bound (static) theorems are direct corollaries of TVW applied to plastic collapse mechanisms. |
| **Plate & Shell Theory** | Derivation of Kirchhoff-Love and Reissner-Mindlin plate equations from 3D elasticity uses TVW with assumed displacement fields. |
| **Vibration & Stability** | Rayleigh-Ritz quotient (used in buckling loads and natural frequencies) is derived from the second variation of virtual work. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Principle of Virtual Work for a 3D Deformable Body

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This derivation is the *most-asked* analytical problem in KTU Module-2 examinations. Memorize each line; examiners award **partial marks for intermediate steps**.

**Step 0 — Setup the Variational Probe**

Let $\delta u_i(\mathbf{x})$ be a *virtual displacement field* that is:
- Infinitesimally small,
- $C^1$ continuous inside $V$,
- Zero on the essential boundary $S_u$.

**Step 1 — Write the External Virtual Work**

Multiply the Cauchy equilibrium equation by the virtual displacement and integrate over the volume:

$$
\delta W_{ext} \;=\; \int_{V} f_i\,\delta u_i \, dV \;+\; \int_{S_T} T_i\,\delta u_i \, dS
$$

> *Interpretation:* This is the work done by the *real* body forces and surface tractions through the *imagined* displacement.

**Step 2 — Start from the Strong Form of Equilibrium**

$$
\sigma_{ij,j} + f_i \;=\; 0 \quad \text{in } V
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the Virtual Displacement and Integrate**

$$
\int_{V} \bigl(\sigma_{ij,j} + f_i\bigr)\,\delta u_i \, dV \;=\; 0
$$

Splitting the integral:

$$
\int_{V} \sigma_{ij,j}\,\delta u_i \, dV \;+\; \int_{V} f_i\,\delta u_i \, dV \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Divergence (Green-Gauss) Theorem to the First Integral**

Using $\sigma_{ij,j}\,\delta u_i = (\sigma_{ij}\,\delta u_i)_{,j} - \sigma_{ij}\,\delta u_{i,j}$:

$$
\int_{V} \sigma_{ij,j}\,\delta u_i \, dV \;=\; \int_{S} \sigma_{ij}\,n_j\,\delta u_i \, dS \;-\; \int_{V} \sigma_{ij}\,\delta u_{i,j} \, dV
$$

**Step 5 — Decompose the Surface Integral**

Since $S = S_T \cup S_u$ and $\delta u_i = 0$ on $S_u$:

$$
\int_{S} \sigma_{ij}\,n_j\,\delta u_i \, dS \;=\; \int_{S_T} \sigma_{ij}\,n_j\,\delta u_i \, dS \;+\; \underbrace{\int_{S_u} \sigma_{ij}\,n_j\,\delta u_i \, dS}_{=\,0 \text{ because } \delta u_i = 0 \text{ on } S_u}
$$

**Step 6 — Invoke the Natural Boundary Condition**

On $S_T$, the prescribed traction is $T_i = \sigma_{ij}\,n_j$. Substituting:

$$
\int_{S_T} \sigma_{ij}\,n_j\,\delta u_i \, dS \;=\; \int_{S_T} T_i\,\delta u_i \, dS
$$

**Step 7 — Recognize the Virtual Strain**

By kinematic compatibility, the symmetric part of the displacement gradient gives:

$$
\delta u_{i,j} \;\longrightarrow\; \delta \varepsilon_{ij} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(\delta u_{i,j} + \delta u_{j,i}\bigr)
$$

In linear theory with symmetric $\sigma_{ij}$:

$$
\sigma_{ij}\,\delta u_{i,j} \;=\; \sigma_{ij}\,\delta \varepsilon_{ij}
$$

**Step 8 — Assemble the Master Equation**

Collecting all surviving terms from Step 3 after substituting Steps 4–7:

$$
\int_{S_T} T_i\,\delta u_i \, dS \;-\; \int_{V} \sigma_{ij}\,\delta \varepsilon_{ij} \, dV \;+\; \int_{V} f_i\,\delta u_i \, dV \;=\; 0
$$

Rearranging (moving the internal work to the right-hand side):

$$
\boxed{\;\int_{V} f_i\,\delta u_i \, dV \;+\; \int_{S_T} T_i\,\delta u_i \, dS \;=\; \int_{V} \sigma_{ij}\,\delta \varepsilon_{ij} \, dV\;}
$$

> This is the **Principle of Virtual Work (External = Internal)** for a 3D deformable continuum in static equilibrium. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Specialization to 1D Bar / Truss Element (Common KTU Sub-Question)

Consider a uniform axial bar of length $L$, cross-section $A$, modulus $E$, under axial end loads $P$. Let a virtual displacement field be $\delta u(x)$.

**Step 1:** Real axial strain: $\varepsilon = du/dx$. Virtual strain: $\delta \varepsilon = d(\delta u)/dx$.

**Step 2:** Internal virtual work (using $\sigma = E\varepsilon$):

$$
\delta W_{int} \;=\; \int_{0}^{L} \sigma\,\delta \varepsilon \, A \, dx \;=\; \int_{0}^{L} EA\,\frac{du}{dx}\,\frac{d(\delta u)}{dx} \, dx
$$

**Step 3:** External virtual work (body force $f$ per unit length, no distributed traction for a bar):

$$
\delta W_{ext} \;=\; \int_{0}^{L} f\,\delta u \, A \, dx \;+\; P_L\,\delta u(L) \;-\; P_0\,\delta u(0)
$$

**Step 4:** Equating $\delta W_{ext} = \delta W_{int}$ yields the weak form whose Euler-Lagrange recovery is $d/dx(EA\,du/dx) + fA = 0$, the classical 1D bar equilibrium equation. This is the foundation of the **FEM stiffness matrix derivation**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping of Variational Identities

| Variational Identity | Mathematical Form | Underlying Theorem | KTU Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Virtual strain definition | $\delta \varepsilon_{ij} = \mathrm{sym}(\delta u_{i,j})$ | Kinematic compatibility | ★★★★★ |
| External virtual work | $\delta W_{ext} = \int_V f_i\,\delta u_i\,dV + \int_{S_T} T_i\,\delta u_i\,dS$ | Definition | ★★★★★ |
| Internal virtual work | $\delta W_{int} = \int_V \sigma_{ij}\,\delta \varepsilon_{ij}\,dV$ | Stress-power conjugate | ★★★★★ |
| Master TVW | $\delta W_{ext} - \delta W_{int} = 0$ | Equilibrium + compatibility | ★★★★★ |
| Rayleigh-Ritz quotient | $\Pi = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_V \sigma_{ij}\varepsilon_{ij}\,dV - \delta W_{ext}$ | Variational principle | ★★★★ |
| Unit-load method | $\Delta = \int \frac{Mm}{EI}\,dx$ | TVW applied to beams | ★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Virtual Work Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Physical System] --> B[Identify Real Forces: body force f and traction T]
    B --> C[Construct Kinematically Admissible Virtual Displacement delta u]
    C --> D{Is delta u zero on Su?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Compute External Virtual Work delta W_ext]
    D -- No --> F[Reject - Inadmissible Field]
    E --> G[Derive Virtual Strain delta epsilon from delta u]
    G --> H[Compute Internal Virtual Work delta W_int]
    H --> I[Equate: delta W_ext = delta W_int]
    I --> J[Extract Governing Equations / Solve Unknowns]
    F --> C

    classDef proc fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef gate fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef ok fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef rej fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    class A,B,C,E,G,H,I,J proc;
    class D gate;
    class F rej;
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Concept Map — Virtual Work Conceptual Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Real World] --> S[Real Forces and Stresses]
    V[Virtual World] --> W[Virtual Displacement delta u]
    W --> X[Virtual Strain delta epsilon]
    S --> Y[Equilibrium Equations]
    X --> Z[Internal Virtual Work]
    Y --> Z
    S --> AA[External Virtual Work]
    W --> AA
    Z --> AB[TVW Equation: Ext = Int]
    AA --> AB
    AB --> AC[FEM Weak Form]
    AB --> AD[Unit Load Method]
    AB --> AE[Limit Analysis Theorems]
    AB --> AF[Rayleigh Ritz Approximation]

    classDef real fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000;
    classDef virt fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000;
    classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000;
    class R,S,Y,AA real;
    class V,W,X,Z virt;
    class AB,AC,AD,AE,AF out;
```

### 4.3 Schematic — TVW Verification Flow for a Cantilever Beam (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Beam fixed at x=0] --> B[Length L, EI flexural rigidity]
    B --> C[Real Load P at free end]
    C --> D[Real Bending Moment Mx = P times L minus x]
    D --> E[Apply Virtual Unit Load at point of interest]
    E --> F[Virtual Moment mx = 1 times L minus x for the probe]
    F --> G[Integrate Mx mx over EI ds over 0 to L]
    G --> H[Deflection delta = Integral result]

    classDef beam fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000;
    classDef calc fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000;
    class A,B beam;
    class C,D,E,F,G,H calc;
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Block A → Block H traces the *operational pipeline* of the unit-load method, which is the textbook KTU application of the Theorem of Virtual Work for computing structural deflections.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1.a]**

**Q1.** Define *virtual displacement* and *virtual work*. State the principle of virtual work for a deformable body in equilibrium.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Virtual Displacement (1 Mark):** A virtual displacement $\delta u_i$ is an *imaginary*, *infinitesimally small* change in the configuration of a system that satisfies the geometric (kinematic) constraints of the body and is zero on boundaries where real displacements are prescribed. It is not a real motion and does not involve time.
- **Virtual Work (1 Mark):** Virtual work is the work performed by *real* (actual) forces acting through a *virtual* displacement. Mathematically, $\delta W = \mathbf{F} \cdot \delta \mathbf{u}$.
- **Principle Statement (1 Mark):** For a deformable body in static equilibrium, the external virtual work equals the internal virtual work:
$$
\int_{V} f_i\,\delta u_i\,dV + \int_{S_T} T_i\,\delta u_i\,dS = \int_{V} \sigma_{ij}\,\delta \varepsilon_{ij}\,dV
$$

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Q1.b]**

**Q2.** State and explain the *kinematic admissibility* conditions that a virtual displacement field must satisfy. Why is it essential that $\delta u_i = 0$ on the prescribed-displacement boundary $S_u$?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Condition 1 — Compatibility (1 Mark):** The virtual displacement must be sufficiently smooth ($C^0$ continuity of $\delta u_i$, $C^{-1}$ continuity of $\delta \varepsilon_{ij}$) so that a well-defined virtual strain field $\delta \varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\delta u_{i,j} + \delta u_{j,i})$ can be derived.
- **Condition 2 — Constraint compliance (1 Mark):** $\delta u_i$ must respect all internal and external geometric constraints (rigid links, supports, interfaces).
- **Condition 3 — Essential BC (1 Mark):** On $S_u$, where the real displacement is prescribed as $\bar{u}_i$, the virtual displacement must vanish ($\delta u_i = 0$). This is because any real displacement $\bar{u}_i$ is *fixed* by the support, so no *virtual* perturbation of it is allowed; otherwise the test function would generate unphysical work contributions from the unknown reaction forces.

---

### PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **INSTRUCTIONS (KTU Style):** Answer **any ONE** full question from Module 2. Each question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 marks** each.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, Modified from Dec 2022 Paper]*

**Q.A.** (a) Derive the Principle of Virtual Work for a three-dimensional deformable continuum in static equilibrium. Clearly state all assumptions and the role of the divergence theorem in the derivation.    **(7 Marks)**

**Q.A.** (b) For a uniform prismatic bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, Young's modulus $E$, fixed at $x=0$ and free at $x=L$, subjected to an axial tip load $P$, apply the Principle of Virtual Work to obtain the governing differential equation. Also determine the tip displacement using a kinematically admissible virtual displacement field of the form $\delta u(x) = a\,x$ where $a$ is an arbitrary constant.    **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Q.A (a) — Derivation of TVW for 3D Continuum (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — State the assumptions:**
- Static equilibrium (no inertia).
- Body $V$ bounded by $S = S_T \cup S_u$.
- Body force $f_i$, traction $T_i$ on $S_T$, prescribed displacement $\bar{u}_i$ on $S_u$.
- Virtual displacement $\delta u_i$ is infinitesimal, smooth, and zero on $S_u$.

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Write Cauchy's equilibrium:**

$$
\sigma_{ij,j} + f_i = 0 \quad \text{in } V
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Multiply by $\delta u_i$ and integrate:**

$$
\int_V (\sigma_{ij,j} + f_i)\,\delta u_i\,dV = 0
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Apply divergence theorem:**

$$
\int_V \sigma_{ij,j}\,\delta u_i\,dV = \int_S \sigma_{ij}\,n_j\,\delta u_i\,dS - \int_V \sigma_{ij}\,\delta u_{i,j}\,dV
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Apply natural BC ($\sigma_{ij} n_j = T_i$ on $S_T$) and essential BC ($\delta u_i = 0$ on $S_u$):**

$$
\int_S \sigma_{ij}\,n_j\,\delta u_i\,dS = \int_{S_T} T_i\,\delta u_i\,dS
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark) — Identify the virtual strain and assemble:**

$$
\int_V \sigma_{ij}\,\delta u_{i,j}\,dV = \int_V \sigma_{ij}\,\delta \varepsilon_{ij}\,dV
$$

Rearranging gives the master equation:

$$
\boxed{\;\int_V f_i\,\delta u_i\,dV + \int_{S_T} T_i\,\delta u_i\,dS = \int_V \sigma_{ij}\,\delta \varepsilon_{ij}\,dV\;}
$$

This is the **Principle of Virtual Work**. $\blacksquare$

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Q.A (b) — Application to 1D Bar (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Set up external and internal virtual work:**

- External: $\delta W_{ext} = P\,\delta u(L)$ (only the tip load contributes since body force is absent).
- Internal: $\delta W_{int} = \int_0^L \sigma(x)\,\delta \varepsilon(x)\,A\,dx$, where $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon = E\,du/dx$ and $\delta \varepsilon = d(\delta u)/dx$.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Choose a kinematically admissible virtual field:**

The fixed-end condition requires $\delta u(0) = 0$. The proposed field $\delta u(x) = a\,x$ satisfies this since $\delta u(0) = 0$. Here $a$ is an arbitrary infinitesimal constant.

Compute derivatives: $du/dx = C$ (constant for equilibrium of a uniform bar with no body force), $d(\delta u)/dx = a$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Equate $\delta W_{ext} = \delta W_{int}$:**

$$
P \cdot a L = \int_0^L (E \cdot C)(a)\,A\,dx = ECAaL
$$

Cancel $aL$ (both are arbitrary, non-zero):

$$
P = ECA \quad\Rightarrow\quad C = \frac{P}{EA}
$$

This is the **axial stress** result: $\sigma = E\,du/dx = P/A$.

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Determine the tip displacement:**

Integrate $du/dx = P/(EA)$ from $0$ to $L$:

$$
u(L) = \int_0^L \frac{P}{EA}\,dx = \frac{PL}{EA}
$$

Hence the **tip displacement is** $\delta_{tip} = \dfrac{PL}{EA}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The constant $C$ is the *real* axial strain. The arbitrary constant $a$ is the *virtual* parameter. Their independence is precisely why equating work terms recovers the equilibrium solution.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, Modified from May 2023 Paper]*

**Q.B.** (a) For a simply supported beam of span $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, subjected to a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length, use the Principle of Virtual Work with the assumed virtual displacement $\delta v(x) = a\,x(L-x)$ to obtain an approximate expression for the mid-span deflection. Comment on the accuracy of the result.    **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B.** (b) Explain how the Principle of Virtual Work forms the theoretical foundation of the **Finite Element Method**. With the help of a block diagram, describe the key steps involved in the FEM assembly procedure.    **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Q.B (a) — Beam Deflection by Rayleigh-Ritz (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — State the principle of minimum potential energy (corollary of TVW):**

For a linear elastic body, the equilibrium configuration minimizes the total potential energy. Using an assumed trial function and applying the TVW (or Rayleigh-Ritz) condition yields an approximate solution.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Compute internal virtual work (strain energy variation):**

For an Euler-Bernoulli beam, the bending strain energy is:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^L EI \left(\frac{d^2v}{dx^2}\right)^2 dx
$$

With trial function $v(x) = c\,x(L-x)$:

$$
\frac{d^2v}{dx^2} = -2c
$$

Hence:

$$
U = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^L EI\,(4c^2)\,dx = 2\,EI\,c^2\,L
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Compute external virtual work (load potential):**

Work done by the distributed load $w$ through $v(x)$:

$$
W = \int_0^L w\,v(x)\,dx = \int_0^L w\,c\,x(L-x)\,dx = w\,c\,\frac{L^3}{6}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Apply TVW: $\partial (U - W) / \partial c = 0$:**

$$
\frac{d}{dc}\left(2\,EI\,c^2\,L - \frac{wcL^3}{6}\right) = 4\,EI\,c\,L - \frac{wL^3}{6} = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
c = \frac{wL^2}{24\,EI}
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Mid-span deflection ($x = L/2$):**

$$
v\left(\tfrac{L}{2}\right) = c\cdot\tfrac{L}{2}\cdot\tfrac{L}{2} = \frac{wL^2}{24\,EI}\cdot\frac{L^2}{4} = \frac{wL^4}{96\,EI}
$$

**Comment (1 Mark, included in Step 5):** The exact mid-span deflection for a simply supported beam under UDL is $\dfrac{5wL^4}{384\,EI}$. The Rayleigh-Ritz estimate using a *single* quadratic trial function overestimates the deflection by about **22%** because the trial function is *stiffer* than the true deflected shape (lower-order polynomial cannot capture the inflection correctly). Increasing the number of trial-function terms (e.g., a cubic + quartic combination) improves the estimate dramatically. $\blacksquare$

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Q.B (b) — TVW as Foundation of FEM (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — The weak form is the TVW:**

The displacement-based FEM begins by writing the Principle of Virtual Work for the discretized domain. For an element $e$:

$$
\int_{V_e} \sigma : \delta\varepsilon\,dV = \int_{V_e} \mathbf{f}\cdot\delta\mathbf{u}\,dV + \int_{S_{T,e}} \mathbf{T}\cdot\delta\mathbf{u}\,dS
$$

This is the **weak form** of the equilibrium equations.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Discretization via shape functions:**

Approximate $\mathbf{u} \approx \mathbf{N}\,\mathbf{d}$ and $\delta\mathbf{u} \approx \mathbf{N}\,\delta\mathbf{d}$ where $\mathbf{N}$ contains shape functions and $\mathbf{d}$ are nodal displacements. Substituting into the weak form yields:

$$
\delta\mathbf{d}^T \left[\int_{V_e} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{D} \mathbf{B}\,dV\right]\mathbf{d} = \delta\mathbf{d}^T \left[\int_{V_e} \mathbf{N}^T \mathbf{f}\,dV + \int_{S_T} \mathbf{N}^T \mathbf{T}\,dS\right]
$$

Since $\delta\mathbf{d}^T$ is arbitrary, the bracket must vanish, giving the **element stiffness matrix** $\mathbf{k}_e$ and **element force vector** $\mathbf{f}_e$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Assembly and solution:**

The global equation $\mathbf{K}\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{F}$ is formed by assembling $\mathbf{k}_e$ over all elements. Essential boundary conditions are imposed by zeroing rows/columns corresponding to prescribed $\mathbf{D}$. The system is solved for unknown nodal displacements, from which strains and stresses are recovered.

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Key insight:**

The TVW is the *single mathematical statement* that, when discretized, becomes the FEM. Without TVW, there is no FEM. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the essential BC** $\delta u_i = 0$ on $S_u$ during derivation — costs **2 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. **Dropping the symmetry of $\sigma_{ij}$** when converting $\sigma_{ij}\delta u_{i,j}$ to $\sigma_{ij}\delta\varepsilon_{ij}$ — costs **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up** $\delta$ (variational) with $d$ (real differential) — examiners immediately deduct **1 mark** for this conceptual confusion.
> 4. **Omitting the natural boundary condition** $\sigma_{ij}n_j = T_i$ on $S_T$ in the derivation — costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Treating TVW as a real-motion statement** — TVW never predicts motion; it certifies *equilibrium*. Confusing this conceptual distinction costs **2–3 marks** in long-answer questions.
> 6. **Wrong units** in numerical parts: $E$ in Pa, $A$ in m², $L$ in m, $I$ in m⁴ — make sure unit consistency is maintained or **1 mark** is deducted.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Cheat-Sheet — 60-Second Read**

- ✅ **Virtual displacement** = imaginary, infinitesimal, kinematically admissible, **zero on $S_u$**.
- ✅ **Virtual work** = real force × virtual displacement; never a real motion.
- ✅ **Master statement:** $\delta W_{ext} = \delta W_{int}$, i.e., $\int_V f_i\delta u_i dV + \int_{S_T} T_i \delta u_i dS = \int_V \sigma_{ij}\delta\varepsilon_{ij} dV$.
- ✅ **Variational strain:** $\delta\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\delta u_{i,j} + \delta u_{j,i})$.
- ✅ **Derivation hinge:** Use the **divergence theorem** to convert $\int_V \sigma_{ij,j}\delta u_i dV$ into a surface integral minus a strain-energy term.
- ✅ **Boundary BC roles:** Essential BC ($\delta u_i = 0$ on $S_u$) eliminates unknown reaction work; Natural BC ($\sigma_{ij} n_j = T_i$ on $S_T$) substitutes prescribed traction.
- ✅ **Symmetry of $\sigma_{ij}$** is essential to replace $\delta u_{i,j}$ by $\delta\varepsilon_{ij}$.
- ✅ **Unit-load (virtual work) method** for beam deflection: $\Delta = \int (Mm/EI)dx + \int (Nn/EA)dx + \int (kVv/GA)dx$.
- ✅ **FEM is the discretized TVW** — every stiffness matrix is the second variation of the internal virtual work.
- ✅ **Theorem applies to *static* equilibrium** — for dynamics, use D'Alembert's principle with inertia forces.
- ✅ **Linear elastic case:** $\delta W_{int} = \delta U$ (variation of strain energy).
- ✅ **Higher-order applications:** Plate theory, shell theory, plastic collapse, and buckling are all derived from TVW with appropriate variational fields.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asymmetrical Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Asymmetrical Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Asymmetrical (or Unsymmetrical) Bending** is defined as the bending of a straight beam about an axis that does **not coincide with either of the principal centroidal axes** of the beam's cross-section. In such a case, the plane of loading (the plane containing the applied bending moments) is not aligned with a principal plane of the section. Consequently, the deflection does not occur in the plane of the applied load, and the resulting neutral axis is *inclined* to the loading plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The condition of unsymmetrical bending arises whenever the bending moment vector is not parallel to a principal centroidal axis. The combined effect of the resolved moment components (about the two principal axes $u$ and $v$) produces a resultant normal stress distribution governed by the general flexure formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction from Symmetric Bending:**
> - In *symmetric* bending, the neutral axis passes through the centroid and is perpendicular to the plane of loading.
> - In *unsymmetrical* bending, the neutral axis is generally **inclined** to the loading plane, and its orientation depends on the geometry of the cross-section and the direction of the load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine a wooden rectangular ruler (a beam) held horizontally. Its "strong" axis is the vertical one (taller dimension resisting bending). If you press down on it normally with your thumb, it bends predictably — this is **symmetric bending**.

Now, take the same ruler and **rotate it by 30°** so that it lies diagonally. Push it straight down. The ruler will still bend, but — surprisingly — it will **twist sideways** and bend out of the vertical plane. It will *not* deflect straight down. The load is vertical, but the deflection is at an angle. This is **asymmetrical bending**.

The "diagonal" orientation means the load is no longer along the principal axis of the section. The bending moment has components along *both* principal axes simultaneously, and the neutral axis (the line of zero stress) tilts away from being perpendicular to the load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Examples of Asymmetrical Bending:**
> - Roof purlins supporting asymmetric gravity loads that are not aligned with the web.
> - Purlins on **sloping roof trusses** (a classic KTU problem) where the load is vertical (gravity) but the principal axes of the Z/C-section purlin are inclined.
> - Wind girders and gable columns receiving eccentric loading.
> - Crane girders subjected to oblique trolley movements.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Constants

- **Modulus of Elasticity ($E$)**: Material property, typically in **GPa (GigaPascals)**.
- **Section Modulus ($Z$)**: Measured in **mm³** or **m³**.
- **Moment of Inertia ($I$)**: Measured in **mm⁴** or **m⁴**.
- **Radius of Gyration ($r$)**: Measured in **mm** or **m**.
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)**: Dimensionless, typically **0.3** for steel.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU problems on unsymmetrical bending, the load is often resolved along the principal axes using trigonometric resolution. The keyword in question papers is usually "**principal axes**" — you must first compute them before any stress analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Coordinate System and Conventions

Let the **principal centroidal axes** of the cross-section be denoted as $u$ and $v$ (these are mutually perpendicular axes about which the product of inertia $I_{uv} = 0$ and the moments of inertia $I_u$ and $I_v$ are maximum/minimum).

Let an arbitrary set of centroidal axes (rotated by angle $\theta$ from the principal axes) be $x$ and $y$. The applied bending moment $\mathbf{M}$ at a section is resolved into components:
- $M_x$: component about the $x$-axis
- $M_y$: component about the $y$-axis

### 2.1.2 The General Unsymmetrical Bending Equation

The normal stress at any point $(x, y)$ in the cross-section is given by the **General Flexure Formula**:

$$\sigma = \frac{M_x \, y}{I_x} - \frac{M_y \, x}{I_y}$$

where $I_x$ and $I_y$ are the moments of inertia about the chosen $x$ and $y$ centroidal axes, and the sign convention follows the standard KTU textbook approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign in the general formula is a **sign-convention artifact** based on the orientation of moment vectors. Always check your sign convention against the diagram in the problem statement.

### 2.1.3 Stress Equation Resolved Along Principal Axes

If the moments are resolved along the principal axes $u$ and $v$ as $M_u$ and $M_v$:

$$\sigma = \frac{M_u \, v}{I_u} - \frac{M_v \, u}{I_v}$$

### 2.1.4 Orientation of the Neutral Axis

The **Neutral Axis (NA)** is the locus of points in the cross-section where $\sigma = 0$. Setting $\sigma = 0$ in the general equation:

$$\frac{M_x \, y}{I_x} = \frac{M_y \, x}{I_y}$$

This gives the slope of the NA in the $x$–$y$ plane:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{y}{x} = \frac{M_y \, I_x}{M_x \, I_y}$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle the neutral axis makes with the $x$-axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The neutral axis is **NOT perpendicular to the load plane** in unsymmetrical bending. It is inclined at an angle $\alpha$ given above.

### 2.1.5 Slope of the Load Plane (Angle of the Plane of Bending)

The plane of loading (plane of applied moment vector) makes an angle $\theta_M$ with the $x$-axis:

$$\tan \theta_M = \frac{M_x}{M_y}$$

### 2.1.6 Relationship Between $\alpha$ and $\theta_M$

$$\tan \alpha \cdot \tan \theta_M = \frac{I_x}{I_y}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Special Check (Symmetric Bening):** If $I_x = I_y$ (e.g., square, circular, or any section with equal principal moments), then $\tan \alpha = 1/\tan \theta_M$, meaning $\alpha + \theta_M = 90°$. This recovers the familiar result that the NA is perpendicular to the load for symmetric sections — confirming our theory is consistent.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Bending Stress (about arbitrary axes $x,y$) | $\sigma = \dfrac{M_x \, y}{I_x} - \dfrac{M_y \, x}{I_y}$ | $\sigma$ in MPa or N/mm² |
| 2 | Stress in Principal Axes form | $\sigma = \dfrac{M_u \, v}{I_u} - \dfrac{M_v \, u}{I_v}$ | $I_{uv}=0$ for principal axes |
| 3 | Slope of Neutral Axis | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{M_y \, I_x}{M_x \, I_y}$ | $\alpha$ is angle of NA with $x$-axis |
| 4 | Slope of Plane of Loading | $\tan \theta_M = \dfrac{M_x}{M_y}$ | $\theta_M$ is angle of moment vector with $x$-axis |
| 5 | Fundamental relationship | $\tan \alpha \cdot \tan \theta_M = \dfrac{I_x}{I_y}$ | Core result for KTU problems |
| 6 | Principal Axes Condition | $I_{xy} = 0$ | $I_u$ max, $I_v$ min (or vice versa) |
| 7 | Principal Axis Orientation | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{-2 I_{xy}}{I_x - I_y}$ | $\theta_p$ is angle to principal axis |
| 8 | Principal Moment Magnitudes | $I_{\max,\min} = \dfrac{I_x + I_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{I_x - I_y}{2}\right)^2 + I_{xy}^2}$ | Quadratic form solution |
| 9 | Moment Resolution (from vertical load $W$ at angle $\theta$) | $M_u = W \cos \theta \cdot L$, $M_v = W \sin \theta \cdot L$ | Common purlin-type problem |
| 10 | Maximum Bending Stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ where $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | Used after neutral axis is found |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The KTU 2024 valuation scheme **demands** that students explicitly identify whether the axes chosen are principal or arbitrary. Failure to mention $I_{xy}=0$ for principal axes may attract mark deductions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The theory of unsymmetrical bending is **the cornerstone of design of roof purlins**, particularly in industrial steel buildings. **Cold-formed Z-purlins and C-purlins** have principal axes that are inclined to the web. The roof load is vertical (gravity), but the section's principal axes are tilted. Designers must compute combined bending about both principal axes to determine the maximum stress and select the correct section size. This avoids the catastrophic failure of purlins under wind uplift or asymmetric snow loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The General Unsymmetrical Bending Stress Equation

### Setup
Consider a straight beam of arbitrary cross-section. Let $x$ and $y$ be two perpendicular centroidal axes (not necessarily principal). An applied bending moment $\mathbf{M}$ at a section is resolved as:
- $M_x$: moment about the $x$-axis
- $M_y$: moment about the $y$-axis

We seek the normal stress $\sigma$ at any point with coordinates $(x, y)$ in the cross-section.

### Assumptions
1. Plane sections remain plane after bending (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).
2. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
3. The cross-section is prismatic along the beam length.
4. Stresses vary linearly with distance from the neutral axis.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1: Strain Compatibility (Kinematic Assumption)**

Since plane sections remain plane, the longitudinal strain $\epsilon_z$ at any point is linearly distributed across the section. Let the deformed cross-section be described by the equation:

$$z = a x + b y + c$$

where $a$, $b$, $c$ are constants defining the orientation of the deformed plane.

**Step 2: Stress–Strain Relation (Hooke's Law)**

For uniaxial bending (no $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$ assumed), the stress is:

$$\sigma_z = E \cdot \epsilon_z = E (a x + b y + c)$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium — Net Force in $z$-direction is zero**

$$\int_A \sigma_z \, dA = 0$$

Substituting and integrating:

$$E \int_A (a x + b y + c) \, dA = 0$$

$$E \left[ a \int_A x \, dA + b \int_A y \, dA + c \int_A dA \right] = 0$$

Since the origin is at the **centroid**: $\int_A x \, dA = 0$ and $\int_A y \, dA = 0$.

Therefore: $c \cdot A = 0 \Rightarrow c = 0$.

This means the neutral axis passes through the centroid.

**Step 4: Equilibrium — Moment about $x$-axis**

$$M_x = \int_A \sigma_z \cdot y \, dA = E a \int_A x y \, dA + E b \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Define: $I_{xy} = \int_A x y \, dA$ (product of inertia) and $I_x = \int_A y^2 \, dA$ (moment of inertia about $x$).

$$M_x = E \left[ a \, I_{xy} + b \, I_x \right]$$

**Step 5: Equilibrium — Moment about $y$-axis**

$$-M_y = \int_A \sigma_z \cdot x \, dA = E a \int_A x^2 \, dA + E b \int_A x y \, dA$$

Define: $I_y = \int_A x^2 \, dA$.

$$-M_y = E \left[ a \, I_y + b \, I_{xy} \right]$$

**Step 6: Solve for constants $a$ and $b$**

We have the linear system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a \, I_{xy} + b \, I_x &= \frac{M_x}{E} \\
a \, I_y + b \, I_{xy} &= -\frac{M_y}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using Cramer's rule, the determinant is:

$$D = I_{xy}^2 - I_x I_y = -I_x I_y \left(1 - \frac{I_{xy}^2}{I_x I_y}\right)$$

Solving for $a$ and $b$ (substituting back into the stress equation):

$$a = \frac{M_x I_{xy} + M_y I_x}{E (I_{xy}^2 - I_x I_y)}, \quad b = \frac{-M_x I_y - M_y I_{xy}}{E (I_{xy}^2 - I_x I_y)}$$

**Step 7: Final Stress Equation**

Substituting $a$ and $b$ into $\sigma_z = E(ax + by)$:

$$\sigma_z = \frac{(M_x I_{xy} + M_y I_x) \, x + (-M_x I_y - M_y I_{xy}) \, y}{I_{xy}^2 - I_x I_y}$$

**Step 8: Simplification for Principal Axes (where $I_{xy} = 0$)**

When the reference axes are the principal centroidal axes (so $I_{xy} = 0$, renaming $I_x \to I_u$, $I_y \to I_v$):

$$\sigma_z = \frac{M_y \, x}{I_y} - \frac{M_x \, y}{I_x}$$

Renaming: let $u$ be the horizontal principal axis and $v$ the vertical. Then moments $M_u$ and $M_v$ act about them. The **General Unsymmetrical Bending Formula** is:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M_u \, v}{I_u} - \frac{M_v \, u}{I_v}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Clarification:** Some textbooks use $\sigma = \dfrac{M_y \cdot x}{I_y} + \dfrac{M_x \cdot y}{I_x}$ depending on whether the moment vectors are taken as positive about the positive axes. KTU follows the convention that **tensile stress is positive**, and moments are positive when they cause tension in the positive fiber direction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Slope of the Neutral Axis

**Step 1:** Neutral Axis is where $\sigma = 0$. Using the general formula:

$$\frac{M_y \, x}{I_y} - \frac{M_x \, y}{I_x} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to find the slope $\tan \alpha$:

$$\frac{y}{x} = \frac{M_y \, I_x}{M_x \, I_y}$$

**Step 3:** The angle the neutral axis makes with the $x$-axis is:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{M_y \, I_x}{M_x \, I_y}$$

**Step 4:** If the moment vector lies in a plane making angle $\theta_M$ with the $x$-axis:

$$M_x = M \cos \theta_M, \quad M_y = M \sin \theta_M$$

Then:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{M \sin \theta_M \cdot I_x}{M \cos \theta_M \cdot I_y} = \frac{I_x}{I_y} \tan \theta_M$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\tan \alpha \cdot \cot \theta_M = \frac{I_x}{I_y} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \tan \alpha \cdot \tan \theta_M = \frac{I_x}{I_y}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix Format)

**Problem:** A rectangular beam of width $b = 100$ mm and depth $d = 200$ mm is bent by a moment $M = 10$ kN·m acting in a vertical plane that makes an angle $\theta_M = 30°$ with the $y$-axis. Determine (a) the orientation of the neutral axis, (b) the maximum bending stress, and (c) the bending stress at a point $P$ located at $(x, y) = (40, 60)$ mm from the centroid.

**Step 1: Compute the moments of inertia (principal axes for a rectangle align with $x$ and $y$ since the section is symmetric).**

$$I_x = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 200^3}{12} = 66.667 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$I_y = \frac{d b^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 100^3}{12} = 16.667 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Resolve the moment into components.**

$$M_x = M \cos \theta_M = 10 \times 10^6 \times \cos 30° = 8.660 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$M_y = M \sin \theta_M = 10 \times 10^6 \times \sin 30° = 5.000 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Find the orientation of the neutral axis.**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{M_y I_x}{M_x I_y} = \frac{(5.000 \times 10^6)(66.667 \times 10^6)}{(8.660 \times 10^6)(16.667 \times 10^6)}$$

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{3.333 \times 10^{14}}{1.443 \times 10^{14}} = 2.309$$

$$\alpha = \arctan(2.309) = 66.59°$$

So the neutral axis makes an angle of **66.59°** with the $x$-axis.

**Step 4: Compute the bending stress at point $P(40, 60)$.**

$$\sigma_P = \frac{M_x \, y}{I_x} - \frac{M_y \, x}{I_y} = \frac{(8.660 \times 10^6)(60)}{66.667 \times 10^6} - \frac{(5.000 \times 10^6)(40)}{16.667 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_P = \frac{5.196 \times 10^{8}}{6.667 \times 10^7} - \frac{2.000 \times 10^{8}}{1.667 \times 10^7}$$

$$\sigma_P = 7.794 - 12.000 = -4.206 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_P = -4.206 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

**Step 5: Find the maximum bending stress.**

The maximum stress occurs at the point **farthest from the neutral axis** in the cross-section. Using the result of stress in a rotated coordinate system, the maximum distance from the NA in the rectangular section:
