# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** State Castigliano's First Theorem. How is it used to find the deflection at a point where no external load is applied?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Castigliano's First Theorem (1 mark):** The partial derivative of the total strain energy of a structure with respect to any particular load gives the deflection of the structure at the point of application of that load and in the direction of the load.
>
> Mathematically: $\delta_P = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial P}$
>
> **Procedure for unloaded points (2 marks):**
> 1. Apply a fictitious (dummy) load $Q$ at the point and in the direction of the desired deflection.
> 2. Express the total strain energy $U$ as a function of both the real load $P$ and the dummy load $Q$.
> 3. Differentiate $U$ partially with respect to $Q$, then substitute $Q = 0$.
> 4. The result gives the required deflection.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between a thin curved bar and a thick curved bar. What is the role of the eccentricity 'e' in thick curved bar analysis?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Distinction (2 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Thin Curved Bar | Thick Curved Bar |
> |---|---|---|
> | Criterion | $R/h > 10$ | $R/h \le 10$ |
> | Neutral axis | Coincides with centroidal axis | Shifts inward from centroidal axis |
> | Stress distribution | Linear | Hyperbolic |
> | Theory used | Simple bending | Winkler–Bach |
>
> **Role of eccentricity 'e' (1 mark):** The eccentricity $e = \bar{R} - R_n$ appears in the denominator of the bending stress and bending strain energy expressions. It accounts for the non-coincidence of the neutral and centroidal axes and is the key parameter that distinguishes thick-bar theory from the simple flexure formula.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule Reminder:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice means you may attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

A curved bar of rectangular cross-section $40 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm}$ (width × depth) is in the form of a **quarter circle** of mean radius $\bar{R} = 120 \text{ mm}$. A vertical load $W = 5 \text{ kN}$ is applied at the free end. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$ and $\nu = 0.3$.

**Compute:**
- **(a)** The vertical and horizontal deflections at the free end (7 marks)
- **(b)** The bending, direct, and shear strain energies stored in the bar (7 marks)

#### Solution (a) — Deflections (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Geometric Parameters (1 mark)**

$$R_i = 120 - 30 = 90 \text{ mm}, \quad R_o = 120 + 30 = 150 \text{ mm}, \quad A = 40 \times 60 = 2400 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$R_n = \frac{h}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} = \frac{60}{\ln(150/90)} = \frac{60}{\ln(1.6667)} = \frac{60}{0.5108} = 117.46 \text{ mm}$$

$$e = \bar{R} - R_n = 120 - 117.46 = 2.54 \text{ mm}$$

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{2\times 10^5}{2(1.3)} = 7.692 \times 10^4 \text{ MPa}$$

Shape factor for rectangle: $k = 6/5 = 1.2$

**Step 2: Internal Force Resultants (1 mark)**

At section angle $\theta$ from fixed end:
$$M = W\bar R\sin\theta, \quad N = W\sin\theta, \quad Q = W\cos\theta$$

**Step 3: Apply Castigliano (1 mark)**

For vertical deflection (load $W$ exists), differentiate strain energy terms w.r.t. $W$. Using the integrals derived in Section 3.2:

$$\delta_v = \frac{\pi W \bar R^3}{4 A E e} + \frac{\pi W \bar R}{4 A E} + \frac{\pi k W \bar R}{4 G A}$$

Substitute values:
- $\pi W \bar R^3 = \pi (5000)(120)^3 = \pi (5000)(1.728 \times 10^6) = 2.714 \times 10^{10}$
- $4 A E e = 4 (2400)(2\times 10^5)(2.54) = 4.876 \times 10^9$
- **Bending term:** $\delta_{v,b} = 2.714 \times 10^{10} / 4.876 \times 10^9 = 5.566$ mm

- $\pi W \bar R = \pi (5000)(120) = 1.885 \times 10^6$
- $4 A E = 4 (2400)(2\times 10^5) = 1.92 \times 10^9$
- **Direct term:** $\delta_{v,d} = 1.885 \times 10^6 / 1.92 \times 10^9 = 9.82 \times 10^{-4}$ mm

- $\pi k W \bar R = \pi (1.2)(5000)(120) = 2.262 \times 10^6$
- $4 G A = 4 (7.692 \times 10^4)(2400) = 7.384 \times 10^8$
- **Shear term:** $\delta_{v,s} = 2.262 \times 10^6 / 7.384 \times 10^8 = 3.06 \times 10^{-3}$ mm

**Total vertical deflection (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{\delta_v = 5.566 + 0.001 + 0.003 \approx 5.57 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$

**Step 4: Horizontal Deflection Using Dummy Load (2 marks)**

Apply dummy load $H$ horizontally. Use the formula derived in Section 3.2:

$$\delta_h = \frac{W \bar R^3}{2 A E e} - \frac{W \bar R}{2 A E} - \frac{k W \bar R}{2 G A}$$

- **Bending term:** $\dfrac{5000 \times (120)^3}{2(2400)(2\times 10^5)(2.54)} = \dfrac{8.64 \times 10^9}{2.438 \times 10^9} = 3.544$ mm
- **Direct term:** $\dfrac{5000 \times 120}{2(2400)(2\times 10^5)} = \dfrac{6\times 10^5}{9.6\times 10^8} = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$ mm
- **Shear term:** $\dfrac{1.2 \times 5000 \times 120}{2(7.692 \times 10^4)(2400)} = \dfrac{7.2\times 10^5}{3.692\times 10^8} = 1.95 \times 10^{-3}$ mm

$$\boxed{\delta_h \approx 3.544 - 0.0006 - 0.002 \approx 3.54 \text{ mm (toward centre of curvature)}}$$

**Step 5: Final answer (1 mark)**
$$\delta_v \approx 5.57 \text{ mm}, \quad \delta_h \approx 3.54 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### Solution (b) — Strain Energies (7 Marks)

**Bending strain energy (3 marks):**

$$U_b = \frac{\pi W^2 \bar R^3}{8 A E e} = \frac{\pi (5000)^2 (120)^3}{8(2400)(2\times 10^5)(2.54)}$$

$$U_b = \frac{\pi (2.5\times 10^7)(1.728 \times 10^6)}{9.753 \times 10^9} = \frac{1.357 \times 10^{14}}{9.753 \times 10^9} = 1.391 \times 10^4 \text{ N·mm} = 13.91 \text{ J}$$

**Direct strain energy (2 marks):**

$$U_d = \frac{\pi W^2 \bar R}{8 A E} = \frac{\pi (2.5\times 10^7)(120)}{8(2400)(2\times 10^5)} = \frac{9.42 \times 10^9}{3.84 \times 10^9} = 2.454 \text{ N·mm} = 2.45 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}$$

**Shear strain energy (2 marks):**

$$U_s = \frac{\pi k W^2 \bar R}{8 G A} = \frac{\pi (1.2)(2.5\times 10^7)(120)}{8(7.692 \times 10^4)(2400)} = \frac{1.131 \times 10^{10}}{1.477 \times 10^9} = 7.66 \text{ N·mm} = 7.66 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}$$

**Total strain energy (summarised at end of solution):**
$$U_{total} = U_b + U_d + U_s \approx 13.91 + 0.0025 + 0.0077 \approx 13.92 \text{ J}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency** — Convert $W$ from kN to N (multiply by $10^3$) before substituting. Mixing units is the #1 mark-loss factor.
> 2. **Sign of $e$** — Always positive for a convex bar with $R_o > \bar{R} > R_n$. Some students write $e = R_n - \bar{R}$ (negative), and the sign flip propagates through every subsequent calculation.
> 3. **Forgetting $\pi/4$** — The integral of $\sin^2\theta$ from $0$ to $\pi/2$ is $\pi/4$, **not $1/2$**. Examiners explicitly check this.
> 4. **Skipping the direct/shear terms** — Full marks require all three terms even if they are small.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A **semicircular curved bar** (mean radius $\bar{R} = 150$ mm, rectangular section $30 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ mm}$) has a radial load $P = 8 \text{ kN}$ applied at the **crown** (midpoint of the arc) directed **toward the centre of curvature**.

**Compute:**
- **(a)** The radial deflection at the crown and the tangential deflection at the crown (7 marks)
- **(b)** The maximum bending stress and its location in the cross-section (7 marks)

#### Solution Outline (Model Sketch for the Student)

**Geometry & Internal Forces (3 marks for setting up):**

For a semicircular bar with $\theta \in [0, \pi]$, take the cut section at angle $\theta$ from the right-hand support. The radial load $P$ at the crown has both horizontal and vertical components w.r.t. the section:

$$M(\theta) = \frac{P \bar R}{2}(1 - \cos\theta) \quad \text{(symmetry result)}$$

$$N(\theta) = -\frac{P}{2}\cos\theta, \quad Q(\theta) = \frac{P}{2}\sin\theta$$

**Castigliano application (4 marks):**
For radial deflection, treat $P$ as the load at the crown. Differentiate $U$ w.r.t. $P$. The bending integral becomes:

$$\int_0^\pi (1 - \cos\theta)^2 d\theta = \int_0^\pi (1 - 2\cos\theta + \cos^2\theta)d\theta = \pi - 0 + \frac{\pi}{2} = \frac{3\pi}{2}$$

$$\delta_r = \frac{P\bar R^3}{2AEe}\cdot\frac{3\pi}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{P}\cdot P = \frac{3\pi P \bar R^3}{4AEe} + \text{(direct + shear corrections)}$$

(The detailed numerical substitution is left to the student as practice — the **method** is what is tested for 7 marks.)

**Maximum Bending Stress (7 marks for part b):**
$$M_{max} = P\bar R \quad \text{(at } \theta = \pi/2 \text{, the supports)}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}(R_n - r)}{A e r}$$

The maximum tensile stress occurs at the **inner fibre** ($r = R_i$):

$$\sigma_{t,\max} = \frac{M_{max}(R_n - R_i)}{A e R_i}$$

The maximum compressive stress occurs at the **outer fibre** ($r = R_o$):

$$\sigma_{c,\max} = \frac{M_{max}(R_n - R_o)}{A e R_o}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget that for a **horizontally mounted** semicircular bar with a vertical load, the **maximum moment occurs at the supports**, not at the crown. This is because the moment arm is the horizontal distance from the support to the load line of action.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12 Golden Rules for KTU 2024 — Deflection of Thick Curved Bars**

1. **Always classify first** — compute $R/h$ and decide whether Winkler–Bach theory is needed. $R/h \le 10 \Rightarrow$ thick bar.
2. **Winkler–Bach neutral axis** for a rectangular section: $R_n = h / \ln(R_o/R_i)$.
3. **Eccentricity** $e = \bar{R} - R_n$ is **always positive** and **always small** (typically 1–3% of $\bar{R}$ for moderately thick bars).
4. **Total strain energy** = Bending + Direct + Shear contributions, all integrated over the length of the bar.
5. **Bending term** is $\dfrac{M^2}{2AEe}$ per unit length — note the $1/e$ (NOT $1/I$).
6. **Direct term** is $\dfrac{N^2}{2AE}$ per unit length — the same as for a straight tie-bar.
7. **Shear term** is $\dfrac{kQ^2}{2GA}$ per unit length, with $k = 6/5$ (rectangular) or $10/9$ (circular).
8. **Castigliano's theorem** gives deflection in the direction of a *real* load; use a **dummy load** when no load exists in the desired direction.
9. **Dummy-load protocol:** Write all internal forces as functions of *both* loads, integrate $U$, differentiate w.r.t. dummy, set dummy = 0 *after* differentiation.
10. **Sign convention** must be **consistent** between real-load and dummy-load moment diagrams. A single sign mismatch loses 1 mark.
11. **The bending strain energy dwarfs the others** — but KTU examiners **expect all three** to be calculated for full marks.
12. **Standard integrals to memorize:**
    - $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin^2\theta\, d\theta = \int_0^{\pi/2}\cos^2\theta\, d\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}$
    - $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi/2}\sin\theta\cos\theta\, d\theta = \frac{1}{2}$
    - $\displaystyle\int_0^{\pi}\cos^2\theta\, d\theta = \int_0^{\pi}\sin^2\theta\, d\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}$

> **Common Real-World Applications:** Crane hooks, chain links, C-clamps, lifting lugs, ship-bow arches, arch dams, spring ends, press frames.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Axisymmetric Problems: Thick Walled Cylinder Subjected to Internal and External Pressures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Axisymmetric Problems: Thick Walled Cylinder Subjected to Internal and External Pressures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Axisymmetric Problem:** A problem in solid mechanics in which the geometry, material properties, loading, and boundary conditions are symmetric about a central axis. Every cross-section taken through the axis looks identical, and the stress/strain fields depend only on the radial coordinate **r**, not on the angular coordinate **θ** or the axial coordinate **z**.

A **Thick-Walled Cylinder** is a hollow cylindrical pressure vessel in which the ratio of wall thickness **(b − a)** to the inner radius **a** is **not negligible** (typically, t/a > 0.1). Unlike thin-walled cylinders where hoop stress is assumed uniform across the thickness, the radial and circumferential (hoop) stresses in a thick-walled cylinder **vary significantly** with the radial distance **r**, and the analysis **cannot** be reduced to simple membrane theory.

A cylinder subjected to both **internal pressure (pᵢ)** and **external pressure (pₒ)** is a classic 2-D axisymmetric problem, solved using **Lamé's Equations** in polar (cylindrical) coordinates.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker & The Submarine Hull"

Imagine a **pressure cooker** at home. The cylindrical steel body of the cooker carries the steam pressure from inside. The inner surface feels a high outward push, while the outer surface feels the (relatively low) atmospheric pressure. The metal **near the bore is stretched the most** (high hoop stress), while the metal **near the outer surface is barely strained**. This radial variation of stress cannot be captured by a single "membrane" formula — it requires the **Lamé solution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Submarine Hull Analogy:** A submarine dives deep, where the sea squeezes it from outside (high **pₒ**) while the inside has normal air pressure (**pᵢ ≈ 0**). The outer fibres are compressed, the inner fibres are in tension — exactly the opposite of a pressure vessel. Lamé's equations handle both cases with the **same formula** simply by changing the sign and magnitude of the pressures.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit (SI) | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **E** | Young's Modulus | N/m² (Pa) | 1.9 × 10¹¹ – 2.1 × 10¹¹ for steel |
| **ν** | Poisson's Ratio | dimensionless | 0.25 – 0.33 for metals |
| **pᵢ** | Internal Pressure | N/m² (Pa) or MPa | 1 – 500 MPa typical |
| **pₒ** | External Pressure | N/m² (Pa) or MPa | 0 – 50 MPa typical |
| **a, b** | Inner and Outer Radius | m or mm | depending on vessel |

## 1.4 When Do We Use Thick-Walled Theory?

A cylinder is classified as **thick** if the wall thickness satisfies:
$$\frac{t}{a} \;=\; \frac{b-a}{a} \;>\; \frac{1}{10} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{D_i}{t} < 20$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examples: **Hydraulic cylinders, gun barrels, high-pressure boiler tubes, nuclear reactor pressure vessels, autofrettaged tubes, BOF tuyeres** — all require Lamé's thick-cylinder analysis, NOT thin-walled hoop stress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions for Axisymmetric Thick-Cylinder Analysis

1. **Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** — Hooke's Law applies.
2. **Plane sections remain plane** and the cylinder is long enough to neglect end effects (**plane strain** assumption in the radial direction → Generalised Plane Strain).
3. **Axisymmetric loading** — pᵢ and pₒ are uniform around the circumference and along the length.
4. **No body forces** (self-weight neglected).
5. **Open or closed ends** determine whether axial stress **σ_z** is zero (open) or non-zero (closed).

## 2.2 Why Does the Stress Vary Radially? — Physical Intuition

In a thin cylinder, we assume fibres at all radii carry the same hoop stress. In reality, consider an **infinitesimal element** at radius **r**:

- The inner face of the element is pulled inward by radial stress **σᵣ**.
- The outer face is pulled outward by radial stress **σᵣ + dσᵣ**.
- The two circumferential faces are pulled by hoop stress **σ_θ**.

For equilibrium of this small wedge (a free-body balance), σ_θ **must** be larger than σᵣ by an amount proportional to **r·(dσᵣ/dr)**, and this single equilibrium equation — combined with the displacement–strain equations — yields the **two Lamé constants A and B**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Lamé's Equations)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_r \;=\; A \;-\; \dfrac{B}{r^{2}}$ | Radial (normal) stress | Find stress at any r |
| 2 | $\sigma_{\theta} \;=\; A \;+\; \dfrac{B}{r^{2}}$ | Hoop / circumferential stress | Find stress at any r |
| 3 | $A \;=\; \dfrac{p_i\,a^{2} \;-\; p_o\,b^{2}}{b^{2} \;-\; a^{2}}$ | First Lamé constant | Boundary condition solution |
| 4 | $B \;=\; \dfrac{(p_i \;-\; p_o)\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}{b^{2} \;-\; a^{2}}$ | Second Lamé constant | Boundary condition solution |
| 5 | $\sigma_r(r\!=\!a) \;=\; -p_i$ | Inner BC: radial stress equals −internal pressure | Apply BC |
| 6 | $\sigma_r(r\!=\!b) \;=\; -p_o$ | Outer BC: radial stress equals −external pressure | Apply BC |
| 7 | $\sigma_{\theta}(r\!=\!a) \;=\; \dfrac{p_i(a^{2}+b^{2}) - 2p_o\,b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}$ | Hoop stress at inner radius | **Max hoop stress** |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\theta}(r\!=\!b) \;=\; \dfrac{2p_i\,a^{2} - p_o(a^{2}+b^{2})}{b^{2}-a^{2}}$ | Hoop stress at outer radius | Min hoop stress (compression) |
| 9 | $u(r) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{E}\!\left[ A(1-\nu)r \;+\; \dfrac{B(1+\nu)}{r} \right]$ | Radial displacement at radius r (Plane Stress) | Deformation |
| 10 | $u(r) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{E}\!\left[ A(1-\nu-2\nu^{2})r \;+\; \dfrac{B(1+\nu)}{r} \right]$ | Radial displacement (Plane Strain) | Deformation |
| 11 | $\Delta D \;=\; 2u_{outer} \;=\; \dfrac{2b}{E}\!\left[ A(1-\nu) \;+\; \dfrac{B(1+\nu)}{b^{2}} \right]$ | Change in outer diameter | Press-fit, shrink-fit calcs |
| 12 | $\sigma_z \;=\; 0$ (open) or $\sigma_z \;=\; \nu(\sigma_r+\sigma_{\theta})$ (closed) | Axial stress | End conditions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** **Tensile stress is POSITIVE**, **compressive stress is NEGATIVE**. Internal pressure pushes the wall outward, so the wall experiences **compressive radial stress** at the inner surface → **σᵣ = −pᵢ**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure vessel design** (ASME Section VIII Div 1/2/3).
- **Compound cylinders** (autofrettage, shrink-fit assemblies) — built up by shrinking one cylinder onto another to pre-load with beneficial compressive stresses.
- **Hydraulic & pneumatic actuator design** (excavator booms, landing gear).
- **Gun barrel design** — autofrettage creates a residual compressive hoop stress at the bore, increasing fatigue life.
- **Nuclear reactor coolant channels**, **rocket motor casings**, **submarine hulls**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Equilibrium Equation in Polar Coordinates

Consider an **infinitesimal element** of the cylinder at radius **r**, with radial thickness **dr** and angular width **dθ**.

The forces on the element are:
- On the inner face (area = $r\,d\theta\,dz$): $\sigma_r \cdot r\,d\theta\,dz$ (acting inward)
- On the outer face (area = $(r+dr)\,d\theta\,dz$): $(\sigma_r + d\sigma_r)\,(r+dr)\,d\theta\,dz$ (acting outward)
- On the two circumferential faces (each of area $dr\cdot dz$, half-thickness $r\cdot d\theta/2$ on each side): hoop stresses $\sigma_\theta$.

Summing forces in the **radial direction** and ignoring higher-order differentials:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} \;+\; \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} \;+\; R \;=\; 0$$

where **R** is the body force per unit volume (set to 0 for this problem).

> **Conversion Logic:** This single equation is the radial equilibrium of a cylindrical wedge. It is the **starting point** of every thick-cylinder derivation.

## 3.2 Compatibility (Strain-Displacement) Equations

In polar coordinates, for axisymmetric motion (no θ or z dependence):

$$\varepsilon_r \;=\; \frac{du}{dr}, \qquad \varepsilon_\theta \;=\; \frac{u}{r}, \qquad \varepsilon_z \;=\; \frac{dw}{dz}$$

Differentiating **ε_θ** with respect to r and using **ε_r** (the compatibility condition in polar coordinates) gives:

$$\frac{d\varepsilon_\theta}{dr} \;+\; \frac{\varepsilon_\theta - \varepsilon_r}{r} \;=\; 0$$

## 3.3 Constitutive (Hooke's) Law — Plane Stress Case

Assuming **plane stress** (σ_z = 0, valid for open-ended cylinders):

$$\varepsilon_r \;=\; \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_r - \nu\,\sigma_\theta\bigr)$$

$$\varepsilon_\theta \;=\; \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_\theta - \nu\,\sigma_r\bigr)$$

## 3.4 Deriving Lamé's Equations

**Step 1:** Write u(r) as an unknown function and compute strains:
$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \quad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into Hooke's law and solve for σᵣ and σ_θ in terms of u:
$$\sigma_r = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\bigl[(1-\nu)\,\varepsilon_r + \nu\,\varepsilon_\theta\bigr]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute u(r) into compatibility, yielding the **Euler-Cauchy** differential equation in u:
$$\frac{d^{2}u}{dr^{2}} \;+\; \frac{1}{r}\,\frac{du}{dr} \;-\; \frac{u}{r^{2}} \;=\; 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve this ODE. The characteristic equation gives solutions of the form $u \propto r$ and $u \propto 1/r$, so:
$$u(r) \;=\; C_1\,r \;+\; \frac{C_2}{r}$$

**Step 5:** Back-substitute u into Hooke's law:
$$\sigma_r(r) \;=\; \frac{E\,C_1}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\bigl[(1-\nu) - \nu\bigr] \;-\; \frac{E\,C_2}{(1+\nu)\,r^{2}}$$

After simplification, define new constants **A** and **B** (Lamé constants) such that:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_r \;=\; A \;-\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}\;} \qquad \text{and} \qquad \boxed{\;\sigma_\theta \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{r^{2}}\;}$$

This is the **famous Lamé result**. Note that **σᵣ + σ_θ = 2A** (constant), which is a powerful check during numerical work.

## 3.5 Applying the Boundary Conditions

The two boundary conditions come from equating the radial stress on the inner and outer surfaces to the applied pressures:

$$\sigma_r(a) \;=\; -p_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A \;-\; \frac{B}{a^{2}} \;=\; -p_i$$

$$\sigma_r(b) \;=\; -p_o \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A \;-\; \frac{B}{b^{2}} \;=\; -p_o$$

**Step A — Subtract the two equations:**
$$-B\!\left(\frac{1}{a^{2}} - \frac{1}{b^{2}}\right) \;=\; -(p_i - p_o)$$

$$B\!\left(\frac{b^{2} - a^{2}}{a^{2}\,b^{2}}\right) \;=\; (p_i - p_o)$$

$$\boxed{\;B \;=\; \frac{(p_i - p_o)\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\;}$$

**Step B — Add to find A.** From the second BC:
$$A \;=\; -p_o \;+\; \frac{B}{b^{2}} \;=\; -p_o \;+\; \frac{(p_i - p_o)\,a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$$

Combining over a common denominator:

$$\boxed{\;A \;=\; \frac{p_i\,a^{2} \;-\; p_o\,b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\;}$$

## 3.6 Hoop Stress at Inner and Outer Surfaces

**At r = a (inner surface):**
$$\sigma_\theta(a) \;=\; A + \frac{B}{a^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_\theta(a) \;=\; \frac{p_i\,a^{2} - p_o\,b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} \;+\; \frac{(p_i - p_o)\,b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_\theta(a) \;=\; \frac{p_i(a^{2} + b^{2}) \;-\; 2\,p_o\,b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The inner surface always carries the **maximum hoop stress** (tensile for internal pressurisation) and is the **critical location for design**.

**At r = b (outer surface):**
$$\boxed{\;\sigma_\theta(b) \;=\; \frac{2\,p_i\,a^{2} \;-\; p_o(a^{2} + b^{2})}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\;}$$

## 3.7 Numerical Example (Worked — KTU Typical Style)

> **Problem:** A thick steel cylinder has inner radius **a = 50 mm**, outer radius **b = 100 mm**. It is subjected to internal pressure **pᵢ = 80 MPa** and external pressure **pₒ = 20 MPa**. Determine the radial and hoop stresses at **r = 50 mm, 75 mm, 100 mm**.

**Step 1 — Compute A and B.**

a² = 2500 mm², b² = 10000 mm², b² − a² = 7500 mm².

$$A = \frac{80(2500) - 20(10000)}{7500} = \frac{200000 - 200000}{7500} = 0 \;\text{MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{(80-20)(2500)(10000)}{7500} = \frac{60 \times 25 \times 10^{6}}{7500} = 2.0 \times 10^{5} \;\text{MPa·mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute stresses at the three radii.**

$$\text{At } r=50\;\text{mm}: \quad \sigma_r = 0 - \frac{2\times10^{5}}{2500} = -80\;\text{MPa}; \quad \sigma_\theta = 0 + 80 = +80\;\text{MPa}$$

$$\text{At } r=75\;\text{mm}: \quad \sigma_r = 0 - \frac{2\times10^{5}}{5625} = -35.56\;\text{MPa}; \quad \sigma_\theta = +35.56\;\text{MPa}$$

$$\text{At } r=100\;\text{mm}: \quad \sigma_r = 0 - \frac{2\times10^{5}}{10000} = -20\;\text{MPa}; \quad \sigma_\theta = +20\;\text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check (a powerful KTU trick):** σᵣ + σ_θ = 2A = 0, **constant** at every radius. Always verify this in your answer — if it does not hold, your calculation has an error.

**Step 3 — Result Table.**

| r (mm) | σᵣ (MPa) | σ_θ (MPa) | σᵣ + σ_θ (MPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | −80.00 | +80.00 | 0 |
| 75 | −35.56 | +35.56 | 0 |
| 100 | −20.00 | +20.00 | 0 |

The radial stress is **compressive** everywhere (negative) and the hoop stress is **tensile** everywhere (positive). Both magnitudes are **maximum at the inner surface** and **decrease outward**, exactly as the physical analogy predicted.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solution Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete logical pipeline used to solve any axisymmetric thick-cylinder problem. The student should mentally walk through this block diagram whenever a new problem is encountered.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given inner radius a, outer radius b, internal pressure p_i, external pressure p_o] --> B[Identify Type: Plane Stress or Plane Strain]
    B --> C{End Condition?}
    C -->|Open ends| D[sigma_z = 0: Plane Stress]
    C -->|Closed ends| E[sigma_z = nu times sigma_r + sigma_theta: Plane Strain]
    D --> F[Write Lame Form: sigma_r = A - B by r squared, sigma_theta = A + B by r squared]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply BC1: At r equals a, sigma_r equals minus p_i]
    G --> H[Apply BC2: At r equals b, sigma_r equals minus p_o]
    H --> I[Solve 2 Equations for A and B]
    I --> J[Compute A: p_i a squared minus p_o b squared over b squared minus a squared]
    I --> K[Compute B: p_i minus p_o times a squared b squared over b squared minus a squared]
    J --> L[Substitute A and B into Lame Formulas]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Evaluate at Required Radii: r = a, mid r, b]
    M --> N[Compute Radial Displacement u of r if needed]
    N --> O[Check Invariant: sigma_r plus sigma_theta equals 2A, constant]
    O --> P[Final Result Table and Stress Diagram]
    P --> Q[END]
```

## 4.2 Stress Distribution Schematic — Conceptual Picture

The following Mermaid block captures the qualitative shape of the stress curves in a thick cylinder (radial coordinate `r` on the x-axis, stress value on y-axis). The student should imagine this as a textbook plot:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Inner["r = a (Inner Surface)"] -->|High compressive sigma_r| Mid["Mid Radius"]
    Mid -->|Zero at centre for pure p_i case| Outer["r = b (Outer Surface)"]
    Outer -->|Low compressive sigma_r| Result[Hoop Stress Sigma_Theta Max at Inner]
```

> **Visualisation cue for students:** Plot σ_r and σ_θ versus r on the same graph. You will see:
> - σ_r is a **concave-up curve**, starting at −pᵢ at r = a, rising smoothly to −pₒ at r = b.
> - σ_θ is a **concave-down curve**, starting at the maximum tensile value at r = a, decreasing to the (often compressive) value at r = b.
> - The two curves **never cross** in the case of pure internal pressure.

## 4.3 Sign-Convention Reference Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Pressure] --> B{Where?}
    B -->|Inside, pushing outward on wall| C[Radial Stress = NEGATIVE (Compression)]
    B -->|Outside, pushing inward on wall| D[Radial Stress = NEGATIVE (Compression)]
    C --> E[Wall tries to expand: Hoop Stress = POSITIVE (Tension)]
    D --> F[Wall tries to contract: Hoop Stress = NEGATIVE (Compression) or reduced tension]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3]

**State Lamé's equations for a thick-walled cylinder subjected to internal and external pressures. List the assumptions made in the derivation.**

**Model Answer:**

Lamé's equations for an axisymmetric thick-walled cylinder are:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}}, \qquad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$

where **A** and **B** are Lamé constants determined from the boundary conditions.

The boundary conditions are: σᵣ(a) = −pᵢ and σᵣ(b) = −pₒ, which yield:

$$A = \frac{p_i a^{2} - p_o b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}, \qquad B = \frac{(p_i - p_o)\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
2. The cylinder is long — plane sections remain plane; end effects neglected (plane strain/stress).
3. Loading is axisymmetric — uniform around the circumference and along the length.
4. No body forces (self-weight neglected).
5. Hooke's law is valid (small deformations).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3]

**Why is the hoop stress maximum at the inner surface of a thick cylinder subjected only to internal pressure?**

**Model Answer:**

From Lamé's equation, the hoop stress at any radius r is:
$$\sigma_\theta(r) = A + \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$

Since **B > 0** for the case pᵢ > 0, pₒ = 0, the term **B/r²** is **maximum at the smallest radius r = a** and decreases as r increases. At r = a, σ_θ attains its maximum value:

$$\sigma_\theta^{max} = \frac{p_i(a^{2} + b^{2})}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$$

Physically, the inner fibres are stretched by the **largest radial expansion** (since they are pulled outward by pressure and resisted by the entire mass of material outside them), while the outer fibres are constrained by the surrounding material. Hence the design of any internally pressurised cylinder is governed by the **inner surface** hoop stress.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A thick-walled steel cylinder has an internal radius of **75 mm** and an external radius of **150 mm**. It is subjected to an internal pressure of **60 MPa** and an external pressure of **15 MPa**. Using Lamé's equations, find the constants A and B. Also calculate the hoop stress at the inner and outer surfaces of the cylinder.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same cylinder, calculate the **change in outer diameter** and the **maximum shear stress** in the wall. Take E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa and ν = 0.3.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Calculation of A, B, and Hoop Stresses

**Step 1 — List the given data.**
a = 75 mm, b = 150 mm, pᵢ = 60 MPa, pₒ = 15 MPa, E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa, ν = 0.3.

**Step 2 — Compute the squares and the difference.**
a² = 5625 mm², b² = 22500 mm², b² − a² = 16875 mm².

**Step 3 — Compute Lamé constant A.**
$$A = \frac{p_i a^{2} - p_o b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{60 \times 5625 - 15 \times 22500}{16875}$$
$$A = \frac{337500 - 337500}{16875} = 0 \;\text{MPa}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** When pᵢ·a² = pₒ·b², the constant A vanishes — a special case where σᵣ + σ_θ = 0 everywhere.

**Step 4 — Compute Lamé constant B.**
$$B = \frac{(p_i - p_o)\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} = \frac{(60 - 15) \times 5625 \times 22500}{16875}$$
$$B = \frac{45 \times 1.2656 \times 10^{8}}{16875} = 3.375 \times 10^{5} \;\text{MPa·mm}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Hoop stress at the inner surface (r = a = 75 mm).**
$$\sigma_\theta(a) = A + \frac{B}{a^{2}} = 0 + \frac{3.375 \times 10^{5}}{5625} = +60 \;\text{MPa} \;(Tensile)$$

**Step 6 — Hoop stress at the outer surface (r = b = 150 mm).**
$$\sigma_\theta(b) = A + \frac{B}{b^{2}} = 0 + \frac{3.375 \times 10^{5}}{22500} = +15 \;\text{MPa} \;(Tensile)$$

> **Result check:** σ_r(a) = 0 − 60 = −60 MPa (matches −pᵢ ✓); σ_r(b) = 0 − 15 = −15 MPa (matches −pₒ ✓). Also σᵣ + σ_θ = 0 = 2A, invariant holds ✓.

#### Part (b) — Change in Outer Diameter and Maximum Shear Stress

**Step 1 — Radial displacement at the outer surface (Plane Stress assumed for open cylinder).**
$$u(b) = \frac{1}{E}\left[A(1-\nu)b + \frac{B(1+\nu)}{b}\right]$$
$$u(b) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{5}}\left[0 + \frac{3.375 \times 10^{5} \times 1.3}{150}\right]$$
$$u(b) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{5}} \times 2925 = 0.014625 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Change in outer diameter.**
$$\Delta D = 2\,u(b) = 2 \times 0.014625 = 0.02925 \;\text{mm} = 29.25 \;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Stresses for shear-stress calculation.**
At r = a (inner): σ_r = −60 MPa, σ_θ = +60 MPa.
At r = b (outer): σ_r = −15 MPa, σ_θ = +15 MPa.

**Step 4 — Principal stresses and maximum shear stress.**
- At **r = a**: σ_θ = 60 MPa, σ_r = −60 MPa, σ_z = 0.
  $$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \frac{60 - (-60)}{2} = 60 \;\text{MPa}$$
- At **r = b**: σ_θ = 15 MPa, σ_r = −15 MPa, σ_z = 0.
  $$\tau_{max} = \frac{15 - (-15)}{2} = 15 \;\text{MPa}$$

**Overall maximum shear stress in the wall = 60 MPa**, occurring at the **inner surface**.

> **Final Answer (Question A):** A = 0 MPa, B = 3.375 × 10⁵ MPa·mm², σ_θ(a) = 60 MPa, σ_θ(b) = 15 MPa, ΔD = 0.02925 mm, τ_max = 60 MPa.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Lamé's equations for the stress distribution in a thick-walled cylinder subjected to internal pressure pᵢ and external pressure pₒ, starting from the **radial equilibrium equation** and the **compatibility condition** in polar coordinates.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thick cylinder has a = 100 mm, b = 200 mm, pᵢ = 100 MPa, pₒ = 0. Determine the radial and hoop stresses at r = 100, 150, 200 mm, and the **radial displacement** of the inner surface. Take E = 2 × 10⁵ MPa, ν = 0.3.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Lamé's Equations

**Step 1 — Radial equilibrium of an elemental wedge.**

For an axisymmetric element of radial thickness dr and angular width dθ, summing forces in the radial direction:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0 \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Step 2 — Strain–displacement relations in polar coordinates.**
$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \qquad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r} \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 3 — Compatibility condition.**
$$\frac{d\varepsilon_\theta}{dr} + \frac{\varepsilon_\theta - \varepsilon_r}{r} = 0 \quad \cdots (3)$$

**Step 4 — Hooke's law (plane stress).**
$$\sigma_r = \frac{E}{1-\nu^{2}}\bigl(\varepsilon_r + \nu\varepsilon_\theta\bigr), \quad \sigma_\theta = \frac{E}{1-\nu^{2}}\bigl(\varepsilon_\theta + \nu\varepsilon_r\bigr) \quad \cdots (4)$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate strains.** From (2), compute the compatibility (3):
$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(\frac{u}{r}\right) + \frac{u/r - du/dr}{r} = 0$$
$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{du}{dr} - \frac{u}{r^{2}} + \frac{u}{r^{2}} - \frac{1}{r}\frac{du}{dr} = 0$$

This is identically satisfied for **any smooth u(r)** — i.e. compatibility is automatically satisfied for axisymmetric deformation. Therefore the **only independent equation** is equilibrium (1).

**Step 6 — Assume a stress function form.** Try $\sigma_r = f(r)$ and $\sigma_\theta = g(r)$. Substitute (4) into (1). Let:
$$\sigma_r + \sigma_\theta = 2A \quad \text{(a constant to be determined)}$$

This is motivated by the fact that $\sigma_r + \sigma_\theta$ depends only on the trace, which is related to the volumetric strain. After algebraic manipulation, one obtains:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}}, \qquad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Apply boundary conditions.**
- $\sigma_r(a) = -p_i \Rightarrow A - B/a^2 = -p_i$
- $\sigma_r(b) = -p_o \Rightarrow A - B/b^2 = -p_o$

Solving simultaneously:
$$A = \frac{p_i a^{2} - p_o b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}, \qquad B = \frac{(p_i - p_o)\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$$

This completes the derivation of Lamé's equations.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation

**Given:** a = 100 mm, b = 200 mm, pᵢ = 100 MPa, pₒ = 0.

**Step 1 — Lamé constants.**
a² = 10000 mm², b² = 40000 mm², b² − a² = 30000 mm².

$$A = \frac{100(10000) - 0}{30000} = \frac{10^{6}}{30000} = 33.33 \;\text{MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{(100 - 0)(10000)(40000)}{30000} = \frac{4 \times 10^{10}}{30000} = 1.3333 \times 10^{6} \;\text{MPa·mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Stresses at r = 100, 150, 200 mm.**

| r (mm) | σᵣ = A − B/r² (MPa) | σ_θ = A + B/r² (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | 33.33 − 133.33 = **−100.00** | 33.33 + 133.33 = **+166.67** |
| 150 | 33.33 − 59.26 = **−25.93** | 33.33 + 59.26 = **+92.59** |
| 200 | 33.33 − 33.33 = **0.00** | 33.33 + 33.33 = **+66.67** |

**Check:** σᵣ(a) = −100 = −pᵢ ✓; σᵣ(b) = 0 = −pₒ ✓; σᵣ + σ_θ = 2A = 66.67 MPa, **constant** ✓.

**Step 3 — Radial displacement at the inner surface (Plane Stress).**
$$u(a) = \frac{1}{E}\left[A(1-\nu)a + \frac{B(1+\nu)}{a}\right]$$
$$u(a) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{5}}\left[33.33 \times 0.7 \times 100 + \frac{1.3333 \times 10^{6} \times 1.3}{100}\right]$$
$$u(a) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{5}}\left[2333.1 + 17333.3\right] = \frac{19666.4}{2 \times 10^{5}} = 0.09833 \;\text{mm}$$

> **Final Answer (Question B):** σᵣ, σ_θ as in table above; u(a) = 0.0983 mm (outward radial displacement of the inner bore).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign errors on radial stress:** Students often write σ_r(a) = +pᵢ. It is **−pᵢ** (compression). Losing 2 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting that A and B have different units.** A is in MPa, but B is in **MPa·mm²**. Plugging B directly into σ = A + B/r² **without dividing by r²** is a frequent blunder.
> 3. **Mixing up "Plane Stress" and "Plane Strain"** displacement formulas. For closed-end (long) cylinders, use plane-strain u(r); for open-end cylinders, use plane-stress u(r).
> 4. **Maximum hoop stress is always at the inner surface** (for internal pressurisation). The student who evaluates σ_θ at the outer surface and declares it "maximum" loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Failing to verify σᵣ + σ_θ = 2A = constant.** Examiners give bonus marks (or at least do not penalise) for a verification check.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Lamé's Equations** are the *exact* elastic solution for axisymmetric pressurised cylinders — they **supersede** thin-walled hoop-stress formulas when t/a > 0.1.
- [x] **Two stress components** only: σᵣ(r) and σ_θ(r). **σ_z depends on end condition** (open ⇒ 0; closed ⇒ ν(σᵣ+σ_θ)).
- [x] **Lamé constants:**
  - $A = \dfrac{p_i a^{2} - p_o b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$
  - $B = \dfrac{(p_i - p_o)\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$
- [x] **Stress formulas:**
  - $\sigma_r = A - B/r^{2}$
  - $\sigma_\theta = A + B/r^{2}$
- [x] **Invariant (a free verification):** $\sigma_r + \sigma_\theta = 2A = \text{constant at every radius.}$
- [x] **Boundary conditions:** $\sigma_r(a) = -p_i$ and $\sigma_r(b) = -p_o$ (tension positive convention).
- [x] **Maximum hoop stress** is at the **inner surface** (for $p_i > p_o$). Formula:
  $$\sigma_\theta^{max} = \frac{p_i(a^{2}+b^{2}) - 2p_o b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$$
- [x] **Minimum hoop stress** is at the **outer surface**:
  $$\sigma_\theta^{min} = \frac{2p_i a^{2} - p_o(a^{2}+b^{2})}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$$
- [x] **Radial displacement (Plane Stress):**
  $$u(r) = \frac{1}{E}\left[A(1-\nu)r + \frac{B(1+\nu)}{r}\right]$$
- [x] **Change in outer diameter:** $\Delta D = 2\,u(b)$.
- [x] **Maximum shear stress** in the wall occurs at the inner surface, equal to $\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_\theta(a) - \sigma_r(a)}{2} = \frac{\sigma_\theta(a) + p_i}{2}$.
- [x] **Special case A = 0** occurs when $p_i a^{2} = p_o b^{2}$ — the radial and hoop stresses are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign at every radius.
- [x] **Compound cylinders** (autofrettage, shrink-fit) use superposition of two single-cylinder Lamé solutions with the **interface pressure** as the unknown — solved using **equal radial displacement** at the common radius.
- [x] **Physical intuition:** Inner surface is the most critical fibre; radial stress is compressive; hoop stress is tensile for internal pressurisation.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy Tip:** In a 14-mark KTU question, always (i) state assumptions, (ii) compute A and B, (iii) tabulate σᵣ, σ_θ at three radii, (iv) verify with σᵣ+σ_θ invariant, (v) compute displacement and/or max shear stress. This 5-step structure fetches **full marks consistently**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compound Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compound Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Compound Cylinder** (also called a **built-up cylinder** or **shrink-fitted cylinder**) is a multi-layered cylindrical assembly in which two or more concentric hollow cylinders of different (or identical) materials are joined together by means of a **shrink fit** (also known as an interference fit). The outer cylinder is heated uniformly and expanded, slipped over the inner cylinder, and then allowed to cool. As it cools, the outer cylinder tries to contract to its original size but is restrained by the inner cylinder, thereby inducing a **radial contact (interface) pressure**, denoted by **p**, at the common interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET411, Module 3):**
> *A compound cylinder is a combination of two or more thick cylinders of different materials assembled together by shrinking one cylinder over the other so that a uniform radial pressure, called the interface pressure, is set up at the junction. This pre-stress counteracts the stresses due to internal fluid pressure and increases the safe working pressure of the vessel.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a compound cylinder like a **Russian nesting doll (Matryoshka)** but pressed together so tightly that they behave as a single unit. Imagine taking a tight metal ring and forcing it over a slightly larger metal shaft using brute mechanical force — once on, the ring squeezes the shaft continuously. The compound cylinder does the same thing, but uses **thermal expansion** rather than mechanical force to achieve the interference.

A more practical analogy: a **bicycle wheel rim and tire**. The tire is slightly smaller than the rim, and once stretched over the rim, the tire's compression keeps it locked in place. In engineering cylinders, this compressive pre-stress **counteracts the tensile hoop stresses** that would otherwise be created by internal pressure — making the vessel stronger and safer.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus (E) for Steel:** **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200 GPa)**
- **Young's Modulus (E) for Cast Iron:** **1.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (100 GPa)**
- **Poisson's ratio (ν) for Steel:** **0.30**
- **Poisson's ratio (ν) for Cast Iron:** **0.25**
- **Typical radial interference (δ):** **0.05 mm to 0.5 mm** depending on cylinder diameter
- **Working pressure range for compound pressure vessels:** **up to 700 MPa** in high-pressure applications (e.g., gun barrels, autoclaves, hydraulic accumulators)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a compound cylinder instead of a single thick cylinder?**
> 1. Reduces the **maximum hoop stress** in the assembly.
> 2. Allows the use of **two different materials** — e.g., a corrosion-resistant inner liner with a high-strength outer shell.
> 3. Permits the construction of vessels with **very large diameters** where single-piece forging is impossible.
> 4. Improves **fatigue life** because the inner cylinder is in compression initially, not tension.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Requisite: Lamé's Equations for Thick Cylinders

Lamé's equations (1852) give the radial and hoop stresses in a thick cylinder under axisymmetric loading:

$$
\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}
$$

where:
- **A, B** = Lamé's constants (determined from boundary conditions)
- **r** = radial distance from the cylinder axis
- **σᵣ** = radial stress (compressive negative)
- **σ_θ** = hoop (circumferential) stress (tensile positive for internal pressure)

The corresponding radial displacement (plane stress) is:

$$
u = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)\,A\,r + (1+\nu)\,\frac{B}{r}\right]
$$

## 2.2 Operational Logic: Why and How the Compound Cylinder Works

**Step 1 — Initial Assembly (No service pressure):**
The outer cylinder is heated, expanded, slipped over the inner cylinder, and cooled. A **contact pressure p** develops at the common interface radius **a**. At this stage:
- The **inner cylinder** is loaded only by external pressure **p** (no internal pressure).
- The **outer cylinder** is loaded by internal pressure **p** at r = a and zero pressure at r = r_o.

**Step 2 — Service Condition (With internal pressure pᵢ):**
When the vessel is put into operation, an internal pressure **pᵢ** is applied. This adds tensile stresses to both cylinders. The **resultant stress** in any cylinder is the **algebraic sum** of:
1. Shrink-fit stresses (from Step 1)
2. Stresses due to the applied internal pressure (computed as if it were a single thick cylinder with no shrinkage)

**Step 3 — Final Working Stresses:**
Since the shrink-fit induces **compressive hoop stress** in the inner cylinder, the tensile stress from internal pressure is **partially cancelled** — yielding a much smaller net hoop stress. This is the central engineering advantage.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions (Master Setup)

Let:
- **rᵢ** = inner radius of the compound cylinder
- **a** = interface radius
- **rₒ** = outer radius of the compound cylinder
- **p** = interface (contact) pressure (unknown, to be determined)
- **pᵢ** = applied internal pressure (service condition)
- **pₒ** = applied external pressure (usually zero)
- **δ** = radial interference (a − b), where b is the original inner radius of the outer cylinder before assembly

| Cylinder | Range | BC at inner radius | BC at outer radius |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inner | rᵢ ≤ r ≤ a | σᵣ = −pᵢ (or 0 if pᵢ = 0) | σᵣ = −p |
| Outer | a ≤ r ≤ rₒ | σᵣ = −p | σᵣ = −pₒ (or 0 if pₒ = 0) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lamé's radial stress | $\sigma_r = A - \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | N/mm² |
| 2 | Lamé's hoop stress | $\sigma_\theta = A + \dfrac{B}{r^2}$ | N/mm² |
| 3 | Inner cyl. radial stress (no pᵢ) | $\sigma_{r,i} = -\dfrac{p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{r_i^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Negative (compressive) |
| 4 | Inner cyl. hoop stress (no pᵢ) | $\sigma_{\theta,i} = -\dfrac{p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}\left(1 + \dfrac{r_i^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Negative (compressive) |
| 5 | Outer cyl. radial stress | $\sigma_{r,o} = \dfrac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Negative (compressive) |
| 6 | Outer cyl. hoop stress | $\sigma_{\theta,o} = \dfrac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \dfrac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)$ | Positive (tensile) |
| 7 | Max hoop stress — inner cyl. (at r = rᵢ) | $\sigma_{\theta,\max}^{(i)} = -\,\dfrac{p\,(a^2 + r_i^2)}{a^2 - r_i^2}$ | Compressive, N/mm² |
| 8 | Max hoop stress — outer cyl. (at r = a) | $\sigma_{\theta,\max}^{(o)} = \dfrac{p\,(a^2 + r_o^2)}{r_o^2 - a^2}$ | Tensile, N/mm² |
| 9 | Contact pressure (same material) | $p = \dfrac{\delta\,E\,(r_o^2 - a^2)(a^2 - r_i^2)}{2a\,\bigl[(a^2 - r_i^2)\bigl(a^2(1-\nu) + r_o^2(1+\nu)\bigr) + (r_o^2 - a^2)\bigl(a^2(1-\nu) + r_i^2(1+\nu)\bigr)\bigr]}$ | N/mm² |
| 10 | Contact pressure (different materials) | $p = \dfrac{\delta}{\dfrac{a}{E_o}\dfrac{a^2(1-\nu_o) + r_o^2(1+\nu_o)}{r_o^2 - a^2} + \dfrac{a}{E_i}\dfrac{a^2(1-\nu_i) + r_i^2(1+\nu_i)}{a^2 - r_i^2}}$ | N/mm² |
| 11 | Radial displacement (plane stress) | $u = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)\,A\,r + (1+\nu)\,\dfrac{B}{r}\right]$ | mm |
| 12 | Shrink fit compatibility | $u_o(a) - u_i(a) = \delta$ | mm |
| 13 | Resultant hoop stress (service) | $\sigma_{\theta,\text{net}} = \sigma_{\theta}^{\text{shrink}} + \sigma_{\theta}^{\text{pressure}}$ | Superposition principle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are routinely applied in the design of **high-pressure autoclaves, hydraulic cylinders, gun barrels, rolling-mill housings, reactor vessels, and submarine hulls** — anywhere the safe working pressure exceeds ~50 MPa. Major industrial codes (ASME Section VIII, IS 2825) provide guidance on permissible shrink allowances.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lamé's Constants for the Inner Cylinder (External Pressure p Only)

**Setup:** The inner cylinder occupies rᵢ ≤ r ≤ a. There is no internal pressure (pᵢ = 0). The outer surface is loaded by contact pressure p (compressive).

**Boundary conditions:**
- At r = rᵢ : σᵣ = 0 (free inner surface)
- At r = a : σᵣ = −p (external contact pressure)

**Step 1:** Apply BC at r = rᵢ to Lamé's equation:

$$
A - \frac{B}{r_i^2} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A = \frac{B}{r_i^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply BC at r = a:

$$
A - \frac{B}{a^2} = -p
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute A from Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
\frac{B}{r_i^2} - \frac{B}{a^2} = -p
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out B:

$$
B\left(\frac{a^2 - r_i^2}{a^2 r_i^2}\right) = -p
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for B:

$$
B = \frac{-p\,a^2 r_i^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for A using A = B / rᵢ²:

$$
A = \frac{B}{r_i^2} = \frac{-p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute A and B back into Lamé's equations:

$$
\sigma_{r,i} = -\frac{p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2} + \frac{p\,a^2 r_i^2}{(a^2 - r_i^2)\,r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{r,i} = -\frac{p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}\left(1 - \frac{r_i^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta,i} = -\frac{p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2} - \frac{p\,a^2 r_i^2}{(a^2 - r_i^2)\,r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta,i} = -\frac{p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}\left(1 + \frac{r_i^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

**Verification:** At r = a, σᵣ,ᵢ = −p·a²(a²−rᵢ²)/[(a²−rᵢ²)·a²] = −p ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lamé's Constants for the Outer Cylinder (Internal Pressure p Only)

**Setup:** The outer cylinder occupies a ≤ r ≤ rₒ. The inner surface is loaded by contact pressure p (compressive on the outer cylinder). The outer surface is free (pₒ = 0).

**Boundary conditions:**
- At r = a : σᵣ = −p
- At r = rₒ : σᵣ = 0

**Step 1:** Apply BC at r = rₒ:

$$
A - \frac{B}{r_o^2} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A = \frac{B}{r_o^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply BC at r = a:

$$
A - \frac{B}{a^2} = -p
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute A from Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
\frac{B}{r_o^2} - \frac{B}{a^2} = -p
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out B:

$$
B\left(\frac{a^2 - r_o^2}{a^2 r_o^2}\right) = -p
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for B:

$$
B = \frac{-p\,a^2 r_o^2}{a^2 - r_o^2} = \frac{p\,a^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for A:

$$
A = \frac{B}{r_o^2} = \frac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute A and B into Lamé's equations:

$$
\sigma_{r,o} = \frac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2} - \frac{p\,a^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - a^2)\,r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{r,o} = \frac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta,o} = \frac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2} + \frac{p\,a^2 r_o^2}{(r_o^2 - a^2)\,r^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\theta,o} = \frac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

**Verification:** At r = a, σᵣ,ₒ = p·a²(a²−rₒ²)/[(rₒ²−a²)·a²] = −p ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Contact Pressure from Shrink-Fit Compatibility

**Compatibility equation:** The radial interference δ must equal the relative displacement of the interface surfaces of the two cylinders after assembly.

$$
u_o(a) - u_i(a) = \delta
$$

where δ = a − b, and b is the original inner radius of the outer cylinder before assembly.

**Step 1:** Displacement of the inner cylinder at r = a (using Aᵢ and Bᵢ from §3.1):

$$
u_i(a) = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)\,A_i\,a + (1+\nu)\,\frac{B_i}{a}\right]
$$

$$
u_i(a) = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)\left(\frac{-p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}\right)a + (1+\nu)\left(\frac{-p\,a^2 r_i^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}\right)\frac{1}{a}\right]
$$

$$
u_i(a) = \frac{-p\,a}{E\,(a^2 - r_i^2)}\left[(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_i^2\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Displacement of the outer cylinder at r = a (using Aₒ and Bₒ from §3.2):

$$
u_o(a) = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)\,A_o\,a + (1+\nu)\,\frac{B_o}{a}\right]
$$

$$
u_o(a) = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)\left(\frac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}\right)a + (1+\nu)\left(\frac{p\,a^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}\right)\frac{1}{a}\right]
$$

$$
u_o(a) = \frac{p\,a}{E\,(r_o^2 - a^2)}\left[(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_o^2\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Apply compatibility uₒ(a) − uᵢ(a) = δ:

$$
\frac{p\,a}{E}\left\{\frac{(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_o^2}{r_o^2 - a^2} + \frac{(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1-\nu)\,r_i^2 \cdot \frac{(1+\nu)}{(1-\nu)}}{\,a^2 - r_i^2\,}\right\} = \delta
$$

Simplifying the second term (note the second term in the braces reverts back to the form already used):

$$
\frac{p\,a}{E}\left\{\frac{(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_o^2}{r_o^2 - a^2} + \frac{(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_i^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}\right\} = \delta
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for p by multiplying through by (rₒ² − a²)(a² − rᵢ²):

$$
p = \frac{\delta\,E\,(r_o^2 - a^2)(a^2 - r_i^2)}{a\left\{\left[(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_o^2\right](a^2 - r_i^2) + \left[(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_i^2\right](r_o^2 - a^2)\right\}}
$$

This is the **master equation for contact pressure** for same-material compound cylinders under plane stress.

## 3.4 Worked Example (Same-Material, No Internal Pressure)

**Given:** A compound cylinder is made of steel with E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² and ν = 0.3. Dimensions: rᵢ = 100 mm, a = 200 mm, rₒ = 300 mm. Radial interference δ = 0.10 mm. Find the contact pressure and the maximum hoop stresses.

**Step 1:** Compute auxiliary terms.

$$
r_o^2 - a^2 = 300^2 - 200^2 = 90000 - 40000 = 50000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
a^2 - r_i^2 = 200^2 - 100^2 = 40000 - 10000 = 30000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_o^2 = 0.7(40000) + 1.3(90000) = 28000 + 117000 = 145000
$$

$$
(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_i^2 = 0.7(40000) + 1.3(10000) = 28000 + 13000 = 41000
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the denominator bracket.

$$
145000 \times 30000 + 41000 \times 50000 = 4.35 \times 10^9 + 2.05 \times 10^9 = 6.40 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 3:** Compute contact pressure p.

$$
p = \frac{0.10 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 50000 \times 30000}{200 \times 6.40 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
p = \frac{3.00 \times 10^{13}}{1.28 \times 10^{12}} = 23.44 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 23.44 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Maximum hoop stress in inner cylinder (at r = rᵢ = 100 mm).

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\max}^{(i)} = -\frac{23.44 \times (200^2 + 100^2)}{200^2 - 100^2} = -\frac{23.44 \times 50000}{30000} = -39.06 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Step 5:** Maximum hoop stress in outer cylinder (at r = a = 200 mm).

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\max}^{(o)} = \frac{23.44 \times (200^2 + 300^2)}{300^2 - 200^2} = \frac{23.44 \times 130000}{50000} = 60.94 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

**Result:** Contact pressure **p = 23.44 MPa**, max hoop stress in inner = **−39.06 MPa**, max hoop stress in outer = **+60.94 MPa**.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Compound Cylinder Under Service Pressure (Superposition)

**Given:** The cylinder from §3.4 is now subjected to an internal pressure pᵢ = 50 MPa (with no external pressure). Find the resultant maximum hoop stresses.

**Step 1 — Shrink-fit stresses (already computed in §3.4):**
- Inner cylinder, r = rᵢ: σ_θ_shrink = −39.06 MPa
- Outer cylinder, r = a: σ_θ_shrink = +60.94 MPa

**Step 2 — Stresses due to applied internal pressure pᵢ = 50 MPa (treat the whole as a single cylinder from rᵢ to rₒ):**

$$
A = \frac{p_i\,r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{50 \times 10000}{90000 - 10000} = \frac{500000}{80000} = 6.25 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
B = \frac{p_i\,r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{50 \times 10000 \times 90000}{80000} = 5.625 \times 10^6 \text{ MPa·mm}^2
$$

**At r = rᵢ = 100 mm (inner surface):**

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\text{pressure}}^{(i)} = A + \frac{B}{r_i^2} = 6.25 + \frac{5.625 \times 10^6}{10000} = 6.25 + 562.5 = 568.75 \text{ MPa}
$$

**At r = a = 200 mm (inner surface of outer cylinder, the critical point):**

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\text{pressure}}^{(a)} = A + \frac{B}{a^2} = 6.25 + \frac{5.625 \times 10^6}{40000} = 6.25 + 140.625 = 146.875 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Superposition (resultant stresses):**

| Location | Shrink-fit (MPa) | Pressure (MPa) | Resultant (MPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inner cyl., r = rᵢ (max tensile zone) | −39.06 | +568.75 | **+529.69** |
| Outer cyl., r = a (max tensile zone) | +60.94 | +146.88 | **+207.81** |
| Outer cyl., r = rₒ (outer surface) | σ_θ_shrink = ? | σ_θ_pressure = 6.25 (≈ A) | small |

**Engineering insight:** Without the shrink fit, the inner surface would have a hoop stress of **568.75 MPa**. With the shrink fit, this drops to **529.69 MPa** — a **6.9% reduction**. With a more aggressive shrink (e.g., δ = 0.3 mm), reductions of 15–25% are achievable. This is why compound cylinders are the design of choice for ultra-high-pressure service.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Compound Cylinder Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compound Cylinder Assembly] --> B[Inner Cylinder]
    A --> C[Outer Cylinder]
    A --> D[Common Interface at Radius a]

    B --> B1[Inner radius ri]
    B --> B2[Outer radius a]
    B --> B3[Material Ei ni]

    C --> C1[Inner radius b lt a]
    C --> C2[Outer radius ro]
    C --> C3[Material Eo no]

    D --> D1[Radial interference delta = a minus b]
    D --> D2[Contact pressure p developed on cooling]

    E[Shrink Fit Process] --> E1[Heat outer cylinder]
    E1 --> E2[Slip over inner cylinder]
    E2 --> E3[Cool to room temperature]
    E3 --> E4[Interface pressure p locks the assembly]

    F[Service Loading] --> F1[Internal pressure pi]
    F --> F2[External pressure po]
    F1 --> G[Superposition of stresses]
    F2 --> G

    G --> H[Final resultant hoop and radial stresses]
    H --> I[Check against allowable stress of each material]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Identify geometry ri, a, ro, delta] --> S2[Step 2: List material properties Ei, Eo, ni, no]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply Lame equations to both cylinders]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Impose 4 boundary conditions]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute 4 Lame constants A and B]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Write radial displacement at r = a for each cylinder]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Apply compatibility uo minus ui = delta]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Solve for interface pressure p]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Back substitute p into stress equations]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: If service pressure exists, superpose]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Identify maximum stresses and check design]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph InnerCyl["Inner Cylinder r from ri to a"]
        I1[At r = ri: hoop stress maximum compression]

    subgraph OuterCyl["Outer Cylinder r from a to ro"]
```
