# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a compound cylinder. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the concept of shrink fit.**

**Model Answer:**

A compound cylinder is an assembly of two or more concentric cylinders fitted together by **shrink fitting**, in which the outer cylinder is heated, expanded, slipped over the inner cylinder, and allowed to cool. Upon cooling, the outer cylinder contracts and develops a **radial contact pressure (p)** at the common interface, locking the two cylinders together.

The shrink-fit introduces **compressive hoop stresses in the inner cylinder** and **tensile hoop stresses in the outer cylinder**. This pre-stressing counteracts the tensile stresses caused by internal fluid pressure during service, allowing the vessel to safely contain higher working pressures than a single thick cylinder of the same overall dimensions.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for procedure, 1 mark for engineering significance.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State Lamé's equations for thick cylinders. What are the boundary conditions used in deriving the stress distribution in a compound cylinder with shrink fit?**

**Model Answer:**

Lamé's equations (1852) for a thick cylinder under internal and/or external pressure are:

$$
\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \qquad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}
$$

where **A** and **B** are constants determined from boundary conditions, and **r** is the radial distance from the axis.

For a **compound cylinder with shrink fit and no service pressure**, the four boundary conditions are:

| # | Surface | Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inner surface of inner cylinder, r = rᵢ | σᵣ = 0 (no internal pressure) |
| 2 | Interface, r = a (inner cylinder side) | σᵣ = −p (contact pressure) |
| 3 | Interface, r = a (outer cylinder side) | σᵣ = −p (continuity of pressure) |
| 4 | Outer surface, r = rₒ | σᵣ = 0 (no external pressure) |

A fifth equation comes from **compatibility** (radial interference): uₒ(a) − uᵢ(a) = δ, which yields the contact pressure p.

*(2 marks for equations, 1 mark for listing BCs.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Module Internal Choice — Select Either A or B

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** *Derive the expressions for hoop and radial stresses in the inner and outer cylinders of a compound cylinder assembled by shrink fit, assuming no internal or external service pressure. State the compatibility equation used to determine the contact pressure. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lamé's equations and unknowns:** Each cylinder has two Lamé constants (A, B), giving four unknowns in total. We have three stress boundary conditions and one compatibility equation, providing four equations to solve.

**Step 2 — Inner cylinder, rᵢ ≤ r ≤ a, external pressure p:**

$$
A_i - \frac{B_i}{r_i^2} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A_i = \frac{B_i}{r_i^2}
$$

$$
A_i - \frac{B_i}{a^2} = -p
$$

Substituting and solving:

$$
A_i = -\frac{p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}, \qquad B_i = -\frac{p\,a^2 r_i^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}
$$

The stresses in the inner cylinder become:

$$
\sigma_{r,i} = -\frac{p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}\left(1 - \frac{r_i^2}{r^2}\right), \qquad \sigma_{\theta,i} = -\frac{p\,a^2}{a^2 - r_i^2}\left(1 + \frac{r_i^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Outer cylinder, a ≤ r ≤ rₒ, internal pressure p:**

$$
A_o - \frac{B_o}{r_o^2} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A_o = \frac{B_o}{r_o^2}
$$

$$
A_o - \frac{B_o}{a^2} = -p
$$

Solving:

$$
A_o = \frac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}, \qquad B_o = \frac{p\,a^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}
$$

The stresses in the outer cylinder become:

$$
\sigma_{r,o} = \frac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}\left(1 - \frac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right), \qquad \sigma_{\theta,o} = \frac{p\,a^2}{r_o^2 - a^2}\left(1 + \frac{r_o^2}{r^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Compatibility equation:** The radial interference equals the relative displacement at r = a:

$$
u_o(a) - u_i(a) = \delta
$$

where **δ = a − b** is the radial interference (b = original inner radius of outer cylinder before assembly). The radial displacement (plane stress) is:

$$
u(r) = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1-\nu)\,A\,r + (1+\nu)\,\frac{B}{r}\right]
$$

Substituting and simplifying yields the contact pressure:

$$
p = \frac{\delta\,E\,(r_o^2 - a^2)(a^2 - r_i^2)}{a\left\{\left[(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_o^2\right](a^2 - r_i^2) + \left[(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_i^2\right](r_o^2 - a^2)\right\}}
$$

*(Full marks: 3 for inner cylinder derivation, 3 for outer cylinder derivation, 1 for compatibility equation.)*

---

**(b)** *A compound cylinder has rᵢ = 50 mm, a = 100 mm, rₒ = 150 mm. The radial interference is δ = 0.08 mm. Both cylinders are made of steel (E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.3). Calculate: (i) the contact pressure at the interface, and (ii) the maximum hoop stresses in both cylinders. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Auxiliary quantities:**

$$
r_o^2 - a^2 = 22500 - 10000 = 12500 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
a^2 - r_i^2 = 10000 - 2500 = 7500 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_o^2 = 0.7(10000) + 1.3(22500) = 7000 + 29250 = 36250
$$

$$
(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_i^2 = 0.7(10000) + 1.3(2500) = 7000 + 3250 = 10250
$$

**Step 2 — Denominator bracket:**

$$
36250 \times 7500 + 10250 \times 12500 = 2.719 \times 10^8 + 1.281 \times 10^8 = 4.000 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Contact pressure:**

$$
p = \frac{0.08 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 12500 \times 7500}{100 \times 4.000 \times 10^8} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{12}}{4.0 \times 10^{10}} = 37.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Max hoop stress in inner cylinder (at r = rᵢ = 50 mm):**

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\max}^{(i)} = -\frac{37.5 \times (100^2 + 50^2)}{100^2 - 50^2} = -\frac{37.5 \times 12500}{7500} = -62.5 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Step 5 — Max hoop stress in outer cylinder (at r = a = 100 mm):**

$$
\sigma_{\theta,\max}^{(o)} = \frac{37.5 \times (100^2 + 150^2)}{150^2 - 100^2} = \frac{37.5 \times 32500}{12500} = 97.5 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

**Results:**
- Contact pressure, p = **37.5 MPa**
- Max hoop stress in inner cylinder = **−62.5 MPa**
- Max hoop stress in outer cylinder = **+97.5 MPa**

*(1 mark each for steps 1 and 2, 1 mark for p, 1 mark each for the two max stresses, 1 mark for units and final statement.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain in detail with neat sketches the constructional features of a compound cylinder. Compare its advantages and limitations over a single thick cylinder. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Constructional Features:**

A compound cylinder consists of two or more hollow cylinders arranged concentrically, as shown in the figure below (conceptually):

1. **Inner cylinder (or core):** A hollow cylinder of inner radius **rᵢ** and outer radius **a**, usually forged or cast from a corrosion-resistant or high-strength material.
2. **Outer cylinder (or shell):** A hollow cylinder of inner radius **b** (slightly less than **a** before assembly) and outer radius **rₒ**, typically a high-tensile material such as alloy steel.
3. **Shrink fit interface:** Before assembly, the outer cylinder is heated uniformly to a temperature at which its inner radius **b** becomes greater than or equal to **a**. It is then slipped over the inner cylinder and allowed to cool naturally. As it cools, the outer cylinder contracts and presses tightly against the inner cylinder, creating a uniform radial contact pressure **p** at the common interface r = a.
4. **End closures:** Spherical or ellipsoidal heads are welded or bolted to seal the vessel. Bolted flanges permit periodic inspection of the inner cylinder.

**Advantages of Compound Cylinders over Single Thick Cylinders:**

| # | Advantage | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Higher safe working pressure | Pre-compression in inner cylinder offsets tensile stress from internal pressure |
| 2 | Use of two different materials | Inner liner can be corrosion-resistant (e.g., stainless steel); outer shell can be high-strength carbon steel |
| 3 | Reduced weight | Overall mass can be 15–30% less than a single thick cylinder of equivalent capacity |
| 4 | Manufacturability | Each cylinder can be cast, forged, or rolled separately — no single piece of required size |
| 5 | Better fatigue life | Inner cylinder is initially in compression, reducing alternating stress amplitude |
| 6 | Repairability | Inner cylinder can be replaced if corroded or eroded |

**Limitations:**

| # | Limitation | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Complex assembly | Requires heating furnace, careful temperature control, and alignment fixtures |
| 2 | Interference loss over time | Creep, thermal cycling, and vibration may relax the contact pressure |
| 3 | Higher initial cost | More materials, inspection, and skilled labour |
| 4 | Difficult quality control | Hidden interface cannot be inspected non-destructively in service |
| 5 | Slip risk under high surge loads | Sudden pressure transients can momentarily overcome friction at the interface |

*(2 marks for constructional sketch/description, 3 marks for advantages with engineering reasoning, 2 marks for limitations.)*

---

**(b)** *A compound cylinder is made of two steel cylinders with E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.3, rᵢ = 75 mm, a = 150 mm, rₒ = 225 mm, and radial interference δ = 0.06 mm. The cylinder is subjected to an internal service pressure pᵢ = 60 MPa (with no external pressure). Determine the resultant maximum hoop stresses in both cylinders using the principle of superposition. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Shrink-fit stresses (no service pressure):** Using the standard formula:

$$
r_o^2 - a^2 = 225^2 - 150^2 = 50625 - 22500 = 28125
$$

$$
a^2 - r_i^2 = 150^2 - 75^2 = 22500 - 5625 = 16875
$$

$$
(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_o^2 = 0.7(22500) + 1.3(50625) = 15750 + 65812.5 = 81562.5
$$

$$
(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_i^2 = 0.7(22500) + 1.3(5625) = 15750 + 7312.5 = 23062.5
$$

Denominator: 81562.5 × 16875 + 23062.5 × 28125 = 1.376 × 10⁹ + 6.487 × 10⁸ = 2.025 × 10⁹ mm⁴

$$
p = \frac{0.06 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 28125 \times 16875}{150 \times 2.025 \times 10^9} = \frac{5.694 \times 10^{12}}{3.038 \times 10^{11}} = 18.75 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Shrink-fit hoop stresses:**
- Inner cyl. at r = rᵢ: σ_θ^shrink = −18.75 × (150² + 75²)/(150² − 75²) = −18.75 × 28125/16875 = **−31.25 MPa**
- Outer cyl. at r = a: σ_θ^shrink = +18.75 × (150² + 225²)/(225² − 150²) = +18.75 × 73125/28125 = **+48.75 MPa**

**Step 2 — Service pressure stresses (treat as single cylinder from rᵢ to rₒ):**

$$
A = \frac{p_i\,r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{60 \times 5625}{50625 - 5625} = \frac{337500}{45000} = 7.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
B = \frac{p_i\,r_i^2\,r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{60 \times 5625 \times 50625}{45000} = 3.797 \times 10^6 \text{ MPa·mm}^2
$$

**At r = rᵢ (inner surface, max stress point):**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{pressure} = 7.5 + \frac{3.797 \times 10^6}{5625} = 7.5 + 675.0 = 682.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**At r = a (interface, max stress point of outer):**

$$
\sigma_\theta^{pressure} = 7.5 + \frac{3.797 \times 10^6}{22500} = 7.5 + 168.75 = 176.25 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Superposition:**

| Location | Shrink-fit (MPa) | Service (MPa) | Resultant (MPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inner cyl., r = rᵢ | −31.25 | +682.5 | **+651.25** |
| Outer cyl., r = a | +48.75 | +176.25 | **+225.00** |

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- The inner cylinder's hoop stress is reduced from 682.5 MPa (without shrink fit) to **651.25 MPa** — a **~4.6% reduction**.
- The outer cylinder's hoop stress at the interface is **225.0 MPa** (worst-case point of outer cylinder).

*(2 marks for shrink-fit analysis, 2 marks for service pressure analysis, 2 marks for superposition, 1 mark for interpretation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners dock marks for:**
> 1. **Sign confusion in BCs:** At the interface, both cylinders see **σᵣ = −p** (compressive). Forgetting the negative sign flips the sign of A and B in both cylinders and corrupts every subsequent calculation. **Always check: contact pressure is compressive on both mating surfaces.**
> 2. **Compatibility sign error:** The correct compatibility is **uₒ(a) − uᵢ(a) = δ** (with δ > 0). Some students write uᵢ − uₒ = δ, giving the wrong sign for p. Tip: outer cylinder's inner surface moves **outward** (+), inner cylinder's outer surface moves **inward** (−); the difference is positive δ.
> 3. **Forgetting to superpose in service-condition problems:** When the vessel has internal pressure, the final hoop stress = (shrink-fit stress) + (pressure-only stress). Many students report only the shrink-fit stress and lose 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Unit errors in δ:** δ must be in **mm** (not m or cm) when E is in N/mm². A δ of "0.1 m" gives 100 mm interference — physically absurd. Use SI consistently.
> 5. **Plane stress vs. plane strain:** Standard derivation assumes **plane stress** (long cylinder with free ends). For closed-end pressure vessels, use plane strain by replacing ν with ν/(1−ν) and E with E/(1−ν²). KTU 2024 problems often specify which case applies.
> 6. **Maximum hoop stress location:** Inner cylinder max compressive hoop stress is at **r = rᵢ** (inner surface). Outer cylinder max tensile hoop stress is at **r = a** (interface). Getting these swapped is a 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Compound cylinder =** two (or more) concentric cylinders joined by **shrink fit**, producing a **radial contact pressure p** at the interface.
- **Lamé's equations:** $\sigma_r = A - B/r^2$, $\sigma_\theta = A + B/r^2$. Two constants per cylinder.
- **Four Lamé constants** (Aᵢ, Bᵢ, Aₒ, Bₒ) are determined from **three stress BCs** and **one compatibility equation**.
- **Compatibility condition:** $u_o(a) - u_i(a) = \delta$, where **δ = a − b** is the radial interference.
- **Sign convention:** Internal pressure → tensile hoop (+). External/contact pressure → compressive hoop (−).
- **Max compressive hoop stress in inner cylinder** is at **r = rᵢ**: $\sigma_{\theta,\max}^{(i)} = -p(a^2 + r_i^2)/(a^2 - r_i^2)$.
- **Max tensile hoop stress in outer cylinder** is at **r = a**: $\sigma_{\theta,\max}^{(o)} = p(a^2 + r_o^2)/(r_o^2 - a^2)$.
- **Service condition:** Use **superposition** — total hoop stress = shrink-fit stress + pressure-only stress.
- **Typical E for steel = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²**, ν = 0.30; **typical δ = 0.05–0.5 mm**.
- **Plane stress assumption** is standard for long open-ended cylinders; use **plane strain** for closed-end pressure vessels (replace ν → ν/(1−ν) and E → E/(1−ν²)).
- **Engineering applications:** gun barrels, autoclaves, hydraulic accumulators, reactor vessels, rolling-mill housings, submarine pressure hulls.
- **Advantage:** reduces peak hoop stress, permits material hybridization, improves fatigue life.
- **Limitation:** assembly complexity, long-term interference loss, slip risk under transient loads.
- **Master contact pressure formula (same material, plane stress):**

$$
p = \frac{\delta\,E\,(r_o^2 - a^2)(a^2 - r_i^2)}{a\left\{\left[(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_o^2\right](a^2 - r_i^2) + \left[(1-\nu)\,a^2 + (1+\nu)\,r_i^2\right](r_o^2 - a^2)\right\}}
$$

- **Quick-look ratios to memorize:** $(a^2 + r_i^2)/(a^2 - r_i^2)$ and $(a^2 + r_o^2)/(r_o^2 - a^2)$ are the "stress concentration factors" relative to the interface pressure p.
- **For KTU numericals:** always tabulate intermediate results, use the formula sheet, and remember to **state the location** (r = ?) of the maximum stress.
- **For KTU derivations:** always begin with Lamé's equations, write all four BCs explicitly, and end with the compatibility equation — examiners reward this structured presentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shrink Fit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Shrink Fit ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shrink Fit (Interference Fit / Press Fit):** A type of mechanical assembly in which a **hub** (outer member) is permanently joined to a **shaft** (inner member) by inducing an *initial radial interference* (δ) between their mating surfaces. The interference is achieved either by **thermal expansion of the hub** (heating it) and subsequent cooling over the shaft, or by **mechanical pressing**. The result is a **contact pressure (p)** at the cylindrical interface that produces a state of **biaxial/axisymmetric self-equilibrated stress** — the hub is held in circumferential (hoop) tension while the shaft is held in hydrostatic compression. Mathematically, the system is analyzed as a pair of **compound (built-up) thick cylinders** in intimate contact, governed by **Lamé's equations** for axisymmetric plane-strain/plane-stress conditions. **Reference Figure Nomenclature (used throughout these notes):** - $a$ = common (nominal) mating radius of shaft and hub inner surface - $b$ = outer radius of the hub - $\delta$ = radial interference (initial overlap) → $\delta \ll a$ - $p$ = radial contact pressure developed at the interface - $E_s, E_h$ = Young's moduli of shaft and hub respectively - $\nu_s, \nu_h$ = Poisson's ratios of shaft and hub - $L$ = axial length of the fitted region - $\mu$ = coefficient of static friction at the interface --- ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cold Mango & The Tight Bottle Lid" Imagine trying to remove a **tight metallic lid** from a glass jar. The lid is slightly *larger* than the jar mouth, but you can still force it on by **heating the lid** (it expands, slides on easily) and then letting it **cool**. As it cools, it tries to shrink back, but the jar is in the way — the lid clamps onto the jar with enormous gripping force. Now scale this up to a **giant steel flywheel** being shrink-fit onto a shaft in a turbine or rolling mill: 1. The hub (lid) is **uniformly heated** to a few hundred degrees Celsius. 2. Its inner diameter grows by thermal expansion $\Delta D = \alpha \cdot D \cdot \Delta T$. 3. It is **slipped over** the cold shaft. 4. As it cools, it **grips** the shaft with a contact pressure $p$ that can easily exceed **100 MPa**. 5. The grip is so strong that **friction** at the interface can transmit enormous **torque and axial load** without keys, splines, or bolts. > [!NOTE] > **Why engineers love shrink fits:** They transmit load uniformly around the circumference (no stress concentration like keyways), are self-centering, and require no fasteners. The downside? They are *non-reversible* without re-machining and demand tight dimensional tolerances. --- ### 1.3 Real-World Engineering Applications | Industry | Application | |---|---| | **Power Generation** | Steam/Gas turbine rotors, generator armatures, flywheels | | **Automotive** | Cylinder liners in engine blocks, gear blanks on shafts | | **Heavy Machinery** | Rolling mill rolls, crusher liners, ship propeller shafts | | **Railways** | Wheel hubs on axles (railway wheelsets) | | **Aerospace** | Rocket motor casings, landing gear assemblies | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Key Concept (Module 3 anchor):** Shrink-fit analysis is a *direct application* of **Lamé's theory for compound cylinders**, which you have already studied in Module 2. The novelty here is the *compatibility condition* that links shaft compression + hub expansion to the *original geometric interference* $\delta$. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — The Six Logical Steps **Step 1 — Recognize the geometry as a compound cylinder system.** The assembly comprises a **solid shaft** of radius $a$ surrounded by a **hollow hub** of inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$. After assembly, the interface pressure $p$ acts radially outward on the shaft and radially inward on the hub. **Step 2 — Identify the loading symmetry.** Loading is *axisymmetric* (rotationally symmetric about the shaft axis), *static* (time-independent), and *equibiaxial* in the radial-circumferential plane. Therefore, **Lamé's equations for thick cylinders** apply directly, and we can use a 2D plane analysis with $\sigma_z = 0$ (plane stress) or $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_r + \sigma_\theta)$ (plane strain) as appropriate. **Step 3 — Solve Lamé's equations separately for shaft and hub.** - **Shaft (solid cylinder under external pressure $p$):** The solid shaft has no hole, so by symmetry, $\sigma_r = \sigma_\theta = -p$ throughout (uniform hydrostatic compression). - **Hub (hollow cylinder under internal pressure $p$, no external pressure):** Apply Lamé's solution: $$\sigma_r = -\frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\left(1 - \frac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$$ $$\sigma_{\theta} = +\frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\left(1 + \frac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Apply the compatibility (geometric) condition.** The initial radial interference $\delta$ must equal the *sum* of: - the *radial compression* of the shaft at $r = a$ (its diameter shrinks), PLUS - the *radial expansion* of the hub's inner surface at $r = a$ (its inner diameter grows). **Step 5 — Solve for the contact pressure $p$.** Substitute the radial displacements from elasticity (Hooke's law integrated) into the compatibility equation, then isolate $p$ in terms of $\delta$, $a$, $b$, $E_s$, $E_h$, $\nu_s$, $\nu_h$. **Step 6 — Compute design quantities.** - Maximum tangential stress in hub (at $r = a$). - Hoop stress in shaft (= $-p$). - Axial load / torque capacity via friction: $T = 2\pi \mu\, p\, a^{2} L$ and $F_{axial} = 2\pi \mu\, p\, a\, L$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" behind each step:** Step 3 uses Lamé because the geometry is axisymmetric and the material is linear-elastic. Step 4 is the *physical glue* of the problem — without it, you have two independent cylinders with no link between them. Step 5 turns a *geometric manufacturing tolerance* ($\delta$) into a *mechanical design parameter* ($p$). --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Location / Notes | |:---:|---|---|---| | 1 | Hoop stress in **solid shaft** (uniform) | $\sigma_{\theta,s} = -p$ | Throughout shaft (compressive) | | 2 | Radial stress in **solid shaft** (uniform) | $\sigma_{r,s} = -p$ | Throughout shaft | | 3 | Hoop stress in **hub at $r=a$** (max tensile) | $\sigma_{\theta,h}^{\max} = p\,\dfrac{b^{2}+a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}$ | Inner surface — design-critical | | 4 | Hoop stress in **hub at $r=b$** (min tensile) | $\sigma_{\theta,h}^{\min} = \dfrac{2p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}$ | Outer surface | | 5 | Radial stress in **hub at $r=a$** | $\sigma_{r,h} = -p$ | Inner surface | | 6 | Radial stress in **hub at $r=b$** | $\sigma_{r,h} = 0$ | Outer surface (free) | | 7 | **Shaft radial displacement** (inward) | $u_s(a) = -\dfrac{p\,a\,(1-\nu_s)}{E_s}$ | Negative = inward | | 8 | **Hub radial displacement** (outward at $r=a$) | $u_h(a) = \dfrac{p\,a}{E_h}\cdot\dfrac{(1-\nu_h)\,a^{2} + (1+\nu_h)\,b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}$ | Positive = outward | | 9 | **Contact pressure** (compatibility solution) | $p = \dfrac{\delta / a}{\dfrac{1-\nu_s}{E_s} + \dfrac{1}{E_h}\cdot\dfrac{(1-\nu_h)\,a^{2} + (1+\nu_h)\,b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}}$ | Core KTU result | | 10 | **Torque transmissible** (friction grip) | $T = 2\pi\,\mu\,p\,a^{2}\,L$ | N·m | | 11 | **Axial force transmissible** | $F = 2\pi\,\mu\,p\,a\,L$ | Newtons | | 12 | **Equivalent (von Mises) stress in shaft** | $\sigma_{eq,s} = \vert p \vert$ (since $\sigma_r = \sigma_\theta = -p$) | Yield check for shaft | | 13 | **Equivalent (von Mises) stress in hub** (at $r=a$, $\sigma_z=0$ plane stress) | $\sigma_{eq,h} = p\,\sqrt{\,1 + 3\!\left(\dfrac{b^{2}+a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\right)^{\!2} - 3\cdot\dfrac{b^{2}+a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\,}$ | Use carefully (principal stress form) | > [!NOTE] > **Table-symbol note:** Absolute-value notation $\vert p \vert$ is used in row 12 to keep the table markup intact. In the derivation below, the negative sign of $p$ is preserved explicitly. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Utility The shrink-fit principle is the backbone of **rotating machinery**. The formulas above let a design engineer answer three critical questions: 1. **What contact pressure will my chosen interference produce?** → Formula 9. 2. **Will the hub yield at the inner surface?** → Check Formula 3 vs. yield strength. 3. **How much torque/axial load can this joint transmit safely?** → Formulas 10 & 11. In railway wheelset design, for example, a $0.4\,\text{mm}$ interference on a $200\,\text{mm}$ diameter axle can produce contact pressures around $120\text{–}180$ MPa — enough to transmit the full braking torque without keys or threads. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation A: Lamé's Stress Equations for the Hub Starting from the **equilibrium equation** in cylindrical coordinates for an axisymmetric body (no body forces, no angular dependence): $$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$ This is a first-order linear ODE in $\sigma_r$. Substituting the general solution form $\sigma_r = A - B/r^{2}$ (standard separation) gives Lamé's solution: $$\sigma_r(r) = A - \frac{B}{r^{2}}, \qquad \sigma_\theta(r) = A + \frac{B}{r^{2}}$$ **Applying boundary conditions for the hub:** - At $r = a$: $\sigma_r = -p$ (hub is pushed inward by shaft, so compressive) - At $r = b$: $\sigma_r = 0$ (outer surface is free) From BC 2: $A - B/b^{2} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; A = B/b^{2}$. From BC 1: $A - B/a^{2} = -p \;\Rightarrow\; A - B/a^{2} = -p$. Substituting $A = B/b^{2}$: $$\frac{B}{b^{2}} - \frac{B}{a^{2}} = -p \;\Rightarrow\; B\!\left(\frac{1}{b^{2}} - \frac{1}{a^{2}}\right) = -p$$ $$B = \frac{-p}{\dfrac{1}{b^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{a^{2}}} = \frac{p\,a^{2}b^{2}}{a^{2} - b^{2}} = -\frac{p\,a^{2}b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$$ Therefore: $$A = \frac{B}{b^{2}} = -\frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}$$ **Final Lamé stress field in the hub:** $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_r(r) &= -\frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\left(1 - \frac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right) \\[4pt] \sigma_\theta(r) &= -\frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\left(1 + \frac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right) \quad \text{(with a sign correction below)} \end{aligned}$$ **Sign-correction check** — Because $B < 0$ (since $b>a$) and $A < 0$: - $\sigma_r = A - B/r^{2}$. At $r = a$: $\sigma_r = A - B/a^{2} = -p$ ✓ - $\sigma_\theta = A + B/r^{2}$. At $r = a$: $\sigma_\theta = A + B/a^{2}$. Let me compute the *corrected* form for hoop stress. Using the derived $A$ and $B$: $$\sigma_\theta(r) = -\frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}} + \left(-\frac{p\,a^{2}b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\right)\cdot\frac{1}{r^{2}} = -\frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\left(1 + \frac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$$ Hmm — but we *know* hoop stress in the hub must be **tensile** (positive). The minus sign is wrong. Let me re-derive carefully. Re-derivation with the sign convention $\sigma_r = -p$ at $r=a$ (compression on the hub's inner wall): Equilibrium: $\dfrac{d(\sigma_r \, r)}{dr} - \sigma_\theta = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sigma_\theta = \sigma_r + r\,d\sigma_r/dr$. Using the standard Lamé pair $A$ and $B$ where $\sigma_r = A - B/r^{2}$ and $\sigma_\theta = A + B/r^{2}$: - BC at $r=b$: $\sigma_r = 0 \Rightarrow A = B/b^{2}$ - BC at $r=a$: $\sigma_r = -p \Rightarrow A - B/a^{2} = -p$ Subtract: $(A - B/b^{2}) - (A - B/a^{2}) = 0 - (-p) = p$ $$-B/b^{2} + B/a^{2} = p \;\Rightarrow\; B\left(\frac{1}{a^{2}} - \frac{1}{b^{2}}\right) = p$$ $$B = \frac{p}{\dfrac{1}{a^{2}} - \dfrac{1}{b^{2}}} = \frac{p\,a^{2}b^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}} \quad \text{(positive, since } b>a\text{)}$$ Then $A = B/b^{2} = p\,a^{2}/(b^{2}-a^{2})$ (positive). **Corrected, final Lamé stress field for the hub:** $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_r(r) &= \frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\left(1 - \frac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right) \\[4pt] \sigma_\theta(r) &= \frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2} - a^{2}}\left(1 + \frac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Verification:** At $r = a$: $\sigma_r = \dfrac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\!\left(1 - b^{2}/a^{2}\right) = \dfrac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{a^{2}-b^{2}}{a^{2}} = -p$ ✓ At $r = b$: $\sigma_r = \dfrac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\!\left(1 - 1\right) = 0$ ✓ At $r = a$: $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\!\left(1 + b^{2}/a^{2}\right) = p\,(b^{2}+a^{2})/(b^{2}-a^{2})$ (tensile, positive) ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Correction note for student:** Earlier I had a sign slip in the preliminary form. The *correct* Lamé equations for an internally-pressurized hub with $\sigma_r(a)=-p$ and $\sigma_r(b)=0$ are the ones above with **positive leading coefficient** in $\sigma_r$, which gives **positive (tensile) hoop stress**. This is the standard textbook form and the one to memorize. --- ### 3.2 Derivation B: Radial Displacements (Elasticity Approach) **Displacement field assumption (axisymmetric, plane-strain-like):** $u_r = u(r)$, $u_\theta = 0$, $u_z = 0$. **Strain–displacement relations:** $$\varepsilon_r = \frac{du}{dr}, \qquad \varepsilon_\theta = \frac{u}{r}$$ **Hooke's law (plane stress, no axial load on the joint):** $$\sigma_r = \frac{E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\varepsilon_r + \nu\,\varepsilon_\theta\right), \qquad \sigma_\theta = \frac{E}{1-\nu^{2}}\left(\varepsilon_\theta + \nu\,\varepsilon_r\right)$$ Substituting strains and solving algebraically (a standard exercise — see any elasticity text, e.g., Timoshenko): **Solid shaft** (with $A_s = -p$, $B_s = 0$, since no hole): $$u_s(r) = \frac{1}{E_s}\left[(1-\nu_s)A_s\,r - (1+\nu_s)\,\frac{B_s}{r}\right] = \frac{-(1-\nu_s)\,p\,r}{E_s}$$ At $r = a$: $$u_s(a) = -\frac{p\,a\,(1-\nu_s)}{E_s}$$ The negative sign confirms the shaft surface moves *inward* (its diameter shrinks). **Hub** (with $A_h = +p\,a^{2}/(b^{2}-a^{2})$ and $B_h = +p\,a^{2}b^{2}/(b^{2}-a^{2})$): $$\begin{aligned} u_h(r) &= \frac{1}{E_h}\left[(1-\nu_h)A_h\,r - (1+\nu_h)\,\frac{B_h}{r}\right] \\ &= \frac{1}{E_h}\left[\frac{(1-\nu_h)\,p\,a^{2}\,r}{b^{2}-a^{2}} - \frac{(1+\nu_h)\,p\,a^{2}b^{2}}{r\,(b^{2}-a^{2})}\right] \\ &= \frac{p\,a^{2}}{E_h\,(b^{2}-a^{2})}\left[(1-\nu_h)r - \frac{(1+\nu_h)b^{2}}{r}\right] \end{aligned}$$ At $r = a$: $$u_h(a) = \frac{p\,a^{2}}{E_h\,(b^{2}-a^{2})}\left[(1-\nu_h)\,a - \frac{(1+\nu_h)\,b^{2}}{a}\right]$$ $$\boxed{u_h(a) = \frac{p\,a}{E_h\,(b^{2}-a^{2})}\left[(1-\nu_h)\,a^{2} + (1+\nu_h)\,b^{2}\right]}$$ (The sign is **positive** because the term in brackets is positive — the hub's inner surface moves *outward*.) --- ### 3.3 Derivation C: Compatibility Equation & Contact Pressure **Physical compatibility condition:** When the hub is shrunk onto the shaft, the *initial* geometric overlap (radial interference) $\delta$ is "absorbed" by: - the *radial compression* of the shaft (magnitude $\vert u_s(a)\vert = p\,a\,(1-\nu_s)/E_s$), AND - the *radial expansion* of the hub's inner surface (magnitude $u_h(a)$). Therefore: $$\delta = \vert u_s(a)\vert + u_h(a)$$ Substituting: $$\delta = \frac{p\,a\,(1-\nu_s)}{E_s} + \frac{p\,a}{E_h\,(b^{2}-a^{2})}\left[(1-\nu_h)\,a^{2} + (1+\nu_h)\,b^{2}\right]$$ Factor out $p\,a$: $$\delta = p\,a\left\{\frac{1-\nu_s}{E_s} + \frac{1}{E_h}\cdot\frac{(1-\nu_h)\,a^{2} + (1+\nu_h)\,b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\right\}$$ **Solving for the contact pressure:** $$\boxed{\,p = \dfrac{\delta / a}{\dfrac{1-\nu_s}{E_s} + \dfrac{1}{E_h}\cdot\dfrac{(1-\nu_h)\,a^{2} + (1+\nu_h)\,b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}}\,}$$ This is the **principal KTU result** for shrink-fit problems. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) > **Problem:** A steel shaft of diameter $200$ mm is to be shrink-fitted into a steel hub of outer diameter $400$ mm. The radial interference is $0.1$ mm. For both members, $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. Determine: > (a) The contact pressure $p$. > (b) The maximum hoop stress in the hub. > (c) The torque that can be transmitted if $\mu = 0.12$ and the fitted length $L = 250$ mm. **Given data:** - $a = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m - $b = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m - $\delta = 0.1$ mm $= 1\times10^{-4}$ m - $E_s = E_h = E = 200$ GPa $= 2\times10^{11}$ Pa - $\nu_s = \nu_h = \nu = 0.3$ - $\mu = 0.12$, $L = 250$ mm $= 0.25$ m **Step 1 — Denominator calculation:** $$\frac{1-\nu}{E} = \frac{1-0.3}{2\times10^{11}} = \frac{0.7}{2\times10^{11}} = 3.5\times10^{-12}\;\text{Pa}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{(1-\nu)\,a^{2} + (1+\nu)\,b^{2}}{E\,(b^{2}-a^{2})} = \frac{0.7\,(0.01) + 1.3\,(0.04)}{2\times10^{11}\,(0.04-0.01)}$$ Numerator: $0.007 + 0.052 = 0.059\;\text{m}^{2}$ Denominator: $2\times10^{11}\times 0.03 = 6\times10^{9}\;\text{Pa·m}^{2}$ $$\Rightarrow \frac{0.059}{6\times10^{9}} = 9.833\times10^{-12}\;\text{Pa}^{-1}$$ Sum in denominator: $(3.5 + 9.833)\times 10^{-12} = 13.333\times 10^{-12}\;\text{Pa}^{-1}$ **Step 2 — Contact pressure:** $$\frac{\delta}{a} = \frac{1\times10^{-4}}{0.1} = 1\times10^{-3}$$ $$p = \frac{1\times10^{-3}}{13.333\times10^{-12}} = 7.5\times10^{7}\;\text{Pa} = 75\;\text{MPa}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum hoop stress in hub (at $r = a$):** $$\sigma_{\theta}^{\max} = p\cdot\frac{b^{2}+a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}} = 75\times\frac{0.04+0.01}{0.04-0.01} = 75\times\frac{0.05}{0.03} = 125\;\text{MPa}$$ **Step 4 — Torque transmissible:** $$T = 2\pi\,\mu\,p\,a^{2}\,L = 2\pi\,(0.12)\,(7.5\times10^{7})\,(0.1)^{2}\,(0.25)$$ $$T = 2\pi \times 0.12 \times 7.5\times10^{7} \times 0.01 \times 0.25$$ $$T = 2\pi \times 22500 = 1.4137\times10^{5}\;\text{N·m} \approx 141.4\;\text{kN·m}$$ **Summary of results:** - $p = 75$ MPa - $\sigma_{\theta,\text{hub,max}} = 125$ MPa (must be less than yield strength of hub material) - $T \approx 141.4$ kN·m --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Shrink-Fit Analysis The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **analytical pipeline** for a complete shrink-fit problem. It maps the logical decision flow from raw input data ($\delta, a, b, E, \nu, \mu, L$) to all required design outputs. ```mermaid flowchart TD start1["START: Raw Inputs"] --> inputData["Geometry and Material Inputs
a b delta E_s E_h nu_s nu_h mu L"] inputData --> step1["Apply Lame Equations to Hub
sigma_r and sigma_theta as f of r"] inputData --> step2["Solid Shaft Stress Field
sigma_r_eq = sigma_theta_eq = minus p"] step1 --> disp1["Compute Hub Inner Displacement
u_h at r equals a"] step2 --> disp2["Compute Shaft Outer Displacement
u_s at r equals a"] disp1 --> compat["Compatibility Equation
delta equals u_h plus abs u_s"] disp2 --> compat compat --> solveP["Solve for Contact Pressure p
p equals delta per a over combined stiffness"] solveP --> stressHub["Compute Hoop Stresses in Hub
max at r equals a min at r equals b"] solveP --> stressShaft["Hoop Stress in Shaft
equals minus p"] solveP --> designCheck{"Yield Check
sigma_max versus sigma_yield"} designCheck --> okCheck["Pass: Proceed to Capacity"] designCheck --> failCheck["Fail: Increase delta or Change Material"] okCheck --> torqueOut["Torque Capacity
T equals 2 pi mu p a squared L"] okCheck --> axialOut["Axial Load Capacity
F equals 2 pi mu p a L"] torqueOut --> finalReport["Design Report"] axialOut --> finalReport finalReport --> endNode["END"] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Stress State Across the Interface ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SHAFT_BLOCK["SOLID SHAFT Region 0 to r equals a"] s_stress["Stress State:
sigma_r equals minus p
sigma_theta equals minus p
sigma_z equals 0 or nu_times_2p
Uniform Hydrostatic Compression"] end subgraph INTERFACE_BLOCK["MATING INTERFACE at r equals a"] iface["Contact Pressure p
Acts radially inward on Hub
Acts radially outward on Shaft
Friction mu_p transmits T and F"] end subgraph HUB_BLOCK["HOLLOW HUB Region a to b"] h_stress_min["At r equals a Inner Surface:
sigma_r equals minus p
sigma_theta equals p times b2 plus a2 over b2 minus a2
MAXIMUM TENSION"] h_grad["Stress Gradient across Hub Wall"] h_stress_max["At r equals b Outer Surface:
sigma_r equals 0
sigma_theta equals 2 p a2 over b2 minus a2
MINIMUM TENSION"] h_stress_min --> h_grad --> h_stress_max end SHAFT_BLOCK --> INTERFACE_BLOCK INTERFACE_BLOCK --> HUB_BLOCK ``` ### 4.3 Compatibility / Equilibrium Flow (Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD q1{"Is the joint carrying axial load or torque?"} --> q1a["Axial load only"] q1 --> q1b["Torque only"] q1 --> q1c["Combined axial plus torque"] q1a --> cap1["Use F equals 2 pi mu p a L"] q1b --> cap2["Use T equals 2 pi mu p a squared L"] q1c --> cap3["Check Combined F over F_allow plus T over T_allow less than 1"] q1a --> design1["Check Hub Yield: sigma_theta_max less than sigma_y"] q1b --> design1 q1c --> design1 design1 --> finalA{"OK?"} finalA --> yesA["Design ACCEPTED"] finalA --> noA["Increase delta or thicker Hub"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram note:** The three Mermaid charts above provide a complete *block-level* functional map of the shrink-fit problem — covering (i) the analytical computation pipeline, (ii) the spatial stress distribution, and (iii) the design verification decision tree. They are drawn using only **alphanumeric node IDs** and **plain uppercase labels** (no markdown formatting inside the labels) to comply with the KTU-Premier-Engine rendering safeguards. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** > > **Q1. Define 'shrink fit'. Mention any two industrial applications where shrink fits are preferred over keyed or splined connections.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Definition (2 marks):* A shrink fit is a permanent mechanical joint between two cylindrical members (a shaft and a hub) in which a *radial interference* is deliberately introduced at the mating interface. This interference is achieved by thermal expansion of the outer member (hub) before assembly; upon cooling, the hub grips the shaft with a radial contact pressure $p$ that generates friction capable of transmitting torque and axial load. *Two industrial applications (1 mark):* 1. **Railway wheel-on-axle assemblies** (wheelsets) — preferred for fatigue resistance and self-centering. 2. **Turbine / generator rotor shafts** — used in power-generation machinery to avoid stress concentrations. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** > > **Q2. In a shrink-fit assembly, why is the maximum tangential (hoop) stress in the hub greater than the contact pressure $p$ at the interface?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** At the inner surface of the hub ($r = a$), Lamé's equation gives: $$\sigma_\theta^{\max} = p\cdot\frac{b^{2}+a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}$$ Since $(b^{2}+a^{2}) > (b^{2}-a^{2})$ for any $a > 0$, the factor $\dfrac{b^{2}+a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}} > 1$. Therefore, the hoop stress at the inner surface is always *amplified* above the contact pressure $p$. Physically, the hub material at $r = a$ must support the entire radial load while simultaneously resisting circumferential stretching — this dual role magnifies the tangential stress. The amplification becomes severe as the hub wall becomes thin ($a \to b$), which is why shrink-fit hubs must always have a *substantial wall thickness*. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 ESE Pattern, 14 Marks]** > > **Question A:** > > A solid steel shaft of diameter $180$ mm is to be shrink-fitted into a steel hub of outer diameter $360$ mm. The radial interference at the interface is $0.08$ mm. For both members: $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa. The coefficient of friction at the interface is $\mu = 0.15$ and the fitted length is $300$ mm. > > **(a)** Derive the expression for the contact pressure $p$ developed at the interface. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Compute the contact pressure, the maximum hoop stress in the hub, and check whether the hub material yields. Also determine the maximum torque that can be safely transmitted by this shrink-fit joint. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a): Derivation of Contact Pressure (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks):** Recognize the system as a compound cylinder pair. State the geometry: shaft radius $a$, hub inner radius $a$, hub outer radius $b$. The interference $\delta$ creates interface pressure $p$ acting radially inward on the hub and radially outward on the shaft. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Lamé's equilibrium to the hub. With $\sigma_r(b) = 0$ and $\sigma_r(a) = -p$, derive: $$\sigma_r(r) = \frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\!\left(1 - \frac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right), \qquad \sigma_\theta(r) = \frac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\!\left(1 + \frac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Use Hooke's law to find the radial displacements: - Shaft: $u_s(a) = -\dfrac{p\,a\,(1-\nu)}{E}$ (inward) - Hub: $u_h(a) = \dfrac{p\,a}{E\,(b^{2}-a^{2})}\!\left[(1-\nu)\,a^{2} + (1+\nu)\,b^{2}\right]$ (outward) **Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply compatibility: $\delta = |u_s(a)| + u_h(a)$. Solve for $p$: $$\boxed{p = \frac{\delta / a}{\dfrac{1-\nu}{E_s} + \dfrac{1}{E_h}\cdot\dfrac{(1-\nu_h)\,a^{2} + (1+\nu_h)\,b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}}}$$ For same-material hub and shaft, $E_s = E_h = E$ and $\nu_s = \nu_h = \nu$: $$p = \frac{\delta\,E\,(b^{2}-a^{2})}{a\left[(1-\nu)(b^{2}-a^{2}) + (1-\nu)a^{2} + (1+\nu)b^{2}\right]}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b): Numerical Evaluation (7 marks) **Given data:** - $a = 90$ mm $= 0.09$ m; $b = 180$ mm $= 0.18$ m - $\delta = 0.08$ mm $= 8\times10^{-5}$ m - $E = 210$ GPa $= 2.1\times10^{11}$ Pa; $\nu = 0.3$ - $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa; $\mu = 0.15$; $L = 300$ mm $= 0.30$ m **Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute contact pressure. $$\frac{1-\nu}{E} = \frac{0.7}{2.1\times10^{11}} = 3.333\times10^{-12}\;\text{Pa}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{(1-\nu)a^{2} + (1+\nu)b^{2}}{E(b^{2}-a^{2})} = \frac{0.7(0.0081) + 1.3(0.0324)}{2.1\times10^{11}(0.0324-0.0081)} = \frac{0.00567 + 0.04212}{2.1\times10^{11}\times 0.0243}$$ $$= \frac{0.04779}{5.103\times10^{9}} = 9.365\times10^{-12}\;\text{Pa}^{-1}$$ Denominator total: $(3.333 + 9.365)\times10^{-12} = 12.698\times10^{-12}\;\text{Pa}^{-1}$ $\delta/a = 8\times10^{-5}/0.09 = 8.889\times10^{-4}$ $$p = \frac{8.889\times10^{-4}}{12.698\times10^{-12}} = 7.0\times10^{7}\;\text{Pa} = 70\;\text{MPa}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Maximum hoop stress in hub (at $r = a$): $$\sigma_\theta^{\max} = 70\cdot\frac{b^{2}+a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}} = 70\cdot\frac{0.0324+0.0081}{0.0324-0.0081} = 70\cdot\frac{0.0405}{0.0243} = 70\times 1.667 = 116.7\;\text{MPa}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Yield check. $\sigma_\theta^{\max} = 116.7$ MPa $< \sigma_y = 320$ MPa → **SAFE (no yielding).** Factor of safety $\approx 320/116.7 = 2.74$. **Step 4 (2 marks):** Transmissible torque. $$T = 2\pi\,\mu\,p\,a^{2}\,L = 2\pi(0.15)(7.0\times10^{7})(0.09)^{2}(0.30)$$ $$T = 2\pi \times 0.15 \times 7.0\times10^{7} \times 0.0081 \times 0.30 = 2\pi \times 25515 = 1.603\times10^{5}\;\text{N·m}$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 160.3\;\text{kN·m}}$$ --- > **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 ESE Pattern, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]** > > **Question B:** > > **(a)** A solid steel shaft of diameter $d = 150$ mm carries a hub whose outer diameter is $D = 300$ mm. The radial interference is $0.06$ mm. For steel, $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. Find the contact pressure and the tangential stresses at the inner and outer surfaces of the hub. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With the same materials and geometry, by what percentage does the contact pressure change if the radial interference is doubled? If the hub's outer diameter is increased to $400$ mm while the interference and shaft diameter are kept constant, what is the new contact pressure? Comment on design implications. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks):** Identify $a = 0.075$ m, $b = 0.15$ m, $\delta = 6\times10^{-5}$ m. **Step 2 (3 marks):** Plug into the contact-pressure formula (same-material case): $$p = \frac{\delta / a}{\dfrac{1-\nu}{E} + \dfrac{1}{E}\cdot\dfrac{(1-\nu)a^{2} + (1+\nu)b^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}}$$ Denominator: $\dfrac{1}{E}\!\left[0.7 + \dfrac{0.7(0.005625) + 1.3(0.0225)}{0.0225-0.005625}\right] = \dfrac{1}{E}\!\left[0.7 + \dfrac{0.00394 + 0.02925}{0.016875}\right]$ $= \dfrac{1}{E}\!\left[0.7 + 1.964\right] = \dfrac{2.664}{2\times10^{11}} = 1.332\times10^{-11}\;\text{Pa}^{-1}$ $\delta/a = 6\times10^{-5}/0.075 = 8\times10^{-4}$ $$p = \frac{8\times10^{-4}}{1.332\times10^{-11}} = 6.006\times10^{7}\;\text{Pa} \approx 60.06\;\text{MPa}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Hub stresses. $$\sigma_\theta^{\max} = p\cdot\frac{b^{2}+a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}} = 60.06\cdot\frac{0.0225+0.005625}{0.0225-0.005625} = 60.06\times 1.667 = 100.1\;\text{MPa (tensile)}$$ $$\sigma_\theta^{\min} = \frac{2p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}} = \frac{2\times60.06\times0.005625}{0.016875} = 40.04\;\text{MPa (tensile)}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1 (2 marks):** Doubling interference: since $p \propto \delta$ (linear in numerator, no other $\delta$ dependence), doubling $\delta$ **doubles $p$**: $$p_{\text{new}} = 2 \times 60.06 = 120.12\;\text{MPa (an increase of 100%)}$$ **Step 2 (3 marks):** Increasing hub outer diameter to $D = 400$ mm $\Rightarrow b = 0.20$ m, while $\delta = 6\times10^{-5}$ m and $a = 0.075$ m are unchanged. New denominator: $\dfrac{1}{E}\!\left[0.7 + \dfrac{0.7(0.005625) + 1.3(0.04)}{0.04-0.005625}\right] = \dfrac{1}{E}\!\left[0.7 + \dfrac{0.00394 + 0.052}{0.034375}\right]$ $= \dfrac{1}{E}\!\left[0.7 + 1.628\right] = \dfrac{2.328}{2\times10^{11}} = 1.164\times10^{-11}\;\text{Pa}^{-1}$ $$p = \frac{8\times10^{-4}}{1.164\times10^{-11}} = 6.873\times10^{7}\;\text{Pa} \approx 68.73\;\text{MPa}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Design comment. - The contact pressure rose by only **~14.4%** despite a 33% increase in hub outer diameter (from 300 to 400 mm). - This shows that **increasing hub wall thickness has *diminishing returns*** on contact pressure beyond a certain point. Most of the stiffness benefit comes from the *inner* layers of the hub; the outer layers are mechanically less engaged. - However, thicker hubs *do* lower the **maximum hoop stress** (because $b/a$ grows), so there is a separate yield-strength benefit. - **Practical rule of thumb:** Use a hub outer-to-inner diameter ratio $D/d \geq 2$ for a good balance between contact pressure amplification and stress relief. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:** > > 1. **Sign error in Lamé's equations** — students often write $\sigma_r = -\dfrac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}(\ldots)$ and end up with *compressive* hoop stress in the hub. The correct form gives **tensile** hoop stress. Watch the boundary conditions! > > 2. **Forgetting Poisson's ratio in the shaft** — the term $(1-\nu_s)$ in the shaft radial displacement is frequently dropped, leading to an **inflated** contact pressure (under-estimation of system compliance). > > 3. **Confusing "radial interference" $\delta$ with "diametral interference"** — they differ by a factor of 2. KTU problems always quote *radial* interference unless explicitly stated. > > 4. **Mixing units** — the worst culprit! Always convert $\delta$ and $a, b$ to the *same* length unit (preferably metres) before substituting into the formula, and express $E$ in Pa. A 1 mm vs 0.001 m slip will cost you the entire 14 marks. > > 5. **Failing to check yield** — the question "compute the contact pressure" is rarely the *end* of the problem. Always follow up with a yield check $\sigma_\theta^{\max} \leq \sigma_y$ to score full marks. > > 6. **Treating the shaft as a hollow cylinder** — the shaft is *solid*; therefore $\sigma_r = \sigma_\theta = -p$ *uniformly*, NOT varying with $r$. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shrink Fit** > > - [x] **Shrink fit = permanent compound cylinder assembly** with deliberate initial *radial interference* $\delta$. > - [x] The interface develops a **contact pressure $p$** that creates a self-equilibrated stress field: shaft in **compression**, hub in **tension**. > - [x] **Lamé's equations** are the foundation: derived from axisymmetric equilibrium $\dfrac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \dfrac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$. > - [x] **Boundary conditions for the hub:** $\sigma_r(a) = -p$ (inner) and $\sigma_r(b) = 0$ (outer). > - [x] **Hub stress formulas** (memorize these!): > - $\sigma_r(r) = \dfrac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$ > - $\sigma_\theta(r) = \dfrac{p\,a^{2}}{b^{2}-a^{2}}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{b^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$ > - $\sigma_\theta^{\max}$ at $r = a$ = $p\,(b^{2}+a^{2})/(b^{2}-a^{2})$ > - $\sigma_\theta^{\min}$ at $r = b$ = $2p\,a^{2}/(b^{2}-a^{2})$ > - [x] **Solid shaft** has $\sigma_r = \sigma_\theta = -p$ *uniformly* — no Lamé needed; it's a simple hydrostatic state. > - [x] **Compatibility condition** is the heart of the problem: $\delta = |u_s(a)| + u_h(a)$. > - [x] **Final contact pressure formula** (same material, both $\nu$ equal): > > $$p = \frac{\delta\,E\,(b^{2}-a^{2})}{a\!\left[(1-\nu)(b^{2}-a^{2}) + (1-\nu)a^{2} + (1+\nu)b^{2}\right]}$$ > - [x] For *different* materials (shaft $E_s, \nu_s$ ≠ hub $E_h, \nu_h$), the generalized form from Section 3.3 must be used. > - [x] **Torque transmission:** $T = 2\pi\,\mu\,p\,a^{2}\,L$ — derived from friction on a cylindrical surface of area $2\pi a L$ acting at radius $a$. > - [x] **Axial load transmission:** $F = 2\pi\,\mu\,p\,a\,L$. > - [x] **Yield check** is mandatory for both members: > - Shaft: equivalent stress $\sigma_{eq} = \vert p \vert$ (since it's a 2D equal-biaxial compression). > - Hub: maximum $\sigma_\theta$ at $r = a$ compared with $\sigma_y$ (use a suitable yield criterion; von Mises in plane stress gives the most realistic check). > - [x] **Linear superposition** holds: doubling $\delta$ doubles $p$. The problem is linear-elastic. > - [x] **Real-world tolerance:** typical interferences for steel-on-steel shrink fits range from $0.05$ mm (small shafts) to $0.5$ mm (large flywheels), with diametral fits specified on engineering drawings. > - [x] **Hub-to-shaft diameter ratio $D/d$** is the key design lever: aim for $D/d \geq 2$ to keep $\sigma_\theta^{\max}$ manageable. ---

topics.title: Lamina and Laminates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lamina and Laminates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Lamina?

A **lamina** (also called a **ply** or **single layer**) is the fundamental building block of a fiber-reinforced composite material. It is a flat arrangement of unidirectional (or woven) fibers embedded in a single matrix material, where all fibers are aligned in a single reference direction known as the **fiber direction** (denoted as the **1-direction** or **longitudinal direction**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A lamina is a *flat (or curved) arrangement of unidirectional or woven fibers in a matrix* such that the fiber orientation within a single layer is uniform. Its thickness is typically **0.1 mm to 0.5 mm** for aerospace-grade prepreg plies, and the fiber volume fraction $V_f$ generally lies between **0.45 and 0.65**.

### 1.1.1 Constituent Materials of a Lamina

A lamina is composed of two principal phases:

| Phase | Function | Common Examples | Typical Volume Fraction $V_f$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Fiber (Reinforcement)** | Carries the load, provides stiffness and strength | Carbon, Glass (E-glass, S-glass), Aramid (Kevlar), Boron | 0.45 – 0.65 |
| **Matrix** | Binds fibers, transfers load, protects fibers, maintains fiber position | Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester, PEEK, Bismaleimide (BMI) | 0.35 – 0.55 |
| **Interface / Interphase** | Bonding region (often a sizing/chemical treatment) | Silane coupling agents | Trace |

The **void content** (trapped air bubbles) should ideally be less than **1%** in aerospace laminates and less than **2–3%** in commercial laminates, as voids drastically reduce matrix-dominated properties such as transverse strength and shear strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fiber vs. Matrix Analogy:** Think of reinforced concrete — the steel rebar (fiber) carries tensile load, while the concrete (matrix) carries compressive load and holds the rebar in place. In a composite lamina, the fiber carries the *axial* load, and the matrix carries the *transverse and shear* load.

### 1.1.2 Macromechanics vs. Micromechanics

- **Micromechanics:** Studies the *constituent-level* behavior (fiber + matrix interactions) to predict lamina properties.
- **Macromechanics:** Treats the lamina as a *homogeneous, anisotropic* continuum and predicts laminate behavior from lamina properties.

The **rule of mixtures** (a micromechanics result) gives the *longitudinal Young's modulus* as:

$$E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

where $E_f$ is the fiber modulus, $E_m$ is the matrix modulus, $V_f$ is the fiber volume fraction, and $V_m = 1 - V_f$ is the matrix volume fraction.

---

## 1.2 What is a Laminate?

A **laminate** is a stack of two or more laminae (plies) bonded together, with each ply potentially having a different fiber orientation, thickness, and material. The stacking sequence is denoted by listing the ply angles from the bottom to the top, e.g., $[0°/45°/-45°/90°]_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A laminate is a bonded stack of laminae whose *integrated structural response* is governed by the **Classical Lamination Theory (CLT)**. The total laminate response is captured by three matrices: $[A]$ (extensional stiffness), $[B]$ (coupling stiffness), and $[D]$ (bending stiffness).

### 1.2.1 Common Laminate Classifications

| Classification | Stacking Sequence | Characteristics |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| **Unidirectional Laminate** | $[0°]_n$ | All fibers in one direction; maximum stiffness in 1-direction |
| **Cross-Ply Laminate** | $[0°/90°_n]_s$ | Alternating 0° and 90° plies |
| **Angle-Ply Laminate** | $[\pm\theta]_s$ | Symmetric stacking of $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies |
| **Quasi-Isotropic Laminate** | $[0°/60°/-60°]_s$ or $[0°/90°/\pm 45°]_s$ | In-plane response is isotropic in the laminate plane |
| **Symmetric Laminate** | $[\cdots]_s$ | $B_{ij} = 0$; no bending–extension coupling |
| **Antisymmetric Laminate** | $[\cdots/\bar{\cdots}]$ | $B_{ij} \neq 0$; bending–extension coupling exists |
| **Balanced Laminate** | Equal $+\theta$ and $-\theta$ plies | $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ |
| **Asymmetric Laminate** | No mid-plane symmetry | Bending–twisting and bending–extension coupling |

### 1.2.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plywood Analogy:** A *single sheet of paper* is weak and tears easily — this is a **lamina**. If you glue multiple sheets of paper together with the grain running in different directions, you get **plywood** — a *laminate*. Just like plywood is far stronger and more dimensionally stable than a single sheet, a laminate is far stiffer, stronger, and more tailorable than a single ply. The orientation of each "ply" (paper sheet) controls the overall stiffness, much like the stacking sequence $[0°/45°/-45°/90°]$ controls a composite laminate.

### 1.2.3 Why Use Laminates?

- **Tailorability of stiffness and strength** by changing the stacking sequence.
- **Reduction of coupling effects** (use of symmetric layups).
- **Design of quasi-isotropic laminates** that behave like metals in the plane.
- **Damage tolerance:** Cracks in one ply are arrested by differently oriented plies.
- **Weight savings** in aerospace (e.g., Boeing 787, Airbus A350 — over 50% composite by mass).
- **Corrosion resistance** for marine and chemical applications.

---

## 1.3 Coordinate System Convention

The **lamina coordinate system** (1–2) is aligned with the fibers:

- **1-axis:** Longitudinal (along the fiber direction)
- **2-axis:** Transverse (perpendicular to the fiber, in the plane of the ply)
- **3-axis:** Through-thickness (perpendicular to the ply, in the $1$–$2$ plane normal)

The **laminate (or global) coordinate system** (x–y) is a fixed reference frame attached to the laminate's geometric mid-plane, with **x along the 0° reference direction** and **z normal to the mid-plane**.

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the *lamina* (1–2) coordinates and the *laminate* (x–y) coordinates. Always remember: **1-axis = fiber direction**, and the rotation angle $\theta$ is measured from the **x-axis to the 1-axis** (counter-clockwise positive).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Orthotropic Lamina: Stress–Strain Relations

A unidirectional lamina is modeled as a **transversely isotropic** or **orthotropic** material. Under **plane stress** (the standard assumption for thin laminates where $\sigma_{33} = \sigma_{13} = \sigma_{23} = 0$), the stress–strain relationship in the lamina (1–2) coordinates is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

This is written compactly as $\{\sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}$, where $[Q]$ is the **reduced stiffness matrix**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why plane stress?** The thickness of each ply is very small (~0.1–0.5 mm) compared to the in-plane dimensions. Therefore, the through-thickness stress $\sigma_3$ and the transverse shear stresses $\tau_{13}$, $\tau_{23}$ are assumed negligible — a direct consequence of the **plane stress assumption** in 2D Classical Lamination Theory.

### 2.1.1 Reduced Stiffness Coefficients $Q_{ij}$

The four independent components of $[Q]$ are expressed in terms of **four engineering constants**: $E_1$, $E_2$, $\nu_{12}$, $G_{12}$.

Define the **compliance constant** $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ (reciprocity relation).

$$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$$

$$Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$$

$$Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{\nu_{21} E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$$

$$Q_{66} = G_{12}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix $[Q]$ is **symmetric** ($Q_{ij} = Q_{ji}$) by Maxwell's reciprocity, and the **in-plane shear coupling terms** $Q_{16} = Q_{26} = 0$ for a *unidirectional* lamina in its principal material coordinates.

### 2.1.2 Compliance Matrix $[S]$ (Inverse of $[Q]$)

The compliance matrix relates strains to stresses: $\{\varepsilon\} = [S]\{\sigma\}$.

$$[S] = \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} & 0 \\ S_{12} & S_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & S_{66} \end{bmatrix}$$

where

$$S_{11} = \frac{1}{E_1}, \quad S_{22} = \frac{1}{E_2}, \quad S_{12} = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = -\frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}, \quad S_{66} = \frac{1}{G_{12}}$$

---

## 2.2 Transformed Reduced Stiffness $[\bar{Q}]$

When the fiber direction (1-axis) is rotated by an angle $\theta$ relative to the laminate x-axis, the stiffness matrix in the x–y frame becomes **fully populated** (i.e., $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26} \neq 0$). The transformed matrix $[\bar{Q}]$ is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \bar{Q}_{11} \\ \bar{Q}_{22} \\ \bar{Q}_{12} \\ \bar{Q}_{66} \\ \bar{Q}_{16} \\ \bar{Q}_{26} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} m^4 & n^4 & 2m^2 n^2 & 4m^2 n^2 & 4m^3 n & 4mn^3 \\ n^4 & m^4 & 2m^2 n^2 & 4m^2 n^2 & -4mn^3 & -4m^3 n \\ m^2 n^2 & m^2 n^2 & m^4 + n^4 & -4m^2 n^2 & 2(m^3 n - mn^3) & 2(mn^3 - m^3 n) \\ m^2 n^2 & m^2 n^2 & -2m^2 n^2 & (m^2 - n^2)^2 & -2mn(m^2 - n^2) & 2mn(m^2 - n^2) \\ m^3 n & -mn^3 & m n^3 - m^3 n & -2mn(m^2 - n^2) & m^4 - 3m^2 n^2 & m^2 n^2 - n^4 + 3m^2 n^2 \\ m n^3 & -m^3 n & m^3 n - m n^3 & 2mn(m^2 - n^2) & m^2 n^2 - n^4 + 3m^2 n^2 & n^4 - 3m^2 n^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} \\ Q_{22} \\ Q_{12} \\ Q_{66} \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $m = \cos\theta$ and $n = \sin\theta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative Compact Form (Stress Transformation):** The transformed stiffness can be written as $[\bar{Q}] = [T_{\sigma}]\,[Q]\,[T_{\sigma}]^T$, where $[T_{\sigma}]$ is the **Bond matrix** for stress transformation. The explicit equations are tabulated below.

### 2.2.1 Compact Form of $[\bar{Q}]$ Components

$$\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{11} m^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{22} n^4$$

$$\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11} n^4 + 2(Q_{12} + 2Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{22} m^4$$

$$\bar{Q}_{12} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 4Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{12}(m^4 + n^4)$$

$$\bar{Q}_{66} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22} - 2Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) m^2 n^2 + Q_{66}(m^4 + n^4)$$

$$\bar{Q}_{16} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) m^3 n + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66}) m n^3$$

$$\bar{Q}_{26} = (Q_{11} - Q_{12} - 2Q_{66}) m n^3 + (Q_{12} - Q_{22} + 2Q_{66}) m^3 n$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** When $\theta = 0°$ (fibers along x-axis), $m=1$, $n=0$, and $[\bar{Q}] = [Q]$ (identity). When $\theta = 90°$ (fibers along y-axis), $m=0$, $n=1$, and $\bar{Q}_{11} \to Q_{22}$, $\bar{Q}_{22} \to Q_{11}$, etc. (swap of 1↔2 axes).

---

## 2.3 Classical Lamination Theory (CLT): The ABD Matrices

The cornerstone of laminate analysis. CLT assumes:
1. **Straight, normal, inextensible lines** (Kirchhoff–Love hypothesis) — transverse normals remain straight, normal to the mid-plane, and of constant length.
2. **No transverse shear deformation** (this is later relaxed in *First-Order Shear Deformation Theory*, FSDT).
3. **Plane stress** in each ply.

The **resultant forces** $\{N\}$ and **resultant moments** $\{M\}$ are related to the **mid-plane strains** $\{\varepsilon^0\}$ and **mid-plane curvatures** $\{\kappa\}$ via:

$$\begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{bmatrix}$$

### 2.3.1 The A, B, D Matrices

$$A_{ij} = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \, dz = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k - z_{k-1})$$

$$B_{ij} = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \, z \, dz = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$$

$$D_{ij} = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \, z^2 \, dz = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$$

Here $z_k$ and $z_{k-1}$ are the through-thickness coordinates of the top and bottom of the $k$-th ply, measured from the geometric mid-plane.

| Matrix | Units | Physical Meaning | Size |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $[A]$ | N/m | Extensional stiffness — relates in-plane forces to mid-plane strains | $3 \times 3$ |
| $[B]$ | N | Coupling stiffness — couples extension and bending | $3 \times 3$ |
| $[D]$ | N·m | Bending stiffness — relates moments to curvatures | $3 \times 3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties:**
> - For a **symmetric laminate**, $[B] = [0]$ — no coupling between extension and bending.
> - For a **balanced laminate**, $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ — no extension–shear coupling.
> - For a **symmetric and balanced** laminate, $D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$ as well.
> - The matrices $[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$ are all **symmetric**: $A_{ij} = A_{ji}$, etc.

### 2.3.2 Inverse Relation: Compliances

$$\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b^T & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix}$$

where $[a] = [A^*]$, $[b] = -[A^*][B][D^*]^{-1}$, $[d] = [D^*]^{-1} + [D^*]^{-1}[B][A^*][B][D^*]^{-1}$, and the asterisks denote *Schur complements*.

---

## 2.4 Failure Theories for a Lamina

Once the lamina stresses $(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12})$ are known, a *failure criterion* is applied in the *lamina (1–2) coordinate system*.

### 2.4.1 Maximum Stress Theory (MST)

The lamina fails when **any** principal stress exceeds its corresponding strength:

$$-X_C < \sigma_1 < X_T, \quad -Y_C < \sigma_2 < Y_T, \quad \vert \tau_{12} \vert < S$$

where $X_T, X_C$ = longitudinal tensile/compressive strength, $Y_T, Y_C$ = transverse tensile/compressive strength, $S$ = in-plane shear strength.

### 2.4.2 Maximum Strain Theory (MStrT)

$$\varepsilon_{1,ult,T/C} = \frac{X_{T/C}}{E_1}, \quad \varepsilon_{2,ult,T/C} = \frac{Y_{T/C}}{E_2}, \quad \gamma_{12,ult} = \frac{S}{G_{12}}$$

### 2.4.3 Tsai–Hill Theory (Interactive, Quadratic)

A phenomenological extension of the *von Mises* criterion to anisotropic materials:

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X}\right)^2 - \frac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}{X^2} + \left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> For combined tension/compression, $X$ and $Y$ are chosen based on the **sign** of the corresponding stress.

### 2.4.4 Tsai–Wu Failure Criterion (Most General, KTU-Favorite)

$$F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11} \sigma_1^2 + F_{22} \sigma_2^2 + F_{66} \tau_{12}^2 + 2 F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2 = 1$$

where the **strength tensors** are:

$$F_1 = \frac{1}{X_T} - \frac{1}{X_C}, \quad F_2 = \frac{1}{Y_T} - \frac{1}{Y_C}$$

$$F_{11} = \frac{1}{X_T X_C}, \quad F_{22} = \frac{1}{Y_T Y_C}, \quad F_{66} = \frac{1}{S^2}$$

$$F_{12} = -\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{F_{11} F_{22}} \quad \text{(often used as a conservative estimate)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Tsai–Wu interaction term** $F_{12}$ is **not** uniquely determined from uniaxial tests. It is often estimated from biaxial experiments (e.g., $\pm 45°$ tube burst tests). A common conservative value is $F_{12} = -\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{F_{11} F_{22}}$.

The **Strength Ratio** $R$ is defined by setting the loading as $(R\sigma_1, R\sigma_2, R\tau_{12})$ and solving for the largest $R$ such that the Tsai–Wu surface is reached. Failure is predicted when $R \le 1$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures (longitudinal modulus) | Pa |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{E_2} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse modulus) | Pa |
| 3 | $\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Major Poisson's ratio | – |
| 4 | $Q_{11} = \dfrac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Reduced stiffness $Q_{11}$ | Pa |
| 5 | $Q_{22} = \dfrac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Reduced stiffness $Q_{22}$ | Pa |
| 6 | $Q_{12} = \dfrac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | Reduced stiffness $Q_{12}$ | Pa |
| 7 | $Q_{66} = G_{12}$ | In-plane shear stiffness | Pa |
| 8 | $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ | Reciprocity relation | – |
| 9 | $[\bar{Q}] = [T_\sigma][Q][T_\sigma]^T$ | Stiffness transformation to off-axis frame | Pa |
| 10 | $A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k - z_{k-1})$ | Extensional stiffness | N/m |
| 11 | $B_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$ | Coupling stiffness | N |
| 12 | $D_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{3} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$ | Bending stiffness | N·m |
| 13 | $\begin{bmatrix} N \\ M \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon^0 \\ \kappa \end{bmatrix}$ | CLT resultant force–moment equation | mixed |
| 14 | $F_1 \sigma_1 + F_2 \sigma_2 + F_{11} \sigma_1^2 + F_{22} \sigma_2^2 + F_{66} \tau_{12}^2 + 2F_{12} \sigma_1 \sigma_2 = 1$ | Tsai–Wu failure criterion | – |
| 15 | $\left(\dfrac{\sigma_1}{X}\right)^2 - \dfrac{\sigma_1\sigma_2}{X^2} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_2}{Y}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 = 1$ | Tsai–Hill failure criterion | – |
| 16 | $R = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\text{quadratic in } (\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12})}}$ | Strength ratio (Tsai–Wu) | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In CLT problems, always compute the **z-coordinates of ply interfaces** relative to the laminate geometric mid-plane, *not* relative to the bottom surface. This is the #1 source of mistakes.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (Boeing 787, Airbus A350, F-22 Raptor):** Quasi-isotropic carbon/epoxy laminates for fuselage skin, wing skins, and tail structures. ~50% weight savings over aluminum.
- **Automotive (BMW i3, i8; Tesla Model S battery enclosure):** CFRP and GFRP laminates for crash structures and battery housings.
- **Wind turbine blades (up to 100 m long):** GFRP laminates with $[0°/\pm 45°/90°]$ layups to balance stiffness and buckling resistance.
- **Sports equipment (tennis rackets, Formula 1 monocoque, hockey sticks):** Tailored CFRP laminates.
- **Civil infrastructure:** CFRP laminates retrofitted on beams and columns for seismic strengthening.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reduced Stiffness Matrix $[Q]$ from Engineering Constants

We begin with the **3D generalized Hooke's law** for an orthotropic material, then specialize to *plane stress* ($\sigma_3 = \tau_{13} = \tau_{23} = 0$).

### Step 1 — Write the 3D Compliance Form

For an orthotropic material, the compliance relations are:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} \sigma_2 - \frac{\nu_{31}}{E_3} \sigma_3$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} \sigma_1 + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} - \frac{\nu_{32}}{E_3} \sigma_3$$

$$\varepsilon_3 = -\frac{\nu_{13}}{E_1} \sigma_1 - \frac{\nu_{23}}{E_2} \sigma_2 + \frac{\sigma_3}{E_3}$$

$$\gamma_{23} = \frac{\tau_{23}}{G_{23}}, \quad \gamma_{13} = \frac{\tau_{13}}{G_{13}}, \quad \gamma_{12} = \frac{\tau_{12}}{G_{12}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Plane Stress Assumption

Setting $\sigma_3 = \tau_{13} = \tau_{23} = 0$, the relations simplify to:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} \sigma_2$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} \sigma_1 + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2}$$

$$\gamma_{12} = \frac{\tau_{12}}{G_{12}}$$

### Step 3 — Express the Compliance Matrix $[S]$

Written in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{E_1} & -\dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} & 0 \\ -\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} & \dfrac{1}{E_2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{12}} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

Using the **Maxwell–Betti reciprocity** $\nu_{12}/E_1 = \nu_{21}/E_2$, the matrix is symmetric.

### Step 4 — Invert the Compliance Matrix to Get $[Q]$

Inverting the $2\times 2$ block:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{E_2} & \dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} \\ \dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} & \dfrac{1}{E_1} \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \quad \text{where } \Delta = \frac{1}{E_1 E_2} - \frac{\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}{E_1 E_2} = \frac{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}{E_1 E_2}$$

The inverse of the $2\times 2$ block is:

$$\frac{1}{\det(S)} \begin{bmatrix} S_{22} & -S_{12} \\ -S_{12} & S_{11} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{E_1 E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{E_2} & \dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} \\ \dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} & \dfrac{1}{E_1} \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$$

$$\boxed{[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Transformation of Reduced Stiffness $[\bar{Q}]$

### Step 1 — Stress Transformation

When the 1-axis is rotated by $\theta$ counter-clockwise from the x-axis, the stresses transform via the **Bond stress transformation matrix** $[T_\sigma]$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{bmatrix} = [T_\sigma] \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$[T_\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} m^2 & n^2 & 2mn \\ n^2 & m^2 & -2mn \\ -mn & mn & m^2 - n^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $m = \cos\theta$, $n = \sin\theta$.

### Step 2 — Strain Transformation

The strains transform via the **Bond strain transformation matrix** $[T_\varepsilon]$:

$$[T_\varepsilon] = \begin{bmatrix} m^2 & n^2 & mn \\ n^2 & m^2 & -mn \\ -2mn & 2mn & m^2 - n^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Note the factor of 2 on the shear terms: this is the standard *tensor-to-engineering-shear* convention.

### Step 3 — Relate $[\bar{Q}]$ to $[Q]$

We have $\{\sigma\}_1 = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}_1$ in lamina coordinates. Substituting the transformation equations:

$$[T_\sigma]\{\sigma\}_x = [Q][T_\varepsilon]\{\varepsilon\}_x$$

Solving for $\{\sigma\}_x$:

$$\{\sigma\}_x = [T_\sigma]^{-1}[Q][T_\varepsilon]\{\varepsilon\}_x \equiv [\bar{Q}]\{\varepsilon\}_x$$

Since $[T_\sigma]^{-1} = [T_\sigma]^T$ (orthogonal matrix for rotation) and using the relation $[T_\varepsilon] = [R][T_\sigma][R]^{-1}$ where $[R] = \text{diag}(1,1,2)$, we obtain the elegant result:

$$\boxed{[\bar{Q}] = [T_\sigma]^{-1} [Q] [T_\sigma]^{-T} = [T_\sigma]^T [Q] [T_\sigma]^{-1 \cdot T} \quad \text{(after manipulation)}}$$

Or more simply, since the inverse of $[T_\sigma]$ is its transpose *with a sign change on the shear row*:

$$[\bar{Q}] = [T_\sigma]^{-T} [Q] [T_\sigma]^{-1}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Compute $[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$ for a $[0°/90°]_s$ Laminate

### Given

A **4-ply cross-ply laminate** $[0°/90°/90°/0°]$ is made of T300/5208 carbon/epoxy with the following properties:

$$E_1 = 132.5 \text{ GPa}, \quad E_2 = 10.8 \text{ GPa}, \quad \nu_{12} = 0.24, \quad G_{12} = 5.7 \text{ GPa}$$

Each ply thickness $t = 0.125$ mm. Total laminate thickness $h = 4t = 0.5$ mm.

### Step 1 — Compute $\nu_{21}$ and $[Q]$

$$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.24 \times \frac{10.8}{132.5} = 0.01957$$

$$1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - 0.24 \times 0.01957 = 1 - 0.004696 = 0.9953$$

$$Q_{11} = \frac{132.5 \times 10^9}{0.9953} = 133.12 \text{ GPa}$$

$$Q_{22} = \frac{10.8 \times 10^9}{0.9953} = 10.851 \text{ GPa}$$

$$Q_{12} = \frac{0.24 \times 10.8 \times 10^9}{0.9953} = 2.604 \text{ GPa}$$

$$Q_{66} = 5.7 \text{ GPa}$$

### Step 2 — Compute $[\bar{Q}]$ for $\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 90°$

For $\theta = 0°$: $[\bar{Q}^{(0°)}] = [Q]$.

For $\theta = 90°$ ($m=0$, $n=1$): from the compact formulas,

$$\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{22} = 10.851 \text{ GPa}, \quad \bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11} = 133.12 \text{ GPa}, \quad \bar{Q}_{12} = Q_{12} = 2.604 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{66} = Q_{66} = 5.7 \text{ GPa}, \quad \bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Determine Ply z-Coordinates (from Geometric Mid-Plane)

For $[0°/90°/90°/0°]$ with $t = 0.125$ mm and mid-plane at $z = 0$:

| Ply (k) | Material | $z_{k-1}$ (mm) | $z_k$ (mm) | Thickness $(z_k - z_{k-1})$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0° | $-0.25$ | $-0.125$ | $0.125$ |
| 2 | 90° | $-0.125$ | $0$ | $0.125$ |
| 3 | 90° | $0$ | $0.125$ | $0.125$ |
| 4 | 0° | $0.125$ | $0.25$ | $0.125$ |

### Step 4 — Compute $[A]$ Matrix

$$A_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{4} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k - z_{k-1})$$

For a **symmetric** cross-ply, the 0° and 90° contributions are equal in magnitude. Note that $A_{11}$ combines $Q_{11}$ (from 0° plies) and $Q_{22}$ (from 90° plies):

$$A_{11} = (Q_{11} + Q_{22}) \times 2t = (133.12 + 10.851) \times 10^9 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} = 35.99 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

$$A_{22} = (Q_{22} + Q_{11}) \times 2t = 35.99 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

$$A_{12} = 2 Q_{12} \times 2t = 2 \times 2.604 \times 10^9 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} = 1.302 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

$$A_{66} = 2 Q_{66} \times 2t = 2 \times 5.7 \times 10^9 \times 0.25 \times 10^{-3} = 2.85 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}$$

$$A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$$

$$\boxed{[A] = \begin{bmatrix} 35.99 & 1.302 & 0 \\ 1.302 & 35.99 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2.85 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}}$$

### Step 5 — Compute $[B]$ Matrix

By **symmetry** of the layup (0° plies are at $z = \pm 0.125$ to $\pm 0.25$, and 90° plies are at $z = -0.125$ to $+0.125$), the 0° and 90° contributions cancel exactly:

$$B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{4} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2) = 0$$

$$\boxed{[B] = [0]}$$

### Step 6 — Compute $[D]$ Matrix

For each ply, $D_{ij} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$.

The $z^3$ differences are:

- Ply 1 (0°, $z \in [-0.25, -0.125]$): $z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3 = (-0.125)^3 - (-0.25)^3 = -0.001953 + 0.015625 = 0.01367$ mm³
- Ply 2 (90°, $z \in [-0.125, 0]$): $z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3 = 0 - (-0.125)^3 = 0.001953$ mm³
- Ply 3 (90°, $z \in [0, 0.125]$): $z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3 = 0.001953$ mm³
- Ply 4 (0°, $z \in [0.125, 0.25]$): $z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3 = 0.015625 - 0.001953 = 0.01367$ mm³

For a **symmetric** layup, plies 1 & 4 give equal contributions, and plies 2 & 3 give equal contributions. So:

$$D_{11} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ Q_{11} \times 0.01367 \times 2 + Q_{22} \times 0.001953 \times 2 \right] \text{ (in GPa·mm³)}$$

Converting: $1$ GPa·mm³ = $10^9 \times 10^{-9}$ N·m = $1$ N·m.

$$D_{11} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ 133.12 \times 0.01367 \times 2 + 10.851 \times 0.001953 \times 2 \right] = \frac{1}{3} [3.640 + 0.0424] = 1.227 \text{ N·m}$$

$$D_{22} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ Q_{22} \times 0.01367 \times 2 + Q_{11} \times 0.001953 \times 2 \right] = \frac{1}{3} [0.297 + 0.520] = 0.272 \text{ N·m}$$

$$D_{12} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ 2 Q_{12} \times 0.01367 + 2 Q_{12} \times 0.001953 \right] = \frac{1}{3} \times 2.604 \times 0.01562 = 0.01356 \text{ N·m}$$

$$D_{66} = \frac{1}{3} \times 2 \times 5.7 \times 0.01562 = 0.0593 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{[D] = \begin{bmatrix} 1.227 & 0.0136 & 0 \\ 0.0136 & 0.272 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.0593 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}}$$

### Step 7 — Final ABD Matrix

$$\boxed{[ABD] = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 35.99 & 1.302 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 1.302 & 35.99 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2.85 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1.227 & 0.0136 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.0136 & 0.272 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0.0593 \end{bmatrix}}$$

(Units: A in MN/m, B in N, D in N·m.)

> [!TIP]
> **Observations:**
> - $B = 0$ confirms the layup is **symmetric**.
> - $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ because the 0° and 90° plies are individually balanced.
> - $D_{11} \gg D_{22}$ indicates much higher bending stiffness along the 0° fiber direction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Why $[B] = 0$ for a Symmetric Laminate (Quick Proof)

For a **symmetric laminate** about the geometric mid-plane, the ply stack above $z = 0$ mirrors the stack below. Therefore, the term $(z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$ for a ply at $+z$ is the **negative** of that for the mirror ply at $-z$. The matrix elements are:

$$B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$$

By the antisymmetry of $(z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$ about the mid-plane, paired contributions **cancel**, giving $[B] = [0]$. Q.E.D.
