## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Classical Lamination Theory — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["INPUT: Stacking sequence, ply thicknesses, material properties"] --> P2
    P2["Compute Q for each ply: Q11, Q22, Q12, Q66"] --> P3
    P3["For each off-axis ply, compute QBAR using Bond matrix"] --> P4
    P4["Compute z-coordinates of ply interfaces from mid-plane"] --> P5
    P5["Compute A by summing QBAR times ply thickness"] --> P6
    P6["Compute B by summing QBAR times half of z-squared difference"] --> P7
    P7["Compute D by summing QBAR times one third of z-cubed difference"] --> P8
    P8["Assemble ABD 6x6 matrix"] --> P9
    P9["Apply loads N and M to laminate"] --> P10
    P10["Invert ABD to get a, b, d compliance matrices"] --> P11
    P11["Compute mid-plane strains eps0 and curvatures kappa"] --> P12
    P12["For each ply: compute ply-level strains via eps minus z kappa"] --> P13
    P13["For each ply: compute ply-level stresses using QBAR"] --> P14
    P14["Apply failure criterion: Tsai-Wu, Tsai-Hill, Max Stress"] --> P15
    P15["OUTPUT: Strength ratio R, safety factor, critical ply"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Symmetric vs. Antisymmetric vs. Asymmetric Layups — Coupling Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["SYMMETRIC LAMINATE"] --> S2["B equals ZERO"]
    S2 --> S3["No extension-bending coupling"]
    S2 --> S4["No bending-twisting coupling"]

    T1["BALANCED LAMINATE"] --> T2["A16 and A26 equal ZERO"]
    T2 --> T3["No extension-shear coupling in mid-plane"]

    U1["SYMMETRIC AND BALANCED"] --> U2["B equals ZERO and A16 A26 equals ZERO"]
    U2 --> U3["Pure decoupled behavior"]

    V1["ANTISYMMETRIC LAMINATE"] --> V2["B not equal to ZERO"]
    V2 --> V3["Bending causes extension and twisting"]

    W1["UNSYMMETRIC LAMINATE"] --> W2["B not equal to ZERO and possible coupling"]
    W2 --> W3["Out of plane warping under in plane load"]
```

---

## 4.4 Coordinate System Schematic (Text-Based)

```
        LAMINATE (x-y-z)                       LAMINA (1-2-3)
        ┌───────────────────┐                  ┌───────────────────┐
       /                   /│                 /                   /│
      /                   / │                /                   / │
     /                   /  │               /                   /  │
    /                   /   │              /      1-axis        /   │
   /                   /    │             /      ↗             /    │
  /                   /     │            /       /             /     │
 ┌───────────────────┐      │           ┌───────────────────┐      │
 │                   │←──── z          │       │           │      │
 │                   │      │          │       ↓           │      │
 │                   │      │          │   2-axis          │      │
 │   y →             │     /          │                   │     /
 │         x         │    /           │   θ is rotation   │    /
 │         ↓         │   /            │   from x to 1     │   /
 │         ↑         │  /             │                   │  /
 │                   │ /              │                   │ /
 │                   │/               │                   │/
 └───────────────────┘                └───────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The rotation angle $\theta$** is measured counter-clockwise from the **positive x-axis** (laminate reference) to the **positive 1-axis** (fiber direction). For a 0° ply, $\theta = 0°$. For a 90° ply, $\theta = 90°$. For a -45° ply, $\theta = -45°$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Define a lamina and a laminate. Differentiate between micromechanics and macromechanics approaches in composite analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **lamina** is a single layer (or ply) of a fiber-reinforced composite consisting of unidirectional (or woven) fibers embedded in a matrix, with all fibers aligned in a single direction called the **fiber direction (1-axis)**. Typical thickness: **0.1–0.5 mm**.

A **laminate** is a stack of two or more laminae bonded together, with each ply having a specified fiber orientation, thickness, and stacking sequence (e.g., $[0°/45°/-45°/90°]_s$).

| Approach | Scale | Question Answered | Key Equation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Micromechanics** | Constituent level (fiber + matrix) | How do fiber and matrix combine to give lamina properties? | $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (rule of mixtures) |
| **Macromechanics** | Lamina/laminate level | How does a *given* lamina behave as a homogeneous anisotropic continuum? | $\{\sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}$, CLT ABD |

**(1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for the difference table, 1 mark for representative formulas.)**

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**State the plane stress assumption used in Classical Lamination Theory. Why is it invoked?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Plane stress assumption:** In each ply, the through-thickness normal stress and the transverse shear stresses are assumed negligible:

$$\sigma_{33} = \sigma_{13} = \sigma_{23} = 0$$

**Why invoked (1 mark each point):**

1. **Geometric reason:** Ply thickness ($\sim 0.1$–$0.5$ mm) is much smaller than in-plane dimensions ($\sim 10$–$1000$ mm), so $\sigma_{33} \to 0$ by equilibrium.
2. **Constitutive reason:** For thin laminates, the planes normal to the mid-plane cannot sustain $\tau_{13}, \tau_{23}$ without deforming (Kirchhoff–Love assumption).
3. **Computational reason:** Reduces 3D Hooke's law to 2D plane stress form, allowing the **reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$** ($3 \times 3$) and ABD ($6 \times 6$) framework.
4. **Practical validity:** Accurately predicts laminate behavior for **thin** laminates ($L/h > 20$). For thick laminates, **First-Order Shear Deformation Theory (FSDT)** is used.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing the **1–2 (lamina) and x–y (laminate)** coordinate systems.
> 2. Computing ply z-coordinates from the **bottom surface** instead of the **geometric mid-plane** — this gives wrong $[B]$ and $[D]$.
> 3. Forgetting to multiply by **thickness** (not just summing $[Q]$s).
> 4. Sign errors in the Tsai–Wu coefficients $F_1, F_2$.
> 5. Using $\theta$ measured the wrong way (clockwise vs. counter-clockwise) — KTU follows the counter-clockwise convention.
> 6. Failing to verify the **symmetry** of $[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$ — this is a 1-mark deduction if missed.

---

### Question B — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

A cross-ply laminate $[0°/90°/0°]$ is made of glass/epoxy with the following properties:

$$E_1 = 38.6 \text{ GPa}, \quad E_2 = 8.27 \text{ GPa}, \quad G_{12} = 4.14 \text{ GPa}, \quad \nu_{12} = 0.26$$

Each ply has thickness $t = 1$ mm.

**Part (a)** (7 marks): Compute the reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ and the transformed reduced stiffness matrices $[\bar{Q}]$ for 0° and 90° plies.

**Part (b)** (7 marks): Compute the $[A]$, $[B]$, and $[D]$ matrices for the laminate and comment on the resulting coupling behavior.

---

#### Solution to Choice 1 — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute $\nu_{21}$ and the denominator (1 mark)**

$$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.26 \times \frac{8.27}{38.6} = 0.0557$$

$$1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - 0.26 \times 0.0557 = 1 - 0.01449 = 0.9855$$

**Step 2: Compute $Q_{ij}$ (2 marks)**

$$Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{38.6}{0.9855} = 39.17 \text{ GPa}$$

$$Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{8.27}{0.9855} = 8.393 \text{ GPa}$$

$$Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12} E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}} = \frac{0.26 \times 8.27}{0.9855} = 2.182 \text{ GPa}$$

$$Q_{66} = G_{12} = 4.14 \text{ GPa}$$

$$[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} 39.17 & 2.182 & 0 \\ 2.182 & 8.393 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.14 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 3: Compute $[\bar{Q}]$ for $\theta = 0°$ (1 mark)**

$$[\bar{Q}^{(0°)}] = [Q] = \begin{bmatrix} 39.17 & 2.182 & 0 \\ 2.182 & 8.393 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.14 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4: Compute $[\bar{Q}]$ for $\theta = 90°$ (3 marks)**

For $\theta = 90°$, $m = 0$, $n = 1$. Substituting into the transformation formulas:

$$\bar{Q}_{11} = Q_{22} = 8.393 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{22} = Q_{11} = 39.17 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{12} = Q_{12} = 2.182 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{66} = Q_{66} = 4.14 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\bar{Q}_{16} = \bar{Q}_{26} = 0$$

$$[\bar{Q}^{(90°)}] = \begin{bmatrix} 8.393 & 2.182 & 0 \\ 2.182 & 39.17 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.14 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The 90° ply effectively swaps $E_1$ and $E_2$ in the transformed frame because the fibers (originally 1-axis) are now along the y-direction.

---

#### Solution to Choice 1 — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the z-coordinates of ply interfaces (2 marks)**

For a 3-ply laminate $[0°/90°/0°]$ with each $t = 1$ mm, total $h = 3$ mm, mid-plane at $z = 0$:

| Ply | Material | $z_{k-1}$ (mm) | $z_k$ (mm) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 (0°) | Top | $+0.5$ | $+1.5$ |
| 2 (90°) | Middle | $-0.5$ | $+0.5$ |
| 3 (0°) | Bottom | $-1.5$ | $-0.5$ |

(Note: Ply 1 is at the top — k increases from top to bottom OR bottom to top; the convention is consistent as long as z-coordinates are measured from the mid-plane.)

**Step 2: Compute $[A]$ (2 marks)**

$$A_{11} = \bar{Q}_{11}^{(0°)} \times 1 + \bar{Q}_{11}^{(90°)} \times 1 + \bar{Q}_{11}^{(0°)} \times 1 = (39.17 + 8.393 + 39.17) \times 10^9 \times 10^{-3} = 86.73 \text{ MN/m}$$

$$A_{22} = (8.393 + 39.17 + 8.393) \times 10^9 \times 10^{-3} = 55.96 \text{ MN/m}$$

$$A_{12} = (2.182 + 2.182 + 2.182) \times 10^6 = 6.546 \text{ MN/m}$$

$$A_{66} = (4.14 + 4.14 + 4.14) \times 10^6 = 12.42 \text{ MN/m}$$

$$[A] = \begin{bmatrix} 86.73 & 6.546 & 0 \\ 6.546 & 55.96 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 12.42 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MN/m}$$

**Step 3: Compute $[B]$ (2 marks)**

For a **symmetric** layup (plies 1 and 3 are both 0° and equidistant from mid-plane):

$$B_{11} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_k \bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$$

For ply 1: $z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2 = 1.5^2 - 0.5^2 = 2.25 - 0.25 = 2.0$ mm²
For ply 2: $z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2 = 0.5^2 - (-0.5)^2 = 0$
For ply 3: $z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2 = (-0.5)^2 - (-1.5)^2 = 0.25 - 2.25 = -2.0$ mm²

Plies 1 and 3 cancel; ply 2 contributes zero. So $[B] = [0]$. (1 mark)

**Step 4: Compute $[D]$ (1 mark)**

For ply 1: $z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3 = 1.5^3 - 0.5^3 = 3.375 - 0.125 = 3.25$ mm³
For ply 2: $z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3 = 0.5^3 - (-0.5)^3 = 0.125 + 0.125 = 0.25$ mm³
For ply 3: $z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3 = (-0.5)^3 - (-1.5)^3 = -0.125 + 3.375 = 3.25$ mm³

$$D_{11} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ 39.17 \times 3.25 \times 2 + 8.393 \times 0.25 \right] \times 10^9 \times 10^{-9} = \frac{1}{3} [254.61 + 2.098] = 85.57 \text{ N·m}$$

$$D_{22} = \frac{1}{3} \left[ 8.393 \times 3.25 \times 2 + 39.17 \times 0.25 \right] = \frac{1}{3} [54.55 + 9.79] = 21.45 \text{ N·m}$$

$$D_{12} = \frac{1}{3} \times 2.182 \times (3.25 \times 2 + 0.25) = \frac{1}{3} \times 2.182 \times 6.75 = 4.91 \text{ N·m}$$

$$D_{66} = \frac{1}{3} \times 4.14 \times 6.75 = 9.32 \text{ N·m}$$

$$[D] = \begin{bmatrix} 85.57 & 4.91 & 0 \\ 4.91 & 21.45 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 9.32 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5: Comment on coupling behavior (1 mark)**

- $[B] = [0]$: The laminate is **symmetric** about its mid-plane, so there is **no extension-bending coupling**. An in-plane force $N_x$ produces pure extension, no curvature.
- $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$: The laminate is **balanced** (for the 0/90/0 case, this holds because off-diagonal coupling terms vanish individually).
- $D_{11} \gg D_{22}$: Bending stiffness is much higher in the 0° fiber direction.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**

A unidirectional glass/epoxy lamina is loaded in the off-axis direction. The following properties are given:

$$E_1 = 45 \text{ GPa}, \quad E_2 = 12 \text{ GPa}, \quad G_{12} = 5 \text{ GPa}, \quad \nu_{12} = 0.3$$

The lamina is subjected to an off-axis stress $\sigma_x = 100$ MPa along a direction making an angle $\theta = 30°$ with the fiber direction. All other stresses are zero.

**Part (a)** (7 marks): Determine the stresses in the principal material coordinates (1–2) using the stress transformation equations.

**Part (b)** (7 marks): Determine the strains in the principal material coordinates and check whether the **Tsai–Hill failure criterion** is satisfied, given $X_T = 1500$ MPa, $Y_T = 40$ MPa, $S = 70$ MPa.

---

#### Solution to Choice 2 — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Write the stress transformation equations (2 marks)**

For a stress $\sigma_x$ applied at angle $\theta$ from the fiber direction:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta = \sigma_x \, m^2$$

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_x \sin^2\theta = \sigma_x \, n^2$$

$$\tau_{12} = -\sigma_x \sin\theta \cos\theta = -\sigma_x \, mn$$

(With $m = \cos\theta$, $n = \sin\theta$. The sign of $\tau_{12}$ depends on the sign convention for positive shear — KTU uses positive shear on the +1 and +2 faces as shown in standard texts.)

**Step 2: Substitute values (2 marks)**

For $\theta = 30°$: $m = \cos 30° = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 0.8660$, $n = \sin 30° = 0.5$.

$$\sigma_1 = 100 \times (0.8660)^2 = 100 \times 0.75 = 75 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 100 \times (0.5)^2 = 100 \times 0.25 = 25 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{12} = -100 \times 0.8660 \times 0.5 = -43.30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Tabulate results (1 mark)**

| Stress | Value | Sign |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $\sigma_1$ | 75 MPa | Tensile |
| $\sigma_2$ | 25 MPa | Tensile |
| $\tau_{12}$ | $-43.30$ MPa | Sign indicates shear sense |

**Step 4: Cross-check using transformation matrix (2 marks)**

$$[T_\sigma] = \begin{bmatrix} m^2 & n^2 & 2mn \\ n^2 & m^2 & -2mn \\ -mn & mn & m^2 - n^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.75 & 0.25 & 0.866 \\ 0.25 & 0.75 & -0.866 \\ -0.433 & 0.433 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\{\sigma\}_{1,2} = [T_\sigma]\{\sigma\}_{x,y} = [T_\sigma] \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 75 \\ 25 \\ -43.3 \end{bmatrix} \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### Solution to Choice 2 — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute strains using the compliance matrix (3 marks)**

Compute $\nu_{21}$:

$$\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.3 \times \frac{12}{45} = 0.08$$

$$1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - 0.3 \times 0.08 = 0.976$$

The compliance matrix:

$$[S] = \begin{bmatrix} 1/E_1 & -\nu_{21}/E_2 & 0 \\ -\nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/G_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.02222 & -0.00667 & 0 \\ -0.00667 & 0.08333 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \text{ (in 1/GPa)}$$

The strains:

$$\varepsilon_1 = S_{11}\sigma_1 + S_{12}\sigma_2 = 0.02222 \times 75 - 0.00667 \times 25 = 1.667 - 0.167 = 1.500 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = S_{12}\sigma_1 + S_{22}\sigma_2 = -0.00667 \times 75 + 0.08333 \times 25 = -0.500 + 2.083 = 1.583 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\gamma_{12} = S_{66}\tau_{12} = 0.2 \times (-43.3) = -8.66 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Tsai–Hill criterion (3 marks)**

The Tsai–Hill criterion (with $\sigma_1, \sigma_2 > 0$ and using $X = X_T$, $Y = Y_T$):

$$\left(\frac{\sigma_1}{X_T}\right)^2 - \frac{\sigma_1 \sigma_2}{X_T^2} + \left(\frac{\sigma_2}{Y_T}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\tau_{12}}{S}\right)^2 \le 1$$

Substitute values:

$$\left(\frac{75}{1500}\right)^2 - \frac{75 \times 25}{1500^2} + \left(\frac{25}{40}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{43.3}{70}\right)^2$$

$$= (0.05)^2 - \frac{1875}{2.25 \times 10^6} + (0.625)^2 + (0.6186)^2$$

$$= 0.0025 - 0.000833 + 0.3906 + 0.3827$$

$$= 0.7750$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result (1 mark)**

Since $0.7750 < 1$, the **Tsai–Hill criterion is satisfied**, and the lamina is predicted to be **safe** under this loading. The **Strength Ratio** is $R = 1/\sqrt{0.7750} = 1.136$, meaning the load can be increased by **~13.6%** before failure.

> [!NOTE]
> The dominant term is the transverse stress contribution $(25/40)^2 = 0.3906$, confirming that **transverse tension** is the most critical failure mode in off-axis loading of unidirectional laminas. This is why designers avoid off-axis loading on unidirectional composites.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Lamina and Laminates**

### Core Definitions
- **Lamina:** A *single* ply of fiber-reinforced material; fibers aligned in one direction (1-axis).
- **Laminate:** A *stack* of two or more laminae; response governed by Classical Lamination Theory.
- **Fiber volume fraction** $V_f$ is typically 0.45–0.65; **void content** < 1% for aerospace.

### Key Equations to Memorize
- Rule of mixtures: $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$
- Reciprocity: $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} E_2 / E_1$
- Reduced stiffness: $Q_{11} = E_1/(1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})$, $Q_{22} = E_2/(1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21})$, $Q_{12} = \nu_{12}E_2/(1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21})$, $Q_{66} = G_{12}$
- ABD formulas: $A = \sum \bar{Q} \Delta z$, $B = \frac{1}{2}\sum \bar{Q} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$, $D = \frac{1}{3}\sum \bar{Q} (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$
- Tsai–Hill: $(\sigma_1/X)^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2/X^2 + (\sigma_2/Y)^2 + (\tau_{12}/S)^2 \le 1$
- Tsai–Wu: $F_1\sigma_1 + F_2\sigma_2 + F_{11}\sigma_1^2 + F_{22}\sigma_2^2 + F_{66}\tau_{12}^2 + 2F_{12}\sigma_1\sigma_2 \le 1$

### Critical Conventions
- **z = 0** at the **geometric mid-plane** (NOT bottom surface).
- **$\theta$ measured counter-clockwise** from x to 1-axis.
- **$[\bar{Q}]$** is in the laminate (x-y) frame; **$[Q]$** is in the lamina (1-2) frame.
- **Plane stress** assumed in each ply: $\sigma_3 = \tau_{13} = \tau_{23} = 0$.

### Symmetry Properties
- **Symmetric layup** → $[B] = [0]$.
- **Balanced layup** → $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$.
- **Cross-ply** (only 0° and 90° plies) → off-diagonal $[Q_{16}]$, $[Q_{26}]$ vanish, so $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ and $D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$ automatically.
- **Quasi-isotropic** layup (e.g., $[0°/60°/-60°]_s$) → in-plane properties are isotropic; $A_{11} = A_{22}$, $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$.

### Numerical Checks for Exam
- Always verify that the **trace** of $[A]$ is positive.
- Check that the $[Q]$ matrix determinant is positive: $Q_{11}Q_{22} - Q_{12}^2 > 0$.
- $[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$ must all be **symmetric** (deduction if not).
- $\bar{Q}_{66}$ should be $\ge 0$ (positive definiteness).
- For $\theta = 0°$, $[\bar{Q}] = [Q]$ (sanity check).
- For $\theta = -\theta$, $\bar{Q}_{16}(\theta) = -\bar{Q}_{16}(-\theta)$ (antisymmetry).

### Practical Design Hints
- For **maximum $E_x$**: use a unidirectional $[0°]_n$ laminate.
- For **balanced in-plane stiffness**: use a quasi-isotropic layup.
- For **no bending–extension coupling**: use a symmetric layup.
- For **maximum buckling resistance**: place $0°$ plies on the outer surfaces.
- For **minimum weight**: use carbon fibers, not glass (higher $E/\rho$).

### Common Failure Modes to Remember
- **Fiber breakage** (tensile or compressive failure along 1-axis).
- **Matrix cracking** (transverse tensile failure along 2-axis).
- **Fiber–matrix debonding** (interfacial failure, often at $\tau_{12} > S$).
- **Delamination** (interlaminar shear failure, between plies).
- **Buckling** of compressive plies in sandwich or thick laminates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micromechanics of FRP Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET411 - ADVANCED SOLID MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Micromechanics of FRP Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — MICROMECHANICS OF FRP COMPOSITES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micromechanics of FRP (Fiber Reinforced Polymer) Composites** is the branch of solid mechanics that studies the **constituent-level mechanical behavior** of a composite material — analyzing how the individual **fiber**, **matrix**, and the **fiber–matrix interface** interact at the micro-scale to produce the **effective (homogenized) macroscopic engineering properties** of the composite lamina.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PECET411), micromechanics is the analytical bridge between:
- **Macro-mechanics** (which treats the laminate as a homogenized anisotropic continuum), and
- **Constituent material properties** (which are measurable from neat fiber and neat matrix coupons).

The primary goal of micromechanics is to **predict the four independent effective elastic constants** of a unidirectional (UD) FRP lamina — namely $E_1$, $E_2$, $G_{12}$, and $\nu_{12}$ — directly from:
- Fiber modulus $E_f$, fiber Poisson's ratio $\nu_f$
- Matrix modulus $E_m$, matrix Poisson's ratio $\nu_m$
- **Fiber volume fraction** $V_f$ (and consequently $V_m = 1 - V_f$)

These predictions rely on idealized internal stress/strain fields (iso-strain, iso-stress, or self-consistent schemes) that are solvable in closed form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module-4 expects students to derive the longitudinal modulus $E_1$ (Rule of Mixtures), transverse modulus $E_2$ (Inverse Rule of Mixtures / Halpin–Tsai), major Poisson's ratio $\nu_{12}$, and density $\rho_c$, and to apply them in numerical problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bundle of dry spaghetti (fibers)** held together by a thick layer of **honey (matrix)**:

- If you **pull the bundle along the spaghetti direction**, the spaghetti carries almost all the load. The honey simply glues the strands together and transfers a small amount of shear. The stiffness you "feel" is essentially the **weighted average of spaghetti stiffness and honey stiffness** — this is the **Rule of Mixtures** giving $E_1$.
- If you **pull the bundle sideways (perpendicular to the spaghetti)**, the load must travel **through the honey only**, because the spaghetti strands don't touch each other. The honey stiffness now dominates, and the bundle feels much weaker in this direction. This lateral weakness leads to the **Inverse Rule of Mixtures** for $E_2$.
- The **interface** between spaghetti and honey is the "glue line" — if it is weak, the bundle slips apart easily. If it is strong, the load transfers efficiently.

This anisotropy ($E_1 \gg E_2$) is the **defining feature** of unidirectional FRP composites and the reason composites are tailored to load paths.

### 1.3 Key Constituent Constants Used in Micromechanics

| Symbol | Property | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_f$ | Fiber Young's modulus | $70$ (E-glass) – $400$ (Carbon) | **GPa** |
| $E_m$ | Matrix Young's modulus | $3$ (Epoxy) – $5$ (Vinylester) | **GPa** |
| $\nu_f$ | Fiber Poisson's ratio | $0.20$ – $0.30$ | dimensionless |
| $\nu_m$ | Matrix Poisson's ratio | $0.30$ – $0.40$ | dimensionless |
| $V_f$ | Fiber volume fraction | $0.30$ – $0.65$ (typical) | dimensionless |
| $\rho_f$ | Fiber density | $1.4$ (Aramid) – $2.6$ (Carbon) | **g/cm³** |
| $\rho_m$ | Matrix density | $1.1$ – $1.3$ | **g/cm³** |
| $\xi$ | Reinforcing factor (Halpin–Tsai) | $1$ (circular) – $2$ (rectangular) | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Micromechanics Assumptions

Micromechanical closed-form solutions are built on two idealized loading states:

### 2.1.1 The Voigt / Iso-Strain Assumption (Rule of Mixtures — ROM)

> Both the fiber and the matrix experience the **same axial strain** $\varepsilon_1$ when a uniaxial load is applied along the fiber direction. The load is shared between fiber and matrix in proportion to their **stiffness × area**.

- **Physical basis:** Fibers are continuous, parallel, and perfectly bonded to the matrix. Under longitudinal loading, fiber and matrix must deform together (compatibility condition).
- **Implication:** Stress in each phase is proportional to its modulus: $\sigma_f / E_f = \sigma_m / E_m = \varepsilon_1$.

### 2.1.2 The Reuss / Iso-Stress Assumption (Inverse Rule of Mixtures — IROM)

> Both the fiber and the matrix experience the **same transverse stress** $\sigma_2$ when a uniaxial load is applied perpendicular to the fibers. The strain in each phase differs.

- **Physical basis:** In the transverse direction, fiber and matrix "see" the same load per unit area across a cross-section perpendicular to the load, but they deform by different amounts because of their stiffness contrast.
- **Implication:** Strain is inversely proportional to modulus: $\varepsilon_f / (1/E_f) = \varepsilon_m / (1/E_m) = \sigma_2 / 1$.

## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Reasoning for Each Effective Property

### Step 1 — Density of the Composite ($\rho_c$)
- Mass is additive; volume is the reference frame.
- The composite density is a **direct volume-weighted average** of fiber and matrix densities.

### Step 2 — Longitudinal Modulus ($E_1$) — Voigt Model
- Apply iso-strain condition: $\varepsilon_1^f = \varepsilon_1^m = \varepsilon_1$.
- Force equilibrium: total load = fiber load + matrix load.
- Divide by total area → volume-weighted average of stresses → divide by common strain.
- **Result:** $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (Rule of Mixtures).

### Step 3 — Major Poisson's Ratio ($\nu_{12}$)
- Iso-strain is still valid along the 1-direction.
- The transverse contraction $\varepsilon_2$ in the matrix differs from that in the fiber.
- The **effective $\nu_{12}$** is the volume-weighted average of the constituent Poisson's ratios.

### Step 4 — Transverse Modulus ($E_2$) — Reuss Model
- Apply iso-stress: $\sigma_2^f = \sigma_2^m = \sigma_2$.
- The total transverse strain is the volume-weighted sum of constituent strains.
- **Result:** $1/E_2 = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ (Inverse Rule of Mixtures).

### Step 5 — Improved Transverse Modulus — Halpin–Tsai Equation
- The simple IROM **over-predicts compliance** (under-predicts $E_2$) because it ignores stress concentration effects around the fiber.
- Halpin and Tsai (1969) introduced a **semi-empirical correction** that includes a geometry factor $\xi$ (aspect ratio / reinforcing factor), producing much better agreement with experiments.
- **Result:** $E_2 = E_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$, where $\eta = \dfrac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + \xi}$.

### Step 6 — In-Plane Shear Modulus ($G_{12}$)
- Obtained by Halpin–Tsai with $\xi = 1$ (since fibers are circular, the shear-lag length equals the diameter).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for KTU Module-4 micromechanics. Memorize the relations, units, and the **direction of applicability** (1 = fiber, 2 = transverse).

| # | Property | Governing Formula | Governing Assumption | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Composite density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Mass additivity | g/cm³ or kg/m³ |
| 2 | Longitudinal modulus (Rule of Mixtures) | $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Voigt (iso-strain) | GPa |
| 3 | Major Poisson's ratio | $\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Voigt (iso-strain) | dimensionless |
| 4 | Transverse modulus (Inverse ROM) | $\dfrac{1}{E_2} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Reuss (iso-stress) | GPa⁻¹ |
| 5 | Halpin–Tsai factor | $\eta = \dfrac{(E_f / E_m) - 1}{(E_f / E_m) + \xi}$ | Semi-empirical | dimensionless |
| 6 | Halpin–Tsai $E_2$ | $E_2 = E_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$ | Semi-empirical | GPa |
| 7 | Halpin–Tsai $G_{12}$ | $G_{12} = G_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta_g V_f}{1 - \eta_g V_f}$, $\xi = 1$ | Semi-empirical | GPa |
| 8 | Fiber volume fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c - \rho_m}{\rho_f - \rho_m}$ | From measured density | dimensionless |
| 9 | Weight-to-volume fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ | Lab weighing | dimensionless |
| 10 | Compliance form (transverse) | $E_2 = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}$ | Reuss | GPa |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace primary structures** (wing skins, fuselage): CFRP laminas are designed with $V_f \approx 0.60$–$0.65$ to maximize $E_1$ while keeping weight minimal.
- **Civil strengthening**: GFRP wraps for retrofitting concrete columns are designed using transverse and shear moduli predicted by Halpin–Tsai.
- **Wind-turbine blades**: Long glass-fiber reinforced epoxy blades rely on micromechanics to predict stiffness variation along the blade length (variable $V_f$).
- **Filament-wound pressure vessels**: Hoop and helical windings are designed with micromechanics-predicted lamina properties to meet burst-pressure safety factors.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. Multi-line derivations use the `\begin{aligned}` environment. **No step is skipped.**

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Density of the Composite, $\rho_c$

**Start:** Let the composite have total volume $V_c$, with fiber sub-volume $V_f^c$ and matrix sub-volume $V_m^c$.

$$
V_c = V_f^c + V_m^c
$$

**Mass of each phase** (additive, no reaction at the interface that changes mass):

$$
M_c = M_f + M_m
$$

**Substitute $M = \rho V$:**

$$
\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f^c + \rho_m V_m^c
$$

**Divide by $V_c$ (note: $V_f^c / V_c \equiv V_f$, $V_m^c / V_c \equiv V_m$):**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f \frac{V_f^c}{V_c} + \rho_m \frac{V_m^c}{V_c}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Because both volume fractions are dimensionless and sum to unity, $\rho_c$ is a simple weighted mean.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal Modulus $E_1$ (Rule of Mixtures)

**Step 1 — Representative Volume Element (RVE).** Consider a UD composite loaded along the fiber axis (1-direction). A cross-section perpendicular to the fibers has area $A_c$, comprising fiber area $A_f$ and matrix area $A_m$.

$$
A_c = A_f + A_m
$$

**Step 2 — Iso-strain assumption (Voigt).** Fiber and matrix deform together along the 1-axis:

$$
\varepsilon_1^f = \varepsilon_1^m = \varepsilon_1
$$

**Step 3 — Force equilibrium.** Total axial force equals the sum of phase forces:

$$
F_c = F_f + F_m
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Hooke's Law** in each phase ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$):

$$
\sigma_c A_c = E_f \varepsilon_1 A_f + E_m \varepsilon_1 A_m
$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $A_c$** and factor out $\varepsilon_1$:

$$
\sigma_c = \varepsilon_1 \left[ E_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \frac{A_m}{A_c} \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the area ratios as volume fractions** (since the fibers are continuous and prismatic, area fraction = volume fraction):

$$
\sigma_c = \varepsilon_1 \left[ E_f V_f + E_m V_m \right]
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Hooke's Law at the composite scale** $\sigma_c = E_1 \varepsilon_1$:

$$
E_1 \varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_1 \left[ E_f V_f + E_m V_m \right]
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Major Poisson's Ratio $\nu_{12}$

**Step 1 — Iso-strain in 1-direction** (same as $E_1$ derivation): $\varepsilon_1^f = \varepsilon_1^m = \varepsilon_1$.

**Step 2 — Transverse strain in each phase** by definition of Poisson's ratio:

$$
\varepsilon_2^f = -\nu_f \varepsilon_1, \quad \varepsilon_2^m = -\nu_m \varepsilon_1
$$

**Step 3 — Effective transverse strain is the volume-weighted average** (because the lateral contraction must be compatible with the overall lateral deformation):

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_2^f V_f + \varepsilon_2^m V_m
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and factor:**

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -\nu_f \varepsilon_1 V_f - \nu_m \varepsilon_1 V_m
$$

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -\varepsilon_1 \left[ \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Apply definition of effective Poisson's ratio** $\nu_{12} = -\varepsilon_2 / \varepsilon_1$:

$$
\boxed{\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Because $\varepsilon_1$ cancels, the effective $\nu_{12}$ is a direct rule of mixtures, just like $E_1$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Transverse Modulus $E_2$ (Inverse Rule of Mixtures)

**Step 1 — Apply a uniaxial stress $\sigma_2$ in the 2-direction** (perpendicular to fibers).

**Step 2 — Iso-stress assumption (Reuss):** Both phases feel the **same transverse stress**.

$$
\sigma_2^f = \sigma_2^m = \sigma_2
$$

**Step 3 — Constituent strains** from Hooke's law:

$$
\varepsilon_2^f = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_f}, \quad \varepsilon_2^m = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Total strain in the composite** is the volume-weighted sum of phase strains (overall transverse elongation of the RVE):

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_2^f V_f + \varepsilon_2^m V_m
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute:**

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_m} V_m
$$

**Step 6 — Factor $\sigma_2$ and apply $\sigma_2 = E_2 \varepsilon_2$:**

$$
\varepsilon_2 = \sigma_2 \left[ \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right]
$$

$$
\frac{\sigma_2}{E_2} = \sigma_2 \left[ \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right]
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}
$$

**Equivalent compliance form:**

$$
E_2 = \frac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f V_m}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Halpin–Tsai Equation for $E_2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Reuss/IROM model **under-predicts** $E_2$ by 20–40% in real composites because it ignores the **2-D stress concentration** around the fiber. Halpin and Tsai (1969) generalized the solution by interpolating between iso-strain and iso-stress with a geometry factor $\xi$.

**Step 1 — Define the modulus ratio:**

$$
R = \frac{E_f}{E_m}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Halpin–Tsai parameter $\eta$** (dimensionless):

$$
\eta = \frac{R - 1}{R + \xi}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the reinforcing factor $\xi$** for the property being computed:

$$
\xi_E = 2 \left( \frac{l}{d} \right) \quad \text{(for } E_2\text{)}, \quad \xi_G = 1 \quad \text{(for } G_{12}\text{)}
$$

where $l/d$ is the **fiber aspect ratio** (for continuous circular fibers, $l/d \to \infty$ and $\xi_E \to 2$).

**Step 4 — Generalized Halpin–Tsai form:**

$$
\frac{P}{P_m} = \frac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E_2$ (with $P = E_2$, $P_m = E_m$):**

$$
\boxed{E_2 = E_m \cdot \frac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}, \quad \eta = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + \xi}}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The denominator $1 - \eta V_f$ ensures the formula reduces to $E_2 = E_m$ when $V_f = 0$ (no fibers) and to $E_2 \to E_f$ as $V_f \to 1$ (full fiber), which is the required physical limit.

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical)

> **Given:** $E_f = 380$ GPa (Carbon), $E_m = 4.5$ GPa (Epoxy), $V_f = 0.55$, $\nu_f = 0.22$, $\nu_m = 0.35$, $\xi = 2$.
>
> **Find:** $E_1$, $\nu_{12}$, $E_2$ (Reuss), $E_2$ (Halpin–Tsai).

**Step 1 — $E_1$ (Rule of Mixtures):**

$$
E_1 = (380)(0.55) + (4.5)(0.45) = 209.0 + 2.025 = 211.025 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — $\nu_{12}$:**

$$
\nu_{12} = (0.22)(0.55) + (0.35)(0.45) = 0.121 + 0.1575 = 0.2785
$$

**Step 3 — $E_2$ (Inverse Rule of Mixtures):**

$$
\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{0.55}{380} + \frac{0.45}{4.5} = 0.001447 + 0.1000 = 0.10145 \text{ GPa}^{-1}
$$

$$
E_2 = 9.857 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 4 — $E_2$ (Halpin–Tsai, $\xi = 2$):**

$$
\eta = \frac{(380/4.5) - 1}{(380/4.5) + 2} = \frac{83.444}{85.444} = 0.97662
$$

$$
E_2 = 4.5 \cdot \frac{1 + (2)(0.97662)(0.55)}{1 - (0.97662)(0.55)} = 4.5 \cdot \frac{2.0743}{0.4629} = 4.5 \cdot 4.4815
$$

$$
\boxed{E_2^{\text{HT}} = 20.17 \text{ GPa}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Halpin–Tsai ($E_2 = 20.17$ GPa) gives a value roughly **twice** the simple Reuss prediction ($E_2 = 9.86$ GPa). This is consistent with experimental data on glass/epoxy and carbon/epoxy systems and is the **preferred engineering model** in KTU problems.

**Step 5 — Anisotropy ratio:**

$$
\frac{E_1}{E_2^{\text{HT}}} = \frac{211.025}{20.17} \approx 10.46
$$

This high ratio (≈ 10) is why UD composites are treated as **transversely isotropic** and not isotropic in classical lamination theory.
